For the Gaussian with both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) unknown, the Fisher information matrix is \[\mathcal{I}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sigma^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2/\sigma^4 \end{pmatrix},\] so \(\det \mathcal{I} = 2/\sigma^6\), and the Jeffreys prior is \[p_J(\mu, \sigma^2) \propto \sqrt{\frac{2}{\sigma^6}} = \frac{1}{\sigma^3}.\]
Equivalently, \(p_J(\mu, \sigma) \propto 1/\sigma^2\) in terms of the standard deviation.
Subtle point: The joint Jeffreys prior \(p_J(\mu, \sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^3\) does not equal the product of the marginal Jeffreys priors for \(\mu\) (uniform) and \(\sigma^2\) (proportional to \(1/\sigma^2\)), which would give \(p(\mu) \cdot p(\sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^2\). Many practitioners use this product-of-marginals prior and call it “Jeffreys,” but it is technically the reference prior or a compromise prior. The true joint Jeffreys prior is \(1/\sigma^3\). This distinction matters for hypothesis tests and Bayes factors.

Reference priors. A natural generalisation kicks in when dimension grows.

Remark. When the parameter dimension is high, computing or integrating over the Jeffreys prior becomes cumbersome. Reference priors (Bernardo, 1979; Berger, Bernardo & Sun, 2009) generalize the idea: they maximize expected Kullback–Leibler divergence between posterior and prior, thus minimizing the information contained in the prior itself. For one-parameter problems, reference priors coincide with Jeffreys; for multiparameter problems, they offer an alternative construction that often performs better in frequentist coverage. Reference priors are not invariant to reparametrization but are designed to be more “objective” in the sense of information loss.



6.12.5 The Bernstein–von Mises Theorem

Under standard regularity conditions, for large sample size \(n\), the posterior distribution \(p(\theta \mid X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) is approximately Gaussian, centered at the MLE \(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}}\) with covariance given by the inverse Fisher information: \[p(\theta \mid X_1, \ldots, X_n) \approx \mathcal{N}\big(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{ML}},\,\mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1}\big),\] provided the prior is continuous and positive at \(\theta_*\) (the true parameter). This holds independently of the choice of prior.

Interpretation and significance: The Bernstein–von Mises theorem is one of the deepest results in Bayesian asymptotics. It says that, given enough data, the posterior “forgets” the prior (as long as it is not zero at the truth) and concentrates around the MLE with the canonical frequentist covariance \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1}\). This explains why, asymptotically, Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist confidence intervals coincide: both converge to the same Gaussian limit. In other words, there is no fundamental conflict between Bayesian and frequentist inference in the large-sample regime—they merge. For a detailed proof, see van der Vaart (1998), Chapter 10.
Unifying theme. Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, Jeffreys, and Bernstein–von Mises are tightly interwoven:

Cramér–Rao sets the performance standard for any unbiased estimator.
MLE asymptotics show that the MLE achieves this standard with variance \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta)^{-1}\).
Jeffreys prior is constructed from \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\), ensuring objective prior choice via reparametrization invariance.
Bernstein–von Mises guarantees that the posterior, regardless of the prior, concentrates at the MLE with covariance \(\mathcal{I}_n(\theta)^{-1}\).

Together, these results form the backbone of both frequentist and Bayesian asymptotic theory.
crumbs: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction

title: 6  Bayesian parameter estimation, intervals and prediction
text:
6.13 Exercises

(Written test, 15 June 2026 — Q2.) Let \(\lambda \mid D \sim \text{Gamma}(a, b)\) with rate parameter \(b\) and \(a > 1\). The MAP estimator of \(\lambda\) is \[\hat\lambda_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{a-1}{b}.\] Now define a new parameter \(\eta = \log \lambda\). Using the posterior density induced for \(\eta\), find the MAP estimator \(\hat\eta_{\text{MAP}}\). Compare \(e^{\hat\eta_{\text{MAP}}}\) with \(\hat\lambda_{\text{MAP}}\).


Solution. With \(\lambda = e^\eta\) and Jacobian \(d\lambda/d\eta = e^\eta\), the induced posterior is \[p(\eta \mid D) \propto \lambda^{a-1} e^{-b\lambda}\cdot \lambda = e^{a\eta} e^{-b e^\eta}.\] Setting \(\partial_\eta \log p = a - b e^\eta = 0\) gives \[\hat\eta_{\text{MAP}} = \log(a/b), \qquad e^{\hat\eta_{\text{MAP}}} = \frac{a}{b} \neq \frac{a-1}{b} = \hat\lambda_{\text{MAP}}.\] The MAP is not invariant under reparametrisation: the Jacobian shifts the mode from \((a-1)/b\) to \(a/b\) (the posterior mean of \(\lambda\)).


(Written test, 15 June 2026 — Q3.) A posterior distribution for a scalar parameter is \[p(\theta \mid D) = 0.9\,\mathcal{N}(0, 0.2^2) + 0.1\,\mathcal{N}(10, 0.2^2).\] Which point estimate is favored by squared-error loss? Which point estimate is given by the MAP? Between these two estimates, which is more representative of a typical posterior draw?


Solution. The squared-error (MMSE) estimator is the posterior mean, \[\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}} = 0.9\cdot 0 + 0.1\cdot 10 = 1.\] The MAP is the global mode; since both components have equal width \(\sigma = 0.2\) and the first carries weight \(0.9 \gg 0.1\), \[\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = 0.\] The MAP is far more representative: \(90\%\) of posterior mass sits near \(0\), while \(\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}} = 1\) falls in the low-density gap between the two modes and is unlike any typical draw. This is the classic failure mode of the posterior mean on multimodal posteriors.


(Written test, 15 June 2026 — Q4.) During an exposure of length \(T\), no events are observed. Assume the number of observed events follows a Poisson distribution with mean \(\lambda T\), where \(\lambda\) is the event rate. Use the improper uniform prior \(p(\lambda) \propto 1\) with \(\lambda \geq 0\). Derive the \(99\%\) upper credible bound on \(\lambda\), then compute it for \(T = 50\).


Solution. With zero counts and a flat prior, \(p(\lambda \mid D) \propto e^{-\lambda T}\), so \[\lambda \mid D \sim \text{Exp}(T), \qquad F(\lambda) = 1 - e^{-T\lambda}.\] The \(99\%\) upper credible bound \(\lambda^*\) solves \(F(\lambda^*) = 0.99\), giving \[\lambda^* = -\frac{\log(0.01)}{T} = \frac{\log 100}{T}.\] For \(T = 50\), \(\lambda^* = \log(100)/50 \approx 4.605/50 \approx 0.092\).


A binary outcome is observed \(n\) times with \(s\) successes. With prior \(\theta \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\), show that the posterior is \(\text{Beta}(s+1, n-s+1)\). Give the posterior mean and the posterior mode.


Solution. Uniform on \((0,1)\) is \(\text{Beta}(1,1)\), so the likelihood \(\theta^s(1-\theta)^{n-s}\) times the prior \(1\) gives \[p(\theta \mid D) \propto \theta^s(1-\theta)^{n-s} \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta \mid D \sim \text{Beta}(s+1,\,n-s+1).\] Hence \[\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}} = \frac{s+1}{n+2}, \qquad \hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{s}{n}\quad(\text{for }1\le s\le n-1).\] The MAP recovers the MLE while the mean is the Laplace-rule-of-succession smoother.


Same Bernoulli likelihood as above. Use prior \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(2, 2)\) and data \(n = 12\), \(s = 8\). Write the posterior and give its mean and mode.


Solution. Conjugate updating gives \[\theta \mid D \sim \text{Beta}(2 + 8,\,2 + 4) = \text{Beta}(10, 6).\] Therefore \[\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}} = \frac{10}{16} = \frac{5}{8}, \qquad \hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{10 - 1}{10 + 6 - 2} = \frac{9}{14}.\]


Conjugate Gamma–Poisson. Observe \(n\) Poisson counts with sum \(s\), prior \(\lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) (rate \(\beta\)). Derive the posterior. Give the posterior mean (MMSE) and the MAP. Verify Bernstein–von Mises numerically by letting \(n \to \infty\) with \(s/n \to \bar x\) and checking that the MAP tends to the MLE.


Solution. The likelihood is \(\prod_i \lambda^{x_i} e^{-\lambda}/x_i! \propto \lambda^s e^{-n\lambda}\), multiplied by the prior \(\lambda^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta\lambda}\), gives \[\lambda \mid D \sim \Gamma(\alpha + s,\,\beta + n).\] Hence \[\hat\lambda_{\text{LMS}} = \frac{\alpha + s}{\beta + n}, \qquad \hat\lambda_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\alpha + s - 1}{\beta + n}.\] As \(n \to \infty\) with \(s/n \to \bar x\), both estimators tend to \(\bar x = \hat\lambda_{\text{ML}}\), and the posterior variance \((\alpha+s)/(\beta+n)^2 \sim \bar x/n = \mathcal{I}_n(\lambda)^{-1}\), confirming Bernstein–von Mises.


Exponential model. Observe \(n\) i.i.d. samples \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\) with sum \(s\) from \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\), and use a \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) prior on \(\lambda\). Derive the posterior and the MMSE estimator.


Solution. The likelihood is \(\lambda^n e^{-\lambda s}\); multiplying by the prior \(\lambda^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta\lambda}\), \[\lambda \mid D \sim \Gamma(\alpha + n,\,\beta + s).\] The MMSE estimator is the posterior mean \[\hat\lambda_{\text{LMS}} = \frac{\alpha + n}{\beta + s}.\] As \(n,s\to\infty\) this tends to \(n/s = 1/\bar x = \hat\lambda_{\text{ML}}\).


Reparameterization. For a Bernoulli model with \(\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\) posterior on \(\theta\), define the odds \(\psi = \theta / (1 - \theta)\). Derive the posterior density \(p(\psi \mid D)\) and locate its mode. Compare with the mode of \(p(\theta \mid D)\) pushed through \(\theta \mapsto \theta / (1 - \theta)\).


Solution. With \(\theta = \psi/(1+\psi)\) and \(|d\theta/d\psi| = (1+\psi)^{-2}\), \[p(\psi \mid D) \propto \frac{\psi^{\alpha-1}}{(1+\psi)^{\alpha+\beta}}, \qquad \psi > 0.\] Setting the derivative of the log density to zero gives \[\hat\psi_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\beta + 1}.\] Meanwhile \(\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = (\alpha-1)/(\alpha+\beta-2)\) pushed through the map gives \((\alpha-1)/(\beta-1)\), which differs from \((\alpha-1)/(\beta+1)\). The discrepancy is exactly the Jacobian \((1+\psi)^{-2}\) shifting the mode — the MAP is not reparametrisation invariant.


A posterior density is \(p(\theta \mid D) = \tfrac{3}{2}(1 - \theta^2)\) on \([-1, 1]\). Compute the MMSE estimator, the MAP, and the posterior median.


Solution. The density is symmetric about \(0\), so by symmetry \[\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}} = E[\theta \mid D] = 0, \qquad \text{median}(\theta \mid D) = 0.\] The MAP maximises \(1 - \theta^2\), giving \[\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = 0.\] All three coincide at the single peak of this symmetric unimodal posterior.


Skewed posterior. Let \(\lambda \mid D \sim \text{Gamma}(2, 1)\). Compute the posterior mean, the posterior mode, and the median (use the exponential CDF after marginalising).


Solution. With density \(\lambda e^{-\lambda}\), \[\hat\lambda_{\text{LMS}} = \frac{a}{b} = 2, \qquad \hat\lambda_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{a-1}{b} = 1.\] The CDF is \(F(\lambda) = 1 - (1+\lambda)e^{-\lambda}\), so the median solves \[(1+\lambda)e^{-\lambda} = \tfrac{1}{2},\] giving \(\text{median}(\lambda \mid D) \approx 1.678\). Mean \(>\) median \(>\) mode, the hallmark of a right-skewed Gamma.


Loss functions. Show that the Bayes estimator under the absolute loss \(L(a, \theta) = |a - \theta|\) is the posterior median. Then show that under the asymmetric loss \[L(a, \theta) = c_1\, (a - \theta)_+ + c_2\, (\theta - a)_+,\] the optimal estimator is the \(c_2/(c_1 + c_2)\) quantile of the posterior.


Solution. Differentiating \(R(a) = E[|a-\theta|\mid D] = \int_{-\infty}^a (a-\theta)p\,d\theta + \int_a^\infty (\theta-a)p\,d\theta\) in \(a\) gives \[R'(a) = P(\theta \le a \mid D) - P(\theta > a \mid D) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; P(\theta \le a \mid D) = \tfrac{1}{2},\] so \(\hat a = \text{median}(\theta \mid D)\). For the asymmetric loss the same calculation gives \[R'(a) = c_1 P(\theta \le a \mid D) - c_2 P(\theta > a \mid D) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; P(\theta \le a \mid D) = \frac{c_2}{c_1 + c_2},\] so the optimal \(\hat a\) is the \(c_2/(c_1+c_2)\)-quantile of the posterior.


Improper flat prior on a location parameter. Let \(x \mid \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, 1)\), prior \(p(\theta) \propto 1\), observe a single value \(x\). Give the posterior and the equal-tailed \(95\%\) credible interval (use \(z_{0.975} \approx 2\)).


Solution. The flat prior leaves the likelihood untouched, so \[\theta \mid x \sim \mathcal{N}(x, 1).\] The equal-tailed \(95\%\) credible interval is \[[\theta^-, \theta^+] = x \pm z_{0.975} \approx [x - 2,\,x + 2].\]


Sequential updating. Start from prior \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(1, 1)\). After observing a single head update the posterior, then update again after observing a single tail. Compare with the one-shot update using the data \((1\text{H}, 1\text{T})\).


Solution. Sequentially, \[\text{Beta}(1,1) \xrightarrow{1\text{H}} \text{Beta}(2,1) \xrightarrow{1\text{T}} \text{Beta}(2,2).\] The one-shot update on the combined data \((s=1, n=2)\) gives \[\text{Beta}(1+1,\,1+1) = \text{Beta}(2,2).\] Identical posteriors — conjugate updating is associative, as it must be by the product rule.


Posterior predictive. Suppose \(\theta \mid D \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\) and the next observation is \(y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\). Compute \(P(y = 1 \mid D)\) in closed form.


Solution. By marginalising over \(\theta\), \[P(y = 1 \mid D) = E[\theta \mid D] = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}.\] For Bernoulli predictives the plug-in posterior-mean estimate and the proper posterior-predictive agree, because the model is linear in \(\theta\).


One-sided credible bound. Let \(\lambda \mid D \sim \text{Gamma}(a, b)\). Express the \(95\%\) upper credible bound in terms of the Gamma quantile function. For \(a = 1\) (so the posterior is \(\text{Exp}(b)\)), give it in closed form.


Solution. The \(95\%\) upper credible bound solves \(P(\lambda \le \lambda^* \mid D) = 0.95\), so \[\lambda^* = F^{-1}_{\Gamma(a,b)}(0.95).\] When \(a = 1\) the posterior is \(\text{Exp}(b)\) with CDF \(1 - e^{-b\lambda}\), hence \[\lambda^* = -\frac{\log(0.05)}{b} = \frac{\log 20}{b} \approx \frac{2.996}{b}.\]


Jeffreys prior for the binomial parameter \(\theta \in (0, 1)\). Starting from the Fisher information \(\mathcal{I}(\theta) = 1/[\theta(1-\theta)]\), derive \(p_J(\theta) \propto \theta^{-1/2}(1-\theta)^{-1/2}\) and identify it as a Beta distribution.


Solution. By definition, \[p_J(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\mathcal{I}(\theta)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\theta(1-\theta)}} = \theta^{-1/2}(1-\theta)^{-1/2}.\] Comparing with the Beta kernel \(\theta^{\alpha-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1}\) identifies \(\alpha = \beta = 1/2\), so \[p_J(\theta) = \text{Beta}(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}).\] This is the arcsine distribution, with U-shaped density placing more mass near the boundaries.


(Define and explain.) Define the posterior mean, MAP, posterior median, and posterior mode. State which of these point estimators are invariant under smooth reparametrisation of \(\theta\) and which are not, with a one-line justification.


Solution. \(\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}} = E[\theta \mid D]\) (squared-error optimum), \(\hat\theta_{\text{med}}\) is the \(0.5\)-quantile (absolute-loss optimum), \(\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \hat\theta_{\text{mode}} = \arg\max_\theta p(\theta \mid D)\) (0–1 loss optimum). Under \(\psi = g(\theta)\): \[\hat\psi_{\text{med}} = g(\hat\theta_{\text{med}}), \quad \hat\psi_{\text{LMS}} \neq g(\hat\theta_{\text{LMS}}), \quad \hat\psi_{\text{MAP}} \neq g(\hat\theta_{\text{MAP}}).\] Only the median is fully invariant (quantiles transform with monotone maps); the mean is integral-based so \(E[g(\theta)] \neq g(E\theta)\) generally; the MAP picks up the Jacobian \(|d\theta/d\psi|\) which shifts the location of the peak.


(Compare and contrast.) In one paragraph each, describe the operational meaning of a \(95\%\) Bayesian credible interval and of a \(95\%\) frequentist confidence interval for a Gaussian mean. Highlight where the randomness lives in each.


Solution. Bayesian. Conditional on the observed sample mean \(\bar x\), prior, and model, \(P(\mu \in [\bar x - 1.96\sigma/\sqrt n,\,\bar x + 1.96\sigma/\sqrt n] \mid D) = 0.95\). Randomness lives in the parameter \(\mu\), treated as a random variable with the posterior distribution; the interval is fixed once the data are seen.
Frequentist. The random interval \(C(X) = [\bar X - 1.96\sigma/\sqrt n,\,\bar X + 1.96\sigma/\sqrt n]\) satisfies \(P_\mu(\mu \in C(X)) = 0.95\) for every fixed \(\mu\). Randomness lives in the data \(X\) across hypothetical replications; the parameter is fixed but unknown. After observing the data the realised interval either contains \(\mu\) or not, with no probability attached.


(Define and explain.) Define the Jeffreys prior for a one-dimensional parameter \(\theta\). State and prove the property that distinguishes it from a flat prior. Give one concrete one-dimensional example.


Solution. The Jeffreys prior is \(p_J(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\mathcal{I}(\theta)}\). It is reparametrisation invariant: under \(\psi = \phi(\theta)\) with Jacobian \(J = d\psi/d\theta\), \(\mathcal{I}_\psi(\psi) = \mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)/J^2\), so \[p_J(\psi) = \sqrt{\mathcal{I}_\psi(\psi)} = \sqrt{\mathcal{I}_\theta(\theta)}\,|J|^{-1} = p_J(\theta)\,\left|d\theta/d\psi\right|,\] which is exactly the change-of-variables formula for a density. A flat prior \(p(\theta) \propto 1\) does not transform this way and so depends on the choice of parametrisation. Example: for Poisson(\(\lambda\)), \(\mathcal{I}(\lambda) = 1/\lambda\) gives \(p_J(\lambda) \propto \lambda^{-1/2}\).


(Show that.) Show that the posterior predictive variance is strictly larger than the plug-in predictive variance unless the posterior is a point mass. Use the law of total variance.


Solution. By the law of total variance, \[\text{Var}(x_{\text{new}} \mid D) = \underbrace{E_\theta[\text{Var}(x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \mid D]}_{\text{aleatoric}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_\theta(E[x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta] \mid D)}_{\text{epistemic}}.\] The plug-in variance evaluates only the first term at \(\hat\theta\), dropping the epistemic piece. Since \(\text{Var}_\theta(\cdot) \ge 0\) with equality iff \(E[x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta]\) is constant on the support of the posterior — equivalently iff the posterior is a point mass — the posterior predictive variance strictly exceeds the plug-in variance whenever there is residual parameter uncertainty.

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.1 Introduction
The Gaussian (or normal) distribution is the most frequently encountered model in statistical inference. Its ubiquity stems from the Central Limit Theorem, which guarantees that sample averages tend toward normality under weak conditions, and from its mathematical tractability—the Gaussian family is closed under many operations and admits conjugate priors. This chapter develops both Bayesian and frequentist approaches to inference of the mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\) of a Gaussian model, emphasizing the conjugate prior structure that yields closed-form posterior distributions.
We begin with the simplest case: inference of the mean with known variance. We then extend to variance estimation with known mean, and finally treat joint inference of both parameters using the Normal-Inverse-Gamma conjugate family. Throughout, we highlight credible intervals, hypothesis testing via the Bayes factor, and the Bayesian \(t\)-test, which naturally marginalizes over the unknown variance.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
section: 7.2 Gaussian Sampling Model and Sufficient Statistics
text:
7.2 Gaussian Sampling Model and Sufficient Statistics

Let \(D = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}\) be \(n\) independent observations from a Gaussian distribution with pdf \[p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

The likelihood function for the data is the product \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^n p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2 \right).\]
Using the key identity \[\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2 + n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2 = S + n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2,\] where \(\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\) and \(S = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2\), the likelihood becomes \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{S + n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

The statistics \(\bar{x}\) and \(S\) are sufficient for \((\mu, \sigma^2)\): they capture all information in the data relevant to parameter inference. Any inference on \((\mu, \sigma^2)\) depends on the data only through these two summaries.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
section: 7.3 Inference of the Mean with Known Variance
text:
7.3 Inference of the Mean with Known Variance
Assume \(\sigma^2\) is a known constant. The likelihood as a function of \(\mu\) is \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right),\] a Gaussian in \(\mu\) centered at the sample mean \(\bar{x}\). Two priors are natural here.

Uniform (Jeffreys) prior. Assign \(p(\mu) \propto 1\). This is also the Jeffreys prior for \(\mu\), since the Fisher information \(\mathcal{I}(\mu) = n/\sigma^2\) does not depend on \(\mu\). By Bayes’ theorem, \[p(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2) \propto p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \cdot p(\mu) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]


Under the flat prior, the posterior distribution of \(\mu\) is \[\mu \mid D, \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \bar{x}, \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \right).\]

The posterior mean equals the maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) \(\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x}\), and the posterior variance shrinks as \(1/n\), reflecting increased confidence with larger sample size.

Conjugate Normal–Normal update. To incorporate prior knowledge, use a Gaussian prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)\). Multiplying likelihood by prior and completing the square gives \[p(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right) \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{\kappa_n (\mu - \mu_n)^2}{2} \right).\]


Normal-Normal Conjugate Update: \[\begin{align}
\kappa_n &= \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2}, \\
\mu_n &= \frac{\mu_0/\sigma_0^2 + n\bar{x}/\sigma^2}{\kappa_n} = \frac{\mu_0/\sigma_0^2 + n\bar{x}/\sigma^2}{1/\sigma_0^2 + n/\sigma^2}.
\end{align}\]

The posterior is \(\mu \mid D, \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, \kappa_n^{-1})\). Interpretation: precisions add; the posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and the sample mean. As \(n \to \infty\) the posterior is dominated by the data (\(\mu_n \to \bar{x}\)); for a weak prior (\(\sigma_0^2 \to \infty\)), \(\kappa_n \to n/\sigma^2\) and \(\mu_n \to \bar{x}\), recovering the uniform-prior result.

Measurements have known variance \(\sigma^2 = 4\). The sample mean from \(n = 9\) measurements is \(\bar x = 3\). A prior for the mean is \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\). Compute the posterior mean and variance, and compare with the flat-prior posterior mean.
Solution. With \(\sigma_0^2 = 1\) and \(n/\sigma^2 = 9/4\), the conjugate posterior precision and variance are \[\kappa_n = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2} = 1 + \frac{9}{4} = \frac{13}{4}, \qquad V_n = \kappa_n^{-1} = \frac{4}{13}.\] The posterior mean is the precision-weighted average \[\mu_n = V_n\!\left(\frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{n\bar x}{\sigma^2}\right) = \frac{4}{13}\!\left(0 + \frac{9 \cdot 3}{4}\right) = \frac{27}{13} \approx 2.08.\] With the flat (improper) prior the posterior would be \(\mu \mid D \sim \mathcal{N}(\bar x, \sigma^2/n) = \mathcal{N}(3,\,4/9)\), so the posterior mean is just \(\bar x = 3\). The proper prior shrinks the estimate from \(3\) toward the prior mean \(0\), by a factor controlled by the ratio of prior to data precision. With only \(n = 9\) samples the prior carries non-trivial weight; with \(n \to \infty\) the shrinkage vanishes.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
section: 7.4 Inference of the Variance with Known Mean
text:
7.4 Inference of the Variance with Known Mean
Now assume \(\mu\) is known and infer \(\sigma^2\). Setting \(\nu = \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2\), the likelihood is \[p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{\nu}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

Jeffreys prior for a scale parameter. Variance is a scale parameter: if we rescale \(x \to ax\), then \(\sigma^2 \to a^2\sigma^2\). As shown in Chapter 5 (§ Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, and the Jeffreys prior), the Jeffreys prior \(p_J(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\det \mathcal{I}(\theta)}\) is reparametrization-invariant. For the Gaussian scale parameter \(\sigma^2\) with known mean, the Fisher information is \(\mathcal{I}(\sigma^2) = n/[2(\sigma^2)^2]\), yielding \(p_J(\sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^2\) (or equivalently \(p_J(\log\sigma^2) \propto 1\), uniform on log-scale). This prior ensures invariance under rescaling — critical for Bayes factors. Chapter 5 gives the extended example and invariance proof; here we apply it directly.
Inverse-Gamma conjugate family. The Inverse-Gamma distribution is conjugate for variance estimation.


If \(X \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with pdf \(f(x) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta x}\), then \(1/X \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with pdf \[f(z) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} (1/z)^{\alpha+1} \exp(-\beta/z).\]

A prior \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\) combined with the likelihood yields the conjugate posterior.

Inverse-Gamma Conjugate Update: \[\sigma^2 \mid D, \mu \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}\left( \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}, \beta_0 + \frac{\nu}{2} \right),\] where \(\nu = \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2\).

The Jeffreys prior corresponds to \(\alpha_0 = 0\) and \(\beta_0 = 0\) (or \(\alpha_0 = -1/2\) to ensure integrability). The posterior mean is \(\mathbb{E}[\sigma^2 \mid D] = \beta_n/(\alpha_n - 1)\) for \(\alpha_n > 1\).
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.5 Joint Inference: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Model
The most realistic case has both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) unknown. Use a factorized prior \[p(\mu, \sigma^2) = p(\mu \mid \sigma^2) p(\sigma^2),\] with \(\mu \mid \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma^2/\kappa_0)\) and \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\). This is the Normal-Inverse-Gamma (NIG) conjugate family with hyperparameters \((\mu_0, \kappa_0, \alpha_0, \beta_0)\).

Posterior update. Combining likelihood with the NIG prior yields a posterior of the same form, with updated hyperparameters.


Normal-Inverse-Gamma Conjugate Update: \[\begin{align}
\kappa_n &= \kappa_0 + n, \\
\mu_n &= \frac{\kappa_0 \mu_0 + n\bar{x}}{\kappa_n}, \\
\alpha_n &= \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}, \\
\beta_n &= \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2} + \frac{\kappa_0 n}{2(\kappa_0 + n)} (\bar{x} - \mu_0)^2,
\end{align}\] where \(S = \sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2\).

The joint posterior factorizes as \[p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid D) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-(\alpha_n + 1)} \exp\left( -\frac{\beta_n}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\kappa_n(\mu - \mu_n)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).\]

Marginal posterior of the mean. Marginalizing \(\sigma^2\) yields a Student-\(t\) posterior for \(\mu\).


The posterior marginal distribution of \(\mu\) given \(D\) is \[\mu \mid D \sim t_{2\alpha_n}\left( \mu_n, \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n \kappa_n} \right),\] a Student-\(t\) distribution with \(\nu = 2\alpha_n\) degrees of freedom, location \(\mu_n\), and scale parameter \(\sqrt{\beta_n/(\alpha_n \kappa_n)}\).

Interpretation: the heavy tails of the \(t\)-distribution reflect additional uncertainty from not knowing \(\sigma^2\). For large \(n\), \(\nu = 2\alpha_n \approx n \to \infty\), so the \(t\)-distribution converges to a Gaussian, recovering asymptotic normality.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.6 Credible Intervals and Regions

Central credible interval for the mean. A central \((1-\alpha)\)% credible interval is \[\mu_n \pm t_{2\alpha_n, 1-\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n \kappa_n}},\] where \(t_{\nu, p}\) denotes the \(p\)-quantile of a Student-\(t\) distribution with \(\nu\) degrees of freedom. Frequentist analogue: in the large-sample limit, this recovers the familiar confidence interval \(\hat{\mu} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2/n}\).
Joint credible region. A joint \((1-\alpha)\)% credible region for \(\theta = (\mu, \sigma^2)\) is the ellipse \[(\theta - \hat{\theta})^T \mathcal{I}_n(\hat{\theta}) (\theta - \hat{\theta}) \le \chi^2_{2, 1-\alpha},\] where \(\mathcal{I}_n(\hat{\theta})\) is the Fisher information matrix evaluated at the MLE and \(\chi^2_{2, 1-\alpha}\) is the \((1-\alpha)\) quantile of the chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom.

The posterior asymptotically factorizes: the estimated correlation between \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) vanishes as \(n \to \infty\). This convergence to Gaussian form and factorization is guaranteed by the Bernstein–von Mises theorem (Chapter 5, § Fisher information, Cramér–Rao, and the Jeffreys prior), which states that the posterior becomes approximately \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\theta}_{\mathrm{ML}}, \mathcal{I}_n(\theta_*)^{-1})\) for large \(n\), independently of the prior. This is why Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist confidence intervals asymptotically agree.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.7 Bayesian Hypothesis Testing

One-sided test for the mean. Consider \(H_0: \mu \le \mu^*\) vs \(H_1: \mu > \mu^*\). The posterior probability of \(H_1\) is \[P(H_1 \mid D) = 1 - F_t\left( \frac{\mu^* - \mu_n}{\sqrt{\beta_n/(\alpha_n \kappa_n)}} \right),\] where \(F_t\) is the CDF of a Student-\(t\) distribution with \(2\alpha_n\) degrees of freedom. Decision rule: reject \(H_0\) if \(P(H_1 \mid D) > 1 - \alpha\). Equivalently, reject if \(\mu^*\) lies below the lower bound of the \((1-\alpha)\)% credible interval.
Two-sided test and Bayes factor. For \(H_0: \mu = \mu^*\) vs \(H_1: \mu \ne \mu^*\), compare the marginal likelihoods.


The Bayes factor of \(H_1\) over \(H_0\) is \[\text{BF}_{10} = \frac{p(D \mid H_1)}{p(D \mid H_0)} = \frac{\int p(D \mid \mu, \sigma^2) p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid H_1) d\mu d\sigma^2}{\int_{\mu=\mu^*} p(D \mid \mu^*, \sigma^2) p(\sigma^2 \mid H_0) d\sigma^2}.\]

Interpretation: \(\text{BF}_{10} > 1\) favors \(H_1\) (mean differs from \(\mu^*\)); \(\text{BF}_{10} < 1\) favors \(H_0\); the magnitude quantifies the strength of evidence. Unlike frequentist \(p\)-values, the Bayes factor is invariant under the choice of scale (the Jeffreys prior ensures this).

Bayesian \(t\)-test. The Bayesian \(t\)-test compares the observed \(t = (\bar{x} - \mu^*) / \sqrt{S/(n(n-1))}\) to its posterior predictive distribution under \(H_0\). With the NIG conjugate prior, the marginal likelihood under \(H_0\) is a \(t\)-distribution, naturally accounting for variance uncertainty without ad-hoc degrees-of-freedom corrections.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.8 Posterior Predictive Distribution
Given posterior \(p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid D)\), the posterior predictive distribution for a new observation \(\tilde{x}\) is \[p(\tilde{x} \mid D) = \int p(\tilde{x} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) p(\mu, \sigma^2 \mid D) d\mu d\sigma^2.\]
For the NIG model, this marginalizes to a Student-\(t\) distribution with the same degrees of freedom and location as the posterior for \(\mu\), but with inflated scale (reflecting both parameter and observational uncertainty): \[\tilde{x} \mid D \sim t_{2\alpha_n}\left( \mu_n, \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n} \left(1 + \frac{1}{\kappa_n}\right) \right).\]
The posterior predictive intervals are wider than credible intervals for the mean, appropriately quantifying full predictive uncertainty.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.9 Frequentist Contrast
The frequentist approach uses the sample \(t\)-statistic \[t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sqrt{S / (n(n-1))}},\] which under \(H_0: \mu = \mu^*\) follows \(t_{n-1}\). Rejection is based on \(p\)-value: \(p = P(|T| \ge |t_{\text{obs}}| \mid H_0)\).
Key differences from Bayes:

Frequentist \(t\)-test: assumes \(\mu^*\) exactly true under \(H_0\); \(p\)-value is the probability of observing data as extreme or more extreme under repeated sampling.
Bayesian \(t\)-test: posterior probability that \(\mu > \mu^*\) (or any specified region); updates beliefs given observed data; allows flexible loss functions for decision-making.
Sample size: Frequentist \(p\)-values can remain small for minor effects with large \(n\). Bayes factors adapt: as data accumulate under \(H_1\), they grow; but if data weakly support \(H_1\) relative to prediction, BF remains modest.
Asymptotic equivalence: For fixed effect size and large \(n\), frequentist confidence intervals and Bayesian credible intervals converge; the posterior becomes approximately Gaussian by Bernstein–von Mises.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.10 Illustrative Examples


Show code
set.seed(42)

mu_true <- 0.5
sigma_true <- 1.0

mu0 <- 0
sigma0 <- 2
n_list <- c(2, 20, 100)

mu_grid <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 2000)

par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(NA, xlim = c(-3, 3), ylim = c(0, 2.5), xlab = expression(mu), ylab = "Density", main = "")

for (i in seq_along(n_list)) {
  n <- n_list[i]
  x <- rnorm(n, mu_true, sigma_true)
  xbar <- mean(x)

  kappa_n <- 1/sigma0^2 + n/sigma_true^2
  mu_n <- (mu0/sigma0^2 + n*xbar/sigma_true^2) / kappa_n
  sigma_n <- sqrt(1/kappa_n)

  posterior_density <- dnorm(mu_grid, mu_n, sigma_n)
  col_vec <- c("black", "gray40", "#b8123e")
  lines(mu_grid, posterior_density, lwd = 1, lty = i, col = col_vec[i])
}

prior_density <- dnorm(mu_grid, mu0, sigma0)
lines(mu_grid, prior_density, lwd = 1, lty = 4, col = "gray70")

legend("topright", legend = c("Prior", "n=2", "n=20", "n=100"),
       lty = c(4, 1, 2, 3), col = c("gray70", "black", "gray40", "#b8123e"),
       lwd = 1, bty = "n", cex = 0.75)





Posterior distribution for the mean under Normal-Normal conjugate model with known variance. As sample size increases (n=2, n=20, n=100), the posterior concentrates around the MLE and becomes narrower.






Show code
set.seed(123)

n <- 50
mu_true <- 0
sigma_true <- 1

x <- rnorm(n, mu_true, sigma_true)
xbar <- mean(x)
S <- sum((x - xbar)^2)

mu_hat <- xbar
sigma2_hat <- S / n

theta_hat <- c(mu_hat, sigma2_hat)

Sigma <- matrix(c(sigma2_hat / n, 0, 0, 2 * sigma2_hat^2 / n), 2, 2)

alpha_level <- 0.05
chi2_val <- qchisq(1 - alpha_level, df = 2)

eig <- eigen(Sigma)
A <- eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(eig$values * chi2_val))

angles <- seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = 200)
circle <- rbind(cos(angles), sin(angles))
ellipse_mat <- A %*% circle
ellipse_df <- data.frame(
  mu = ellipse_mat[1,] + theta_hat[1],
  sigma2 = ellipse_mat[2,] + theta_hat[2]
)

par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(ellipse_df$mu, ellipse_df$sigma2, type = "l", lwd = 1,
     xlab = expression(mu), ylab = expression(sigma^2),
     main = paste("n =", n),
     xlim = c(theta_hat[1] - 0.4, theta_hat[1] + 0.4),
     ylim = c(max(0, theta_hat[2] - 0.5), theta_hat[2] + 0.5))

points(theta_hat[1], theta_hat[2], pch = 19, col = "#b8123e", cex = 1)

legend("topright", legend = c("95% credible ellipse", "MLE"),
       lty = c(1, NA), pch = c(NA, 19), col = c("black", "#b8123e"),
       lwd = 1, bty = "n", cex = 0.75)





95% credible region (ellipse) for the mean and variance. Shows asymptotic factorization: mean and variance are nearly uncorrelated in the large-sample limit.






Show code
mu_star <- 0
alpha0 <- 1.1
beta0 <- 0.5
kappa0 <- 0.01
mu0 <- 0

n <- 30
sigma_true <- 1

xbar_grid <- seq(-1, 2, length.out = 100)

log10_bf <- numeric(length(xbar_grid))

for (j in seq_along(xbar_grid)) {
  xbar <- xbar_grid[j]

  kappa_n <- kappa0 + n
  mu_n <- (kappa0*mu0 + n*xbar) / kappa_n
  alpha_n <- alpha0 + n/2

  S_approx <- 0
  beta_n <- beta0 + S_approx/2 + (kappa0*n)/(2*(kappa0+n)) * (xbar - mu0)^2

  log_bf <- 0.5*log(kappa0) - 0.5*log(kappa_n) +
            alpha0*log(beta0) - alpha_n*log(beta_n) +
            lgamma(alpha_n) - lgamma(alpha0) -
            0.5*log(pi) + 0.5*log(2*alpha_n) +
            lgamma(alpha_n - 0.5) - lgamma(alpha0 - 0.5)

  log10_bf[j] <- log_bf / log(10)
}

par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(xbar_grid, log10_bf, type = "l", lwd = 1,
     xlab = expression(bar(x)), ylab = expression(log[10]*" BF"[1*","*0]),
     main = paste("Test: mu > 0, n =", n), col = "black")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray70", lwd = 1)
abline(v = mu_star, lty = 2, col = "gray40", lwd = 1)

legend("bottomright", legend = c("BF > 1: favor H_1", "BF = 1: indifferent", "BF < 1: favor H_0"),
       bty = "n", cex = 0.75)





Bayes factor BF_{1,0} (base 10 log scale) versus observed mean, for test H_0: mu <= 0 vs H_1: mu > 0. Shows transition from evidence favoring H_0 to H_1 as sample mean increases.
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.11 Exercises

Conjugate Normal–Normal model with known variance. Observe \(n = 4\) measurements with sample mean \(\bar x = 2\) from \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 = 1)\), with prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\). Compute the posterior mean and variance.


Solution. Precisions add: \(\kappa_n = 1/\sigma_0^2 + n/\sigma^2 = 1 + 4 = 5\), so \(V_n = \kappa_n^{-1} = 1/5\). The posterior mean is the precision-weighted average \[\mu_n = V_n\!\left(\frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{n\bar x}{\sigma^2}\right) = \frac{1}{5}\,(0 + 4\cdot 2) = \frac{8}{5}.\] Hence \(\mu \mid D \sim \mathcal{N}(8/5,\ 1/5)\).


Same model as above. As \(n \to \infty\) with \(\bar x \to \mu_*\) fixed, what does the posterior tend to? State the result in terms of \(\mu_*\) and \(\sigma^2\).


Solution. The data precision \(n/\sigma^2\) dominates the prior precision \(1/\sigma_0^2\), so \(\kappa_n \to n/\sigma^2\) and \(\mu_n \to \bar x \to \mu_*\). Therefore \[\mu \mid D \ \xrightarrow{n\to\infty}\ \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_*,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right),\] which collapses to a point mass at \(\mu_*\) and matches the flat-prior result, illustrating Bernstein–von Mises.


Inference of the variance with known mean. Observe \(n\) i.i.d. samples from \(\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\) with sum of squares \(S = \sum_i x_i^2\). Use the conjugate Inverse-Gamma prior \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{IG}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\) and derive the posterior.


Solution. The likelihood is \(p(D\mid\sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp(-S/(2\sigma^2))\), and the prior is \(p(\sigma^2)\propto (\sigma^2)^{-(\alpha_0+1)}\exp(-\beta_0/\sigma^2)\). Multiplying gives an IG kernel with \[\sigma^2 \mid D \sim \text{IG}\!\left(\alpha_0 + \tfrac{n}{2},\ \beta_0 + \tfrac{S}{2}\right) = \text{IG}(\alpha_n, \beta_n).\]


Show that \((\bar x,\,s^2) = (\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i x_i,\ \tfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_i (x_i - \bar x)^2)\) are sufficient statistics for \((\mu, \sigma^2)\) in the i.i.d. \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) model, using the factorisation theorem.


Solution. Using \(\sum_i(x_i-\mu)^2 = (n-1)s^2 + n(\bar x - \mu)^2\), the joint density factorises as \[p(D\mid\mu,\sigma^2) = \underbrace{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{(n-1)s^2 + n(\bar x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)}_{g(\bar x, s^2;\,\mu,\sigma^2)}\cdot\underbrace{1}_{h(D)}.\] Since the data enter only through \((\bar x, s^2)\), the Fisher–Neyman factorisation theorem gives sufficiency.


Conjugate Normal–Inverse-Gamma joint posterior. Under prior \((\mu, \sigma^2) \sim \text{NIG}(\mu_0, \kappa_0, \alpha_0, \beta_0)\), write the posterior parameters \((\mu_n, \kappa_n, \alpha_n, \beta_n)\) after observing \(n\) samples with sample mean \(\bar x\) and sum of squares from \(\bar x\) equal to \(S_{xx}\).


Solution. Conjugacy preserves the NIG form with updates \[\begin{align}
\kappa_n &= \kappa_0 + n, &\mu_n &= \frac{\kappa_0\mu_0 + n\bar x}{\kappa_n},\\
\alpha_n &= \alpha_0 + \tfrac{n}{2}, &\beta_n &= \beta_0 + \tfrac{S_{xx}}{2} + \frac{\kappa_0 n}{2(\kappa_0+n)}(\bar x - \mu_0)^2.
\end{align}\] The last term in \(\beta_n\) penalises prior–data disagreement between \(\mu_0\) and \(\bar x\).


For the NIG conjugate model, derive the marginal posterior \(p(\mu \mid D)\) and identify it as a Student-\(t\) distribution. State its location, scale, and degrees of freedom.


Solution. Integrating \(\sigma^2\) out of the joint NIG posterior gives an IG integral in \(\sigma^2\) with shape \(\alpha_n + 1/2\) and rate \(\beta_n + \kappa_n(\mu-\mu_n)^2/2\), leading to \[p(\mu\mid D) \propto \left[1 + \frac{\kappa_n(\mu-\mu_n)^2}{2\beta_n}\right]^{-(\alpha_n + 1/2)},\] i.e. a Student-\(t\) with \(\nu = 2\alpha_n\) degrees of freedom, location \(\mu_n\), and squared scale \(\beta_n/(\alpha_n\kappa_n)\).


Posterior predictive in the conjugate Normal model with known \(\sigma^2\). Derive \(p(x_{\text{new}} \mid D)\) when \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)\) and the data have sample mean \(\bar x\).


Solution. With posterior \(\mu\mid D \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, V_n)\) where \(V_n = \kappa_n^{-1}\), and \(x_{\text{new}}\mid\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\), the sum of independent Gaussians gives \[x_{\text{new}}\mid D \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_n,\ V_n + \sigma^2\right).\] Parameter uncertainty contributes \(V_n\) and irreducible noise contributes \(\sigma^2\).


For the conjugate model with known \(\sigma^2\), derive the \(95\%\) equal-tailed credible interval for \(\mu\) using the conjugate posterior parameters \((\mu_n, V_n)\). Use \(z_{0.975} \approx 2\).


Solution. Since \(\mu\mid D \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, V_n)\), the equal-tailed \(95\%\) interval is \[\mu_n \pm z_{0.975}\sqrt{V_n} \approx \mu_n \pm 2\sqrt{V_n}.\]


Sequential updating. With known \(\sigma^2\) and prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\), you observe data in two batches with sample means \(\bar x_1, \bar x_2\) and counts \(n_1, n_2\). Show that updating sequentially gives the same posterior as updating in one batch.


Solution. After batch 1: \(\kappa_1 = 1/\tau^2 + n_1/\sigma^2\) and \(\mu_1 = \kappa_1^{-1}(n_1\bar x_1/\sigma^2)\). Using this as prior for batch 2: \[\kappa_n = \kappa_1 + n_2/\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{\tau^2} + \frac{n_1+n_2}{\sigma^2},\qquad \mu_n = \kappa_n^{-1}\!\left(\kappa_1\mu_1 + \frac{n_2\bar x_2}{\sigma^2}\right) = \kappa_n^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_1\bar x_1 + n_2\bar x_2}{\sigma^2}\right),\] which is identical to the one-shot update with pooled mean \((n_1\bar x_1 + n_2\bar x_2)/(n_1+n_2)\) and count \(n_1+n_2\). Precisions and precision-weighted means simply concatenate.


Posterior predictive variance decomposition. Show that the posterior predictive variance in the conjugate Normal model is \(V_n + \sigma^2\), and identify which term is parameter uncertainty and which is irreducible noise.


Solution. By the law of total variance, \[\mathrm{Var}(x_{\text{new}}\mid D) = \mathbb{E}_\mu[\mathrm{Var}(x_{\text{new}}\mid\mu)] + \mathrm{Var}_\mu[\mathbb{E}(x_{\text{new}}\mid\mu)] = \sigma^2 + V_n.\] Here \(\sigma^2\) is the irreducible observation noise and \(V_n = \kappa_n^{-1}\) is the posterior (parameter) uncertainty about \(\mu\).


Multivariate conjugate update. For \(\mathbf x \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol\mu, \boldsymbol\Sigma)\) with \(\boldsymbol\Sigma\) known and prior \(\boldsymbol\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol\mu_0, \boldsymbol\Sigma_0)\), write the posterior \(p(\boldsymbol\mu \mid D)\) after observing one sample \(\mathbf x\).


Solution. Precision matrices add and the posterior mean is precision-weighted: \[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n^{-1} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_0^{-1} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1},\qquad \boldsymbol{\mu}_n = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n\!\left(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_0^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_0 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf x\right),\] so \(\boldsymbol{\mu}\mid D \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n)\).


Bayesian \(z\)-test. With prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\) and known \(\sigma^2\), compute the Bayes factor between \(H_0:\mu = 0\) and \(H_1:\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\) as a function of the standardised sample mean \(z = \bar x \sqrt{n}/\sigma\). (You can reuse the worked example “Closed-form Bayes factor for a Gaussian mean” in Chapter 4.)


Solution. Let \(g = n\tau^2/\sigma^2\) measure prior relative to data precision. The marginal likelihood under \(H_1\) inflates the data variance by the prior, while \(H_0\) uses just \(\sigma^2/n\). Standard Gaussian marginalisation (Ch. 4) gives \[\mathrm{BF}_{10} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+g}}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{g}{1+g}\cdot\frac{z^2}{2}\right).\] Large \(|z|\) exponentially favours \(H_1\); the prefactor \((1+g)^{-1/2}\) is the Ockham penalty for the broader alternative.


Improper Jeffreys prior for the variance. Take \(p(\sigma^2) \propto 1/\sigma^2\). Show that the posterior for \(\sigma^2\) given \(n\) i.i.d. \(\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\) samples is Inverse-Gamma, and identify its parameters.


Solution. The Jeffreys prior is the formal limit \(\alpha_0 \to 0,\ \beta_0 \to 0\) of \(\text{IG}(\alpha_0,\beta_0)\). Multiplying by the likelihood \(\propto (\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\exp(-S/(2\sigma^2))\) with \(S = \sum_i x_i^2\) gives \[\sigma^2 \mid D \sim \text{IG}\!\left(\alpha_n = \tfrac{n}{2},\ \beta_n = \tfrac{S}{2}\right),\] which is proper for \(n \ge 1\).


Posterior comparison. With \(n = 9\), \(\sigma^2 = 4\), \(\bar x = 3\): compute the posterior mean for \(\mu\) under the flat prior, the \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) prior, and the \(\mathcal{N}(0, 100)\) prior. Comment on how the posterior depends on prior width.


Solution. Data precision is \(n/\sigma^2 = 9/4 = 2.25\). The precision-weighted-mean form \(\mu_n = (n\bar x/\sigma^2)/(1/\sigma_0^2 + n/\sigma^2)\) gives \[\mu_n^{\text{flat}} = \bar x = 3,\qquad \mu_n^{(\sigma_0^2 = 1)} = \frac{27/4}{1 + 9/4} = \frac{27}{13} \approx 2.08,\qquad \mu_n^{(\sigma_0^2 = 100)} = \frac{27/4}{0.01 + 9/4} \approx 2.99.\] A tight prior (\(\sigma_0^2 = 1\)) shrinks the estimate sharply toward \(\mu_0 = 0\); a vague prior (\(\sigma_0^2 = 100\)) recovers the flat-prior MLE to within \(1\%\). Posterior dependence on prior width vanishes as \(\sigma_0^2 \to \infty\).


Sample size and prior dominance. For the conjugate Normal–Normal model with prior precision \(\tau_0\) and likelihood precision \(n/\sigma^2\), find the sample size \(n^\star\) such that the posterior is half-determined by the prior and half by the data.


Solution. Equal weighting means prior precision equals data precision: \(\tau_0 = n^\star/\sigma^2\), so \[n^\star = \tau_0\,\sigma^2.\] For \(n \ll n^\star\) the prior dominates, while for \(n \gg n^\star\) the likelihood dominates and the posterior approaches the flat-prior result.
