title: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian
text:
7.12 Sources
Sivia, D.S. & Skilling, J. (2006). Data Analysis: A Bayesian Tutorial (2nd ed.). Oxford University Press. Ch. 3 (Gaussian estimation).
van der Linden, W.J. (2016). Bayesian Assessment and Reduction of Uncertainty in Measurement. Springer. Ch. 17 (Bayesian z-test, \(t\)-test).
Falasco, G. (2026). Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Bayesian Inference. Lecture Notes. §5.4 (Gaussian mean and variance).
crumbs: 7  Bayesian inference for the Gaussian

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.1 Motivation and the curse of dimensionality
In Bayesian inference, the central computational challenge is to evaluate expectations of the form \[\mathbb{E}_\pi[f(\theta)] = \int f(\theta) \pi(\theta \mid D) \, d\theta,\] where \(\pi(\theta \mid D)\) is the posterior distribution. These integrals appear everywhere: computing the evidence, credible intervals, point estimates, and predictions. Most realistic models lack analytical solutions, especially in high dimensions.
Classical numerical integration methods fail catastrophically in high dimensions. Consider a grid-based quadrature with \(n\) points per dimension in a \(d\)-dimensional space. The total number of grid points grows as \(N = n^d\)—exponential in dimension. This is the curse of dimensionality for deterministic methods.
Monte Carlo to the rescue. Monte Carlo methods replace high-dimensional integration with averaging over random samples. The fundamental principle is
\[\mathbb{E}_p[f(X)] \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i), \quad x_i \sim p(x).\]
By the law of large numbers, this average converges to the true expectation as \(N \to \infty\). Crucially, the error does not depend explicitly on dimension.
However, there is a catch: while the error rate is independent of dimension, the variance \(\text{Var}_p(f)\) itself can grow exponentially with \(d\). For the hypercube example, the variance approaches zero as \(d \to \infty\) (since \(I_d \to 0\)), but the relative error \[\frac{\sqrt{\text{Var}(\widehat{I}_N)}}{\mathbb{E}[\widehat{I}_N]} \sim \frac{1}{\sqrt{N I_d}} \to \infty\] explodes exponentially. We need \(N \sim 1/I_d\) to maintain constant relative error—which is infeasible for rare events. Techniques like rejection sampling and importance sampling exist precisely to mitigate this degeneracy by concentrating samples where the integrand is large.
Show code
# True integral: volume of ball of radius 0.5 in [0,1]^d
true_val <- function(d) {
  pi^(d/2) / gamma(d/2 + 1) * 2^(-2*d)
}

# Naive MC
dims <- c(2, 5, 10, 15, 20)
N <- 1e6
results <- data.frame(d = dims, est = NA, rel_err = NA)

for (i in seq_along(dims)) {
  d <- dims[i]
  x <- matrix(runif(N * d), ncol = d)
  inside <- rowSums(x^2) < 0.25
  est <- mean(inside)
  true <- true_val(d)
  rel_err <- if (true > 0) abs(est - true) / true else NA
  results$est[i] <- est
  results$rel_err[i] <- rel_err
}

# Plot
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 5, 3, 1))

plot(results$d, results$est, type = "o", pch = 16, cex = 1.2, lwd = 2,
     col = rgb(0.2, 0.4, 0.8), log = "y",
     xlab = "Dimension d", ylab = "Estimated I_d",
     main = "Hypersphere volume (log scale)", ylim = c(1e-15, 1))


Warning in xy.coords(x, y, xlabel, ylabel, log): 2 y values <= 0 omitted from
logarithmic plot


Show code
lines(results$d, true_val(results$d), col = "red", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("MC estimate", "True value"),
       col = c(rgb(0.2, 0.4, 0.8), "red"), lwd = 2, pch = c(16, NA))

plot(results$d, results$rel_err, type = "o", pch = 16, cex = 1.2, lwd = 2,
     col = rgb(0.8, 0.2, 0.2), log = "y",
     xlab = "Dimension d", ylab = "Relative error",
     main = "Relative error (log scale)")





Naive uniform sampling in high-dimensional hypersphere: both relative and absolute error grow exponentially with dimension.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
Consider the integral over a unit hypercube \([0,1]^d\) with an indicator function that counts volume inside a ball of radius \(r = 1/2\) centered at the origin: \[I_d = \int_{[0,1]^d} \mathbb{1}_{\|x\| < 1/2} \, dx.\]
The volume of the region is proportional to \(r^d\) while the hypercube has volume 1. As \(d\) grows, nearly all of the hypercube’s volume lies outside the ball (since \(V_{\text{ball}} / V_{\text{cube}} \sim (1/2)^d \to 0\)). A uniform grid wastes almost all points. Even with \(N = 10^6\) samples in dimension \(d = 20\), we expect essentially zero hits, making the variance unbounded and estimation impossible.





Let \(x_1, \ldots, x_N \sim p(x)\) be i.i.d. samples. The Monte Carlo estimator \[\widehat{I}_N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i)\] satisfies: - Unbiasedness: \(\mathbb{E}[\widehat{I}_N] = \mathbb{E}_p[f(X)]\) - Variance: \(\text{Var}(\widehat{I}_N) = \frac{1}{N} \text{Var}_p(f)\) - Convergence: \(\widehat{I}_N - \mathbb{E}_p[f] = \mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})\)
The error scales universally as \(N^{-1/2}\) regardless of dimension \(d\).




Explain why Monte Carlo methods are central in Bayesian inference. What is the difference between using independent posterior samples and samples generated by a Markov chain?
Solution. Bayesian inference is built on posterior expectations, \[E[f(\theta) \mid D] = \int f(\theta)\,p(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta.\] In moderate to high dimensions, deterministic grid integration becomes impractical because the number of grid points grows exponentially with the parameter dimension (curse of dimensionality). Monte Carlo replaces the integral by an average over samples: \[E[f(\theta) \mid D] \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(\theta_i), \qquad \theta_i \sim p(\theta \mid D).\] If the samples \(\theta_i\) are independent, the variance of the estimator scales as \(\text{Var}(f)/N\). With MCMC the samples are correlated by construction, so the variance is inflated by the integrated autocorrelation time \(\tau_{\text{int}}\): \[\text{Var}(\widehat I_N) \approx \frac{\tau_{\text{int}}}{N}\,\text{Var}_\pi(f).\] The relevant sample size is therefore the effective sample size \(N_{\text{eff}} \approx N/\tau_{\text{int}}\), not the raw chain length. MCMC remains valid after burn-in and convergence to the stationary distribution, but reporting \(N_{\text{eff}}\) — not \(N\) — is essential.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.2 Direct sampling: inverse transform method
The most straightforward sampling approach is the inverse transform method (or inverse CDF method). It is exact and works whenever the CDF can be computed and inverted analytically.

Let \(F(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^x p(x') \, dx'\) be the cumulative distribution function of a continuous random variable \(X \sim p(x)\). The inverse transform method generates samples by: 1. Compute \(F(x)\) and its inverse \(F^{-1}(u)\) (the quantile function). 2. Sample \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\). 3. Set \(X = F^{-1}(U)\).
Then \(X \sim p(x)\).

Proof: By the probability integral transform, \[P(X \le x) = P(F^{-1}(U) \le x) = P(U \le F(x)) = F(x),\] so the distribution of \(X\) is \(p(x)\) as required.
Example: exponential distribution. For \(p(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) with \(x \ge 0\), the CDF is \(F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}\). Inverting \(u = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}\) gives \[x = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \log(1-u).\] Since \(1 - U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\) when \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\), we have \[X = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \log(U).\]


Show code
N <- 10000
u <- runif(N)
lambda <- 2
x <- -log(u) / lambda
x_builtin <- rexp(N, rate = lambda)

hist(x, probability = TRUE, col = rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.4),
     breaks = 50, main = "Inverse transform vs. rexp",
     xlab = "x", xlim = c(0, 3))
hist(x_builtin, probability = TRUE, col = rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.4),
     breaks = 50, add = TRUE)
curve(dexp(x, rate = lambda), col = "darkred", lwd = 2, add = TRUE)
legend("topright", legend = c("Inverse transform", "rexp"),
       fill = c(rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.4), rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.4)))





Overlaid histograms show agreement between inverse-transform (red) and built-in (blue) exponential samples.




The inverse transform method is exact, simple, and efficient in one dimension. However, it requires analytical inversion of \(F\), which is rarely available for complex distributions (e.g., posteriors) or in high dimensions.

Let \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\) and define \(X = -\lambda^{-1}\log(1 - U)\) with \(\lambda > 0\). Find the density of \(X\) and explain the Monte Carlo interpretation.
Solution. For \(x \geq 0\), \[P(X \leq x) = P\!\left(-\lambda^{-1}\log(1 - U) \leq x\right) = P\!\left(U \leq 1 - e^{-\lambda x}\right) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}.\] Differentiating gives \(f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) for \(x \geq 0\). Thus the inverse-CDF construction transforms uniform random numbers into exponential waiting times with rate \(\lambda\). This is the simplest non-trivial Monte Carlo recipe and underlies almost every random-number generator in Poisson process simulation.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.3 Rejection sampling
When the target density \(\pi(x)\) is difficult to sample from directly, rejection sampling uses a proposal distribution \(q(x)\) from which sampling is easy, and accepts or rejects each proposal to asymptotically recover the target.

Suppose we want to sample from a target \(\pi(x) = \tilde{\pi}(x) / Z\) where \(\tilde{\pi}(x)\) is the unnormalized density and \(Z\) is unknown. Choose a proposal density \(q(x)\) with the same support. If there exists \(M > 0\) such that \[\tilde{\pi}(x) \le M \, q(x) \quad \forall x,\] then the following algorithm produces samples from \(\pi(x)\):
Rejection Sampling Algorithm: 1. Sample \(x \sim q(x)\). 2. Sample \(u \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\). 3. Accept \(x\) if \(u \le \tilde{\pi}(x) / (M q(x))\); else reject and repeat.
The accepted samples are i.i.d. from \(\pi(x)\).

Correctness: The joint density of proposing and accepting is \[p(x, \text{accept}) = q(x) \cdot \frac{\tilde{\pi}(x)}{M q(x)} = \frac{\tilde{\pi}(x)}{M}.\] The marginal acceptance probability is \[P(\text{accept}) = \int \frac{\tilde{\pi}(x)}{M} \, dx = \frac{Z}{M},\] and conditioning on acceptance yields \[p(x \mid \text{accept}) = \frac{\tilde{\pi}(x)/M}{Z/M} = \frac{\tilde{\pi}(x)}{Z} = \pi(x).\]
Acceptance ratio and efficiency. The acceptance probability is \(P(\text{accept}) = Z / M\). For efficiency, we want \(M\) as small as possible (ideally \(M^* = \max_x [\tilde{\pi}(x) / q(x)]\)). When acceptance probability is low, most computational effort is wasted on rejected proposals.
In high dimensions, rejection sampling becomes pathologically inefficient. Consider sampling from a Gaussian target in dimension \(d\). A reasonably-scaled proposal will have acceptance probability that typically decays as \(e^{-\alpha d}\) for some constant \(\alpha > 0\). The algorithm wastes an exponentially growing fraction of samples, making it impractical for large \(d\).

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.4 Importance sampling
Importance sampling avoids the rejection step by using weighted samples. Every sample is retained, but weighted by how much the proposal underestimates the target.

To compute \(\mathbb{E}_\pi[f(\theta)] = \int f(\theta) \pi(\theta) \, d\theta\), rewrite the integral in terms of a proposal \(q(\theta)\) with the same support: \[\mathbb{E}_\pi[f] = \int f(\theta) \pi(\theta) \, d\theta = \int f(\theta) \frac{\pi(\theta)}{q(\theta)} q(\theta) \, d\theta = \mathbb{E}_q\left[ f(\theta) w(\theta) \right],\] where the importance weight is \[w(\theta) = \frac{\pi(\theta)}{q(\theta)}.\]
Sample \(\theta_i \sim q(\theta)\) i.i.d. and estimate \[\widehat{I} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(\theta_i) w(\theta_i).\] This is unbiased when \(\pi\) is known up to a normalizing constant.

Self-normalized importance sampling. In practice, the target \(\pi(\theta)\) is often known only up to normalization: \(\pi(\theta) = \tilde{\pi}(\theta) / Z\) where \(Z\) is unknown (as in Bayesian posteriors). Use unnormalized weights \(\tilde{w}_i = \tilde{\pi}(\theta_i) / q(\theta_i)\) and estimate via the ratio \[\widehat{I} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^N f(\theta_i) \tilde{w}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^N \tilde{w}_i}.\] This estimator is biased but consistent (converges to the true value as \(N \to \infty\)).
The efficiency of importance sampling depends critically on how well the weights match the target distribution. If the proposal \(q\) is far from the target \(\pi\), most weight concentrates on a few samples, and the effective sample size collapses.

The effective sample size (ESS) quantifies this degeneracy: \[\text{ESS} = \frac{(\sum_i \tilde{w}_i)^2}{\sum_i \tilde{w}_i^2}.\]
Intuitively, if all weights are equal (\(\tilde{w}_i = \tilde{w}^*\)), then \(\text{ESS} = N\). If one weight dominates, \(\text{ESS} \ll N\), signaling weight degeneracy. As a rule of thumb, \(\text{ESS} < N / 10\) indicates serious efficiency loss.

Optimal proposal. The variance of the importance-sampling estimator is proportional to \(\mathbb{E}_q[(f(X) w(X))^2]\). By calculus of variations, the variance-minimizing proposal is \[q^*(x) \propto |f(x)| \pi(x).\] However, this optimal proposal is itself as difficult to sample from as \(\pi\), so it is rarely useful in practice. Practical guidance: match the shape and tail behavior of \(\pi\), ensure heavy tails to avoid undersampling the tail regions, and capture correlations.
High-dimensional limitations. In very high dimensions, any suboptimal proposal leads to catastrophic weight degeneracy. The mass of \(\pi\) concentrates in thin, high-curvature regions (e.g., the typical set of a high-dimensional Gaussian). Unless \(q\) places comparable mass in those regions, the ESS shrinks exponentially with dimension. This is a fundamental curse of dimensionality for importance sampling, which motivates the turn to Markov chain methods.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.5 Variance reduction
Before moving to MCMC, two classical variance-reduction tricks deserve mention.

Antithetic sampling: Use correlated pairs to reduce variance. If \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\), sample both \(U\) and \(1-U\) (which is also uniform). Since \(f(F^{-1}(U))\) and \(f(F^{-1}(1-U))\) are negatively correlated for many smooth functions, averaging them reduces variance.
Control variates: If you know the expectation of a related function \(g(x)\) under \(\pi\), use \[\widehat{I}_{\text{cv}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i [f(x_i) - c(g(x_i) - \mathbb{E}[g])]\] for a suitably chosen \(c\). The term \(c(g(x_i) - \mathbb{E}[g])\) has zero mean but can have large negative correlation with \(f(x_i) - \mathbb{E}[f]\), reducing variance.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.6 Markov chains and stationarity
When neither inverse sampling, rejection, nor importance sampling are practical, we turn to Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). The key idea is to construct a stochastic dynamics whose long-time distribution equals the target \(\pi(x)\).

A Markov chain is a discrete-time stochastic process \(\{X_t\}_{t \ge 0}\) where the next state depends only on the current state (the Markov property): \[P(X_{t+1} = x' \mid X_t = x, X_{t-1}, \ldots, X_0) = P(X_{t+1} = x' \mid X_t = x) \equiv T(x \to x').\]
The transition operator \(T(x \to x')\) is the core object; it specifies how the chain evolves in a single step.

Stationarity: A distribution \(\pi(x)\) is stationary with respect to \(T\) if \[\pi(x) = \sum_{x'} T(x' \to x) \pi(x'),\] or in integral form, \[\pi(x) = \int T(x' \to x) \pi(x') \, dx'.\] Under one transition step, the distribution remains unchanged.
Convergence: For the chain to be useful, we need it to converge to \(\pi\) starting from any initial distribution \(p_0\). This requires: - Irreducibility: all states (or regions) are mutually reachable with positive probability. - Aperiodicity: the chain does not cycle with a fixed period.
Under these conditions, the distribution evolves as \[p_t(x) = T^t p_0(x) \xrightarrow{t \to \infty} \pi(x).\]

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.7 Detailed balance and reversibility
A sufficient condition for stationarity is detailed balance: \[\pi(x) T(x \to x') = \pi(x') T(x' \to x) \quad \forall x, x'.\]
This says that the probability current from \(x\) to \(x'\) balances the current from \(x'\) to \(x\) at equilibrium. While not necessary for stationarity (some stationary distributions have non-zero net currents), detailed balance is mathematically convenient and physically intuitive—it ensures the chain is reversible.

A Markov chain on states \(\{0, 1\}\) has transition probabilities \(P(0 \to 1) = a\) and \(P(1 \to 0) = b\), with \(a, b \in (0, 1)\). Find the stationary distribution and verify detailed balance.
Solution. Let the stationary distribution be \((\pi_0, \pi_1)\). Stationarity requires the probability flow out of \(0\) to equal the flow in: \[\pi_0\,a = \pi_1\,b, \qquad \pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1.\] Solving, \[\pi_0 = \frac{b}{a + b}, \qquad \pi_1 = \frac{a}{a + b}.\] Detailed balance asks \(\pi_0\,P(0 \to 1) = \pi_1\,P(1 \to 0)\). Substituting, \[\frac{b}{a + b}\,a = \frac{a}{a + b}\,b,\] which holds trivially. In two-state chains stationarity and detailed balance are equivalent — any stationary distribution is automatically reversible.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.8 Metropolis–Hastings algorithm
The Metropolis–Hastings (MH) algorithm constructs a transition operator satisfying detailed balance by combining a proposal \(q(x' \mid x)\) with an accept/reject step.

Given a target \(\pi(x)\) (known up to normalization), the MH transition is \[T(x \to x') = q(x' \mid x) \, \alpha(x \to x'),\] where the acceptance probability is \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(x') q(x \mid x')}{\pi(x) q(x' \mid x)}\right).\]
This is the MH ratio or Hastings ratio.

Proof of detailed balance: Define the ratio \(r(x, x') = \frac{\pi(x') q(x \mid x')}{\pi(x) q(x' \mid x)}\).
Case 1: \(r \ge 1\). Then \(\alpha(x \to x') = 1\) and \(\alpha(x' \to x) = 1/r\), so \[\pi(x) q(x' \mid x) \alpha(x \to x') = \pi(x) q(x' \mid x) = \pi(x') q(x \mid x') \cdot \frac{1}{r} = \pi(x') q(x \mid x') \alpha(x' \to x).\]
Case 2: \(r < 1\). Then \(\alpha(x \to x') = r\) and \(\alpha(x' \to x) = 1\), giving the same identity.
Thus detailed balance holds, and \(\pi\) is stationary. \(\square\)
Practical MH algorithm. One step from the current state \(x_t\):

Propose \(x' \sim q(x' \mid x_t)\).
Compute the ratio \(r = \pi(x') q(x_t \mid x') / [\pi(x_t) q(x' \mid x_t)]\).
Draw \(u \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\).
Accept if \(u < \min(1, r)\) and set \(x_{t+1} = x'\); reject otherwise and set \(x_{t+1} = x_t\).

For a symmetric proposal \(q(x' \mid x) = q(x \mid x')\) (e.g., a Gaussian random walk), the acceptance probability simplifies to \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(x')}{\pi(x)}\right).\]

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.9 Convergence and ergodicity
After an initial burn-in period during which the chain forgets its starting point, the samples \(x_t\) are approximately distributed as \(\pi(x)\). Estimates are formed as time averages: \[\widehat{I} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^N f(x_t) \approx \mathbb{E}_\pi[f].\]
However, successive samples are correlated. The integrated autocorrelation time \[\tau_{\text{int}} = 1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^\infty \rho_f(k)\] quantifies this correlation, where \(\rho_f(k)\) is the normalized autocorrelation at lag \(k\). The effective number of independent samples is \[N_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{N}{2 \tau_{\text{int}}},\] which can be much smaller than the total number of draws.
The decay of autocorrelation is controlled by the spectral gap of the transition operator—the inverse of the largest non-zero eigenvalue. A small gap indicates slow mixing and long memory, forcing either longer chains or better proposals.

A single MCMC chain has \(N = 20\,000\) stored samples. The estimated normalised autocorrelation is approximately \(\rho(\tau) = 0.8^\tau\) for \(\tau \geq 1\). Estimate \(\tau_{\text{int}}\) and \(N_{\text{eff}}\) using \[\tau_{\text{int}} = 1 + 2\sum_{\tau=1}^\infty \rho(\tau).\]
Solution. The tail is a geometric series: \[\sum_{\tau=1}^\infty 0.8^\tau = \frac{0.8}{1 - 0.8} = 4.\] Hence \[\tau_{\text{int}} = 1 + 2\cdot 4 = 9, \qquad N_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{N}{\tau_{\text{int}}} = \frac{20\,000}{9} \approx 2\,222.\] The chain contains far less information than its raw length suggests — long-memory autocorrelation discounts every additional sample.


For a stationary Markov chain \(\{x_t\}_{t=1}^N\) and an observable \(A(x)\), define the autocorrelation function, the normalised autocorrelation function, and the integrated autocorrelation time. If \(M = 5\) independent chains each have length \(N = 10^4\) and the integrated autocorrelation time is \(\tau_{\text{int}} = 250\), estimate the total effective sample size. Is it acceptable or should the proposal be improved?
Solution. The autocorrelation function of \(A\) is \[C_A(\tau) = E[A(x_t)\,A(x_{t+\tau})] - E[A]^2,\] the normalised version is \(\rho_A(\tau) = C_A(\tau)/C_A(0)\), and \[\tau_{\text{int}} = 1 + 2\sum_{\tau=1}^\infty \rho_A(\tau).\] With \(M = 5\) chains of length \(N = 10^4\), the total number of stored samples is \(N_{\text{tot}} = M N = 5\cdot 10^4\). Inflating the variance by \(\tau_{\text{int}}\), \[N_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{N_{\text{tot}}}{\tau_{\text{int}}} = \frac{5\cdot 10^4}{250} = 200.\] Five correlated chains carry the same information as roughly \(2\cdot 10^2\) independent draws. The ratio \(N/\tau_{\text{int}} = 40\) per chain shows that most moves are wasteful — the proposal is poorly matched to the target geometry, and either a better proposal (HMC, adapted scale) or a reparametrisation is needed.



Show code
# Target: mixture of two Gaussians
target_density <- function(x) {
  0.5 * dnorm(x, mean = -3, sd = 1) + 0.5 * dnorm(x, mean = 3, sd = 1)
}

log_target <- function(x) log(target_density(x))

# MH sampler
mh_sample <- function(n_steps, x0, proposal_sd) {
  x <- numeric(n_steps)
  x[1] <- x0
  n_accept <- 0
  for (t in 2:n_steps) {
    x_current <- x[t - 1]
    x_prop <- rnorm(1, mean = x_current, sd = proposal_sd)
    log_r <- log_target(x_prop) - log_target(x_current)
    if (log(runif(1)) < log_r) {
      x[t] <- x_prop
      n_accept <- n_accept + 1
    } else {
      x[t] <- x_current
    }
  }
  list(samples = x, accept_rate = n_accept / (n_steps - 1))
}

# Run sampler
n_steps <- 50000
burn_in <- 5000
proposal_sd <- 2.0
mh <- mh_sample(n_steps, x0 = -8, proposal_sd = proposal_sd)
samples <- mh$samples
samples_burn <- samples[(burn_in + 1):n_steps]

cat("Acceptance rate:", mh$accept_rate, "\n")


Acceptance rate: 0.5304306 


Show code
# Compute autocorrelation
lag_max <- 500
acf_vals <- acf(samples_burn, lag.max = lag_max, plot = FALSE)
lags <- as.numeric(acf_vals$lag)
rho <- as.numeric(acf_vals$acf)

# Integrated autocorrelation time
t_int <- 1 + 2 * sum(rho[2:min(which(abs(rho[-1]) < 0.05)[1], length(rho))])
N_eff <- length(samples_burn) / (2 * t_int)

cat("Integrated autocorr time:", round(t_int, 2), "\n")


Integrated autocorr time: 38.65 


Show code
cat("N_eff:", round(N_eff, 0), "\n")


N_eff: 582 


Show code
# Plots
par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Trace before and after burn-in
plot(1:burn_in, samples[1:burn_in], type = "l", xlab = "Iteration",
     ylab = "x", main = "Before burn-in", lwd = 0.5, col = rgb(1, 0, 0, 0.6))

plot((burn_in + 1):n_steps, samples_burn, type = "l",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "x", main = "After burn-in",
     lwd = 0.5, col = rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.6))

# Histogram + target
hist(samples_burn, breaks = 80, probability = TRUE,
     main = "Posterior samples", xlab = "x",
     col = rgb(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.4), border = NA)
curve(target_density(x), from = -8, to = 8, lwd = 2, col = "red", add = TRUE)





Trace plot of chain (before and after burn-in), histogram, and autocorrelation. Low acceptance rate (ca. 47%) due to small proposal variance.




Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.10 Summary and roadmap
This chapter has covered the foundational sampling methods:

Direct sampling (inverse transform) is exact when the CDF is invertible, but unavailable for complex posteriors.
Rejection sampling corrects for sampling from the wrong density but wastes samples in high dimensions.
Importance sampling avoids rejection but suffers from weight degeneracy when the proposal is misspecified.
Markov chain Monte Carlo constructs a dynamics whose stationary distribution is the target, enabling asymptotically exact sampling even in high dimensions.

The next chapters develop practical refinements and extensions:

Chapter 8 explores advanced MCMC algorithms: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (which uses gradient information to reduce autocorrelation), adaptive proposal tuning, and parallel tempering for multimodal distributions.
Chapter 9 covers variational inference—a fast, approximate alternative that trades accuracy for speed via optimization.
Chapter 10 introduces particle methods: sequential Monte Carlo and resampling strategies for filtering and online inference.

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.11 Exercises

(Written test, 15 June 2026 — Q5.) A target density is \(f(x) = 3x^2\) with \(0 < x \leq 1\). A proposal density is \(g(x) = 1\) with \(0 < x \leq 1\). Find the smallest constant \(M\) so that \(g\) can be used in a rejection sampling algorithm to sample \(f\). Give the expected acceptance probability.


Solution. We need \(M \geq \sup_{x \in (0,1]} f(x)/g(x) = \sup 3x^2 = 3\), so the smallest admissible constant is \[M = 3.\] The expected acceptance probability is \[P(\text{accept}) = \frac{1}{M} = \frac{1}{3}.\]


Inverse transform. Show that if \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\) and \(\lambda > 0\), then \(X = -\log(U)/\lambda\) has the \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) distribution.


Solution. For \(x \geq 0\), using that \(U\) and \(1-U\) are both uniform on \((0,1)\), \[P(X \leq x) = P(-\log U / \lambda \leq x) = P(U \geq e^{-\lambda x}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}.\] Differentiating yields the density \(f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) for \(x \geq 0\), i.e. \(X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\).


Inverse transform for the Pareto distribution with CDF \(F(x) = 1 - (x_m / x)^\alpha\) for \(x \geq x_m\). Derive the sampling formula in terms of \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\).


Solution. Setting \(U = F(X) = 1 - (x_m/X)^\alpha\) and solving for \(X\), \[(x_m / X)^\alpha = 1 - U \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X = \frac{x_m}{(1 - U)^{1/\alpha}}.\] Since \(1 - U \stackrel{d}{=} U\), one may equivalently use \(X = x_m\,U^{-1/\alpha}\).


Inverse transform for the geometric distribution on \(\{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}\) with parameter \(p\). Derive the smallest integer \(X\) such that \(1 - (1-p)^X \geq U\), with \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\).


Solution. The CDF is \(F(k) = 1 - (1-p)^k\) for \(k \geq 1\). Requiring \(F(X) \geq U\) gives \((1-p)^X \leq 1 - U\), i.e. \[X \geq \frac{\log(1 - U)}{\log(1 - p)}.\] The smallest such integer is \[X = \left\lceil \frac{\log(1 - U)}{\log(1 - p)} \right\rceil,\] which (using \(1-U \stackrel{d}{=} U\)) is the standard geometric sampler.


Rejection from \(\text{Gamma}(2, 1)\) with density \(f(x) = x\,e^{-x}\) for \(x > 0\), using the proposal \(g(x) = e^{-x}\) (i.e. \(\text{Exp}(1)\)). Find the smallest constant \(M\) such that \(f(x) \leq M\,g(x)\). Give the expected acceptance probability.


Solution. The ratio \(f(x)/g(x) = x\) is unbounded on \((0, \infty)\), so no finite \(M\) exists and rejection from \(\text{Exp}(1)\) fails. Choosing instead a lighter exponential \(g_\lambda(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) with \(\lambda < 1\) gives a bounded ratio \[\frac{f(x)}{g_\lambda(x)} = \frac{x\,e^{-(1-\lambda)x}}{\lambda},\] maximised at \(x^\star = 1/(1-\lambda)\), yielding \(M(\lambda) = 1/[\lambda e(1-\lambda)]\), minimised at \(\lambda = 1/2\) with \(M = 4/e\) and acceptance probability \[P(\text{accept}) = \frac{1}{M} = \frac{e}{4}.\]


Rejection sampling from the half-normal \(f(x) \propto e^{-x^2/2}\) on \(x \geq 0\) using the exponential proposal \(g(x) = e^{-x}\). Find the smallest \(M\) and the acceptance probability.


Solution. Using the normalised half-normal \(f(x) = \sqrt{2/\pi}\,e^{-x^2/2}\), the ratio is \[\frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\,e^{x - x^2/2},\] which is maximised at \(x^\star = 1\), giving \[M = \sqrt{\frac{2e}{\pi}}, \qquad P(\text{accept}) = \frac{1}{M} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2e}} \approx 0.76.\]


Show that in rejection sampling the overall acceptance probability is exactly \(1/M\), where \(M = \sup_x f(x)/g(x)\). State the implication for the expected number of proposals needed to obtain one sample.


Solution. Marginalising over the proposal, \[P(\text{accept}) = \int g(x)\,\frac{f(x)}{M\,g(x)}\,dx = \frac{1}{M}\int f(x)\,dx = \frac{1}{M}.\] Hence the number of proposals until the first acceptance is geometric with mean \[E[N_{\text{prop}}] = M,\] so large \(M\) (poor proposal) makes rejection sampling prohibitively expensive.


Importance sampling. Estimate \(\mathbb{E}_p[X^2]\) for \(p = \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) using the proposal \(q = \mathcal{N}(0, 4)\). Write the importance weights \(w(x) = p(x)/q(x)\) explicitly and the unbiased estimator based on \(N\) samples.


Solution. The weight ratio of two zero-mean Gaussians is \[w(x) = \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} = \frac{(2\pi)^{-1/2}e^{-x^2/2}}{(2\pi\cdot 4)^{-1/2}e^{-x^2/8}} = 2\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{3x^2}{8}\right).\] With \(x_i \sim q\) i.i.d., the unbiased importance-sampling estimator is \[\widehat{I} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N x_i^2\,w(x_i),\] which converges to \(E_p[X^2] = 1\).


Importance sampling for a tail probability. Estimate \(\mathbb{P}_p(X > 3)\) for \(p = \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) using the shifted proposal \(q = \mathcal{N}(3, 1)\). Write the estimator and explain why the proposal beats naïve Monte Carlo.


Solution. With \(f(x) = \mathbb{1}_{x > 3}\) and weights \[w(x) = \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} = \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}x^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(x-3)^2\right) = e^{-3x + 9/2},\] the estimator with \(x_i \sim \mathcal N(3, 1)\) is \[\widehat{P} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{1}_{x_i > 3}\,e^{-3 x_i + 9/2}.\] Naïve sampling from \(p\) produces a hit only with probability \(\Phi^c(3) \approx 1.35\cdot 10^{-3}\), so its relative error blows up; the shifted proposal places roughly half the samples in the rare region, making the variance of order \(1/N\) instead of \(1/(N\,\Phi^c(3))\).


Effective sample size. For three normalised importance weights \((w_1, w_2, w_3) = (0.7,\, 0.2,\, 0.1)\) compute \(\text{ESS} = 1 / \sum_i w_i^2\).


Solution. Computing the sum of squares, \[\sum_i w_i^2 = 0.49 + 0.04 + 0.01 = 0.54,\] so \[\text{ESS} = \frac{1}{0.54} = \frac{50}{27} \approx 1.85.\] Three samples carry less than two effective ones — the first weight dominates and signals severe degeneracy.


Antithetic variates. Let \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\) and consider the estimator \(\tfrac{1}{2}[g(U) + g(1 - U)]\). Show that when \(g\) is monotone, \(\text{Cov}(g(U), g(1-U)) \leq 0\) and the estimator has variance no larger than the single-sample estimator.


Solution. If \(g\) is monotone, \(g(U)\) and \(g(1-U)\) are monotone in \(U\) in opposite senses, so by the Chebyshev/FKG correlation inequality \[\text{Cov}(g(U),\,g(1-U)) \leq 0.\] With \(\sigma^2 = \text{Var}\,g(U) = \text{Var}\,g(1-U)\), \[\text{Var}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}[g(U) + g(1-U)]\right) = \tfrac{1}{4}\!\left[2\sigma^2 + 2\,\text{Cov}\right] = \tfrac{\sigma^2}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Cov} \leq \tfrac{\sigma^2}{2},\] which is at most the variance \(\sigma^2/2\) of the average of two independent draws of \(g(U)\), and strictly smaller when the covariance is negative.


Control variates. Estimate \(\theta = \mathbb{E}[h(X)]\) with \(X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\) and \(h(x) = e^x\), using \(X\) itself as a control variate (its mean is \(1/2\)). Derive the optimal coefficient \(c^\star = -\text{Cov}(h(X), X) / \text{Var}(X)\) and write the control-variate estimator.


Solution. For \(X \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\), \(\text{Var}(X) = 1/12\), and integrating by parts \[E[X e^X] = e - (e - 1) = 1, \qquad \text{Cov}(e^X, X) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2}(e - 1) = \tfrac{3 - e}{2}.\] Hence \[c^\star = -\frac{\text{Cov}(e^X, X)}{\text{Var}(X)} = -12\cdot\frac{3 - e}{2} = 6(e - 3),\] and the control-variate estimator is \[\widehat{\theta}_{\text{cv}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \left[e^{X_i} + c^\star\!\left(X_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right].\]


Two-state Markov chain. The transition matrix is \[T = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}.\] Find the stationary distribution \(\pi\).


Solution. With \(a = T_{12} = 0.3\) and \(b = T_{21} = 0.4\), balancing the off-diagonal flows \(\pi_1 a = \pi_2 b\) together with \(\pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1\) gives \[\pi_1 = \frac{b}{a + b} = \frac{0.4}{0.7} = \frac{4}{7}, \qquad \pi_2 = \frac{a}{a + b} = \frac{0.3}{0.7} = \frac{3}{7}.\]


Detailed balance check. With \(T\) as above, verify whether the candidate stationary distribution \(\pi = (4/7,\, 3/7)\) satisfies \(\pi_i T_{ij} = \pi_j T_{ji}\).


Solution. Comparing the two flows, \[\pi_1 T_{12} = \tfrac{4}{7}\cdot 0.3 = \tfrac{1.2}{7}, \qquad \pi_2 T_{21} = \tfrac{3}{7}\cdot 0.4 = \tfrac{1.2}{7}.\] The two are equal, so detailed balance holds and the chain is reversible with stationary law \(\pi = (4/7, 3/7)\).


Metropolis acceptance. For target \(\pi(x) \propto e^{-x^2/2}\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) with a symmetric proposal \(q(x' \mid x) = q(x \mid x')\), write the acceptance probability \(\alpha(x \to x')\) in closed form and identify when it equals \(1\).


Solution. Symmetry of \(q\) cancels the proposal ratio, leaving \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\!\left(1,\,\frac{\pi(x')}{\pi(x)}\right) = \min\!\left(1,\,\exp\!\left[\tfrac{1}{2}(x^2 - x'^2)\right]\right).\] Acceptance equals \(1\) whenever \(x'^2 \leq x^2\), i.e. the proposal moves closer to the mode at the origin; otherwise it accepts with probability \(\exp[(x^2 - x'^2)/2] < 1\).


(Define and explain.) For a stationary Markov chain \(\{x_t\}_{t=1}^N\) and observable \(A(x)\), define the autocorrelation function \(C_A(\tau)\), the normalised autocorrelation \(\rho_A(\tau)\), and the integrated autocorrelation time \(\tau_{\text{int}}\). State the formula for the effective sample size \(N_{\text{eff}}\).


Solution. The autocorrelation, its normalised version, and the integrated time are \[C_A(\tau) = E[A(x_t)A(x_{t+\tau})] - E[A]^2, \quad \rho_A(\tau) = \frac{C_A(\tau)}{C_A(0)}, \quad \tau_{\text{int}} = 1 + 2\sum_{\tau=1}^\infty \rho_A(\tau).\] The effective sample size after \(N\) correlated draws is \[N_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{N}{\tau_{\text{int}}},\] i.e. the chain carries \(N_{\text{eff}}\) independent draws’ worth of information about \(E_\pi[A]\).


(Define and explain.) Explain in one paragraph why Monte Carlo methods are central in Bayesian inference. Contrast with grid-based numerical integration in \(d\) dimensions. What is the difference between using independent posterior samples and samples from a Markov chain?


Solution. Bayesian inference reduces to posterior expectations \(E[f(\theta) \mid D] = \int f(\theta)\pi(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta\), which Monte Carlo replaces by \(\widehat{I}_N = N^{-1}\sum_i f(\theta_i)\). The Monte Carlo error scales as \(\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})\) regardless of dimension, whereas a grid with \(n\) points per axis costs \(n^d\) evaluations — exponentially worse. Independent samples give \(\text{Var}(\widehat{I}_N) = \text{Var}_\pi(f)/N\), while an MCMC chain inflates this by the integrated autocorrelation time, \[\text{Var}(\widehat{I}_N) \approx \frac{\tau_{\text{int}}}{N}\,\text{Var}_\pi(f),\] so the relevant figure of merit is the effective sample size \(N_{\text{eff}} = N/\tau_{\text{int}}\), not the raw number of stored draws.


(Compare and contrast.) Define rejection sampling and importance sampling. State one situation where each one is preferable over the other. What is the cost of a bad proposal in each method?


Solution. Rejection sampling draws \(x \sim q\) and accepts with probability \(\tilde\pi(x)/[M\,q(x)]\), producing exact i.i.d. samples from \(\pi\); importance sampling keeps every draw \(x_i \sim q\) and weights it by \(w(x_i) = \pi(x_i)/q(x_i)\) in \(\widehat I = N^{-1}\sum_i f(x_i) w(x_i)\). Rejection is preferable when downstream code requires unweighted samples (e.g. visualisation, plugging into a black-box estimator); importance sampling is preferable for rare events or tail probabilities where \(q\) can be shifted into the region of interest. The cost of a bad proposal is: in rejection, a vanishing acceptance rate \(1/M\) and roughly \(M\) proposals per sample; in importance sampling, weight degeneracy quantified by \(\text{ESS} = 1/\sum_i w_i^2 \ll N\) and unbounded estimator variance.


(Define and explain.) State the conditions for a Markov chain to converge to its stationary distribution. Define detailed balance and explain why it is sufficient (but not necessary) for stationarity.


Solution. A Markov chain with transition \(T(x, y)\) converges to a unique stationary law \(\pi\) provided it is irreducible (any state reachable from any other in finite time), aperiodic (no cyclic class), and admits \(\pi\) as an invariant measure satisfying \[\pi(y) = \int T(x, y)\,\pi(x)\,dx.\] Detailed balance is the local-equilibrium condition \[\pi(x)\,T(x, y) = \pi(y)\,T(y, x).\] Integrating both sides over \(x\) gives the global balance equation above, so detailed balance implies stationarity. It is not necessary: chains can be stationary with non-zero probability currents that close up globally without balancing pairwise (e.g. deterministic cyclic chains).

title: 8  Monte Carlo methods
text:
8.12 Sources
Robert, C. P., & Casella, G. (2004). Monte Carlo Statistical Methods (2nd ed.). Springer. Chapters 2–3 (fundamentals and inverse sampling) and Chapter 6 (importance sampling and rejection sampling).
Sivia, D. S., & Skilling, J. (2006). Data Analysis: A Bayesian Tutorial (2nd ed.). Oxford University Press. Chapter 9 on computational methods.
Falasco, G. Lecture notes on advanced statistics for physics analysis, April 2026. Sections 6–7.

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
text:
9.1 Introduction
The Metropolis–Hastings algorithm, introduced in the previous chapter, is a general framework for constructing Markov chains with a target stationary distribution. However, the choice of proposal distribution critically affects both computational efficiency and the quality of samples. In this chapter, we explore two important special cases that reveal a fundamental trade-off in MCMC design: global vs. local exploration, and the role of acceptance rates in controlling mixing.
The independence sampler uses a fixed global proposal, independent of the current state. This allows long-distance jumps but requires the proposal to approximate the target well. The Gibbs sampler, by contrast, updates coordinates sequentially using exact full conditional distributions, guaranteeing acceptance but potentially moving slowly through high-dimensional spaces when parameters are correlated. Both methods illustrate how MCMC designers must balance the competing demands of efficient exploration and computational tractability.
crumbs: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
text:
9.2 The independence sampler: global proposals
In a random-walk Metropolis algorithm, the proposal distribution depends on the current state: \[
q(x' \mid x) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{\|x' - x\|^2}{2\sigma^2} \right).
\]
This locality allows the chain to take small, tentative steps and gradually explore the target space. However, if the proposal variance is too small, the chain mixes slowly; if too large, it wastes computation on distant, low-probability regions.
The independence sampler takes a different approach: the proposal is independent of the current state, \[
q(x') = q(x' \mid x),
\] depending only on \(x'\). This allows the chain to jump from anywhere to anywhere in a single step, potentially escaping local modes and exploring the target space more globally.

Acceptance probability. For a generic Metropolis–Hastings step the acceptance probability is \[
\alpha(x \to x') = \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(x') \, q(x \mid x')}{\pi(x) \, q(x' \mid x)}\right).
\] For the independence sampler, \(q(x \mid x') = q(x)\) and \(q(x' \mid x) = q(x')\), so \[
\alpha(x \to x') = \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(x') \, q(x)}{\pi(x) \, q(x')}\right).
\] Rearranging in terms of importance weights \(w(x) = \pi(x)/q(x)\): \[
\alpha(x \to x') = \min\left(1, \frac{w(x')}{w(x)}\right).
\]


The acceptance probability compares the importance weights at the proposed and current states. If \(w(x')\) is larger, the proposal is always accepted. If \(w(x')\) is smaller, it is accepted with probability \(w(x') / w(x)\). When the proposal closely matches the target, weights are nearly constant and acceptance is high.


Connection to importance sampling and ESS. The independence sampler inherits its theoretical motivation from importance sampling. In importance sampling, we approximate an expectation \[
E_\pi[f(x)] = \int f(x) \, \pi(x) \, dx
\] by sampling from a simpler proposal \(q\) and weighting: \[
\widehat{E_\pi}[f(x)] = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^N w(x_i) f(x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^N w(x_i)}, \quad w(x_i) = \frac{\pi(x_i)}{q(x_i)}.
\] The effective sample size (ESS) reflects how much information the weighted samples contain. If weights are unequal—as they are when \(q\) deviates from \(\pi\)—the ESS is smaller than \(N\): \[
\text{ESS} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_i / \bar{w})^2}.
\] When \(w(x)\) varies widely, many samples receive vanishingly small weight, and the variance of the estimator becomes very large. The independence sampler’s acceptance rate is directly related to the relative variation in weights: a narrow or poorly-matched proposal produces highly variable weights, leading to low acceptance and slow exploration. This is the narrow-proposal failure mode — if the proposal is too concentrated, the chain accepts rarely and gets stuck.
When it works well. The key requirement is that \(q\) must approximate \(\pi\) globally. A sufficient ergodicity condition is \[
\pi(x) \leq C \, q(x) \quad \forall x
\] for some finite constant \(C > 0\) (analogous to the envelope condition in rejection sampling), which ensures the chain does not get trapped in regions of arbitrarily high relative weight. The independence sampler is best when we have a good global approximation to the posterior — e.g., from a Laplace approximation, variational inference, or other fast approximate methods — and when the target is unimodal or has well-separated modes the proposal can accommodate. It fails when the proposal underestimates the tails or misses modes, so that states with large \(\pi(x)/q(x)\) are rare and hard to reach.
Curse of dimensionality. A profound limitation emerges in high dimensions. Suppose \(\log w(x) = \log \pi(x) - \log q(x)\) decomposes as a sum of roughly independent contributions: \[
\log w(x) \approx \sum_{i=1}^d \Delta_i(x_i),
\] where each \(\Delta_i\) represents the local mismatch between \(\pi\) and \(q\) in coordinate \(i\). Even if each \(\Delta_i\) is small (order \(O(1)\)), the total deviation is \(\log w(x) = O(d)\), so \(w(x) = \exp(O(d))\). The weights vary exponentially in dimension, and the acceptance rate typically decays like \(P(A) \sim \exp(-cd)\).


Concentration of measure: In high dimensions, probability mass concentrates on thin regions. For a standard Gaussian \(\pi = N(0, I_d)\), the norm \(\|x\|\) concentrates around \(\sqrt{d}\). Even a slightly mis-scaled proposal \(q\) will place its probability mass in regions irrelevant to \(\pi\), causing acceptance to decay exponentially with \(d\).

This is the motivation for local proposals (random-walk Metropolis), gradient-based methods (Hamiltonian MC), and coordinate-wise samplers (Gibbs sampling), which we turn to next.
crumbs: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
text:
9.3 The Gibbs sampler: coordinate-wise updates
In many Bayesian models, jointly sampling from \(p(\theta \mid D)\) where \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_d)\) is intractable, but sampling from each full conditional distribution is simple.

The full conditional distribution of \(\theta_i\) is \[
p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D),
\] where \(\theta_{-i} = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i-1}, \theta_{i+1}, \ldots, \theta_d)\) denotes all other parameters. It is obtained by treating \(\theta_{-i}\) as fixed and proportional to the joint posterior: \[
p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \propto p(\theta_i, \theta_{-i} \mid D).
\]


9.3.1 Algorithm: deterministic and random scan
Gibbs sampling iteratively samples each parameter from its full conditional.

Gibbs Sampler Algorithm
Initialize \(\theta^{(0)} = (\theta_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \theta_d^{(0)})\).
For \(t = 1, 2, \ldots\):

\(\theta_1^{(t)} \sim p(\theta_1 \mid \theta_2^{(t-1)}, \ldots, \theta_d^{(t-1)}, D)\)
\(\theta_2^{(t)} \sim p(\theta_2 \mid \theta_1^{(t)}, \theta_3^{(t-1)}, \ldots, \theta_d^{(t-1)}, D)\)
\(\vdots\)
\(\theta_d^{(t)} \sim p(\theta_d \mid \theta_1^{(t)}, \ldots, \theta_{d-1}^{(t)}, D)\)

One complete cycle of all \(d\) updates constitutes one iteration of the Gibbs sampler.
Variants: The random-scan Gibbs sampler updates coordinates in random order instead of a fixed sequence. For convergence, both deterministic and random scans are ergodic under mild conditions.


Gibbs as a special case of Metropolis–Hastings. Gibbs sampling can be viewed as Metropolis–Hastings with the proposal being the full conditional itself. At step \(t\), we propose a new value for \(\theta_i\) by sampling from \[
q(\theta_i' \mid \theta_i^{(t-1)}, \theta_{-i}^{(t)}) = p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t)}, D).
\] The acceptance probability is \[
\alpha(\theta^{(t-1)} \to \theta^{(t)}) = \min\left(1, \frac{p(\theta_i^{(t)}, \theta_{-i}^{(t)} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i^{(t-1)} \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t)}, D)}{p(\theta_i^{(t-1)}, \theta_{-i}^{(t-1)} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i^{(t)} \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t)}, D)}\right).
\] Using the factorization \(p(\theta_i, \theta_{-i} \mid D) = p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, p(\theta_{-i} \mid D)\), the ratio simplifies: \[
\frac{p(\theta_i^{(t)} \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t)}, D) \, p(\theta_{-i}^{(t)} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i^{(t-1)} \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t)}, D)}{p(\theta_i^{(t-1)} \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t-1)}, D) \, p(\theta_{-i}^{(t-1)} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i^{(t)} \mid \theta_{-i}^{(t)}, D)} = 1.
\] Therefore, \(\alpha = 1\): every Gibbs step is accepted.


Acceptance is guaranteed, but samples are still correlated. The chain does not jump to a new state at each step; instead, it updates coordinates sequentially, and autocorrelation arises because coordinates are strongly dependent.



9.3.2 Detailed balance for Gibbs updates
For the full Gibbs update (one complete sweep of all \(d\) coordinates), detailed balance holds with respect to the joint posterior. We verify that \[
p(\theta \mid D) \, T(\theta \to \theta') = p(\theta' \mid D) \, T(\theta' \to \theta),
\] where \(T\) is the transition kernel of one complete sweep.
For a single coordinate update \(T_i\) applied to coordinate \(i\), left-multiply by \(p(\theta \mid D)\) and expand using the factorization: \[\begin{align}
p(\theta \mid D) \, T_i(\theta \to \theta') &= p(\theta_i, \theta_{-i} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, \delta(\theta_{-i}' - \theta_{-i})\\
&= p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, p(\theta_{-i} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, \delta(\theta_{-i}' - \theta_{-i}).
\end{align}\]
Permuting terms and using \(p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D) = p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}', D)\) (since \(\theta_{-i} = \theta_{-i}'\)): \[
= p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, p(\theta_{-i} \mid D) \, p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, \delta(\theta_{-i}' - \theta_{-i}) = p(\theta' \mid D) \, T_i(\theta' \to \theta).
\]
Thus each coordinate-wise update preserves the posterior. Since the full sweep is a composition of such updates, it also satisfies detailed balance.

Mixing and slow mixing from correlation. Unlike the independence sampler, Gibbs always accepts. However, this does not imply fast mixing. When parameters are strongly correlated — as in high-dimensional hierarchical models or when the posterior is elongated along a narrow ridge — coordinate-wise updates must take many small steps to explore the space.


Bivariate Gaussian with high correlation
Consider a bivariate normal distribution: \[
\begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \end{pmatrix} \sim N\left( \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix} \right).
\]
The full conditionals are: \[
X \mid Y = y \sim N(\rho y, 1 - \rho^2), \quad Y \mid X = x \sim N(\rho x, 1 - \rho^2).
\]
When \(\rho = 0.9\), the posterior concentrates on a narrow diagonal ridge. A Gibbs step first moves horizontally (X-update), then vertically (Y-update), taking many iterations to traverse the ridge. In contrast, updating both coordinates jointly (e.g., via a rotated-coordinate Gibbs or HMC) moves along the ridge in fewer steps.


Metropolis-within-Gibbs and block Gibbs. When the full conditional for a component is intractable, we can replace the Gibbs step with a Metropolis–Hastings step; when several coordinates are strongly correlated, we can group them and update them jointly.


Metropolis-within-Gibbs: Replace the Gibbs step for \(\theta_i\) with a Metropolis–Hastings move using a local proposal \(q_i(\theta_i' \mid \theta_i)\). The acceptance probability is \[
\alpha_i(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}) = \min\left(1, \frac{p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, q_i(\theta_i \mid \theta_i')}{p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D) \, q_i(\theta_i' \mid \theta_i)}\right).
\]
The chain remains valid (it still has \(p(\theta \mid D)\) as stationary distribution), but some steps are rejected.

Block Gibbs sampling updates multiple coordinates jointly, \[
(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_k) \sim p(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_k \mid \theta_{k+1}, \ldots, \theta_d, D),
\] which is effective when joint conditionals are tractable and dramatically reduces autocorrelation in highly correlated subsets.
crumbs: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
section: 9.4 Worked example: Gibbs sampling for a bivariate Gaussian
text:
9.4 Worked example: Gibbs sampling for a bivariate Gaussian
We now demonstrate Gibbs sampling on the bivariate Gaussian with \(\rho = 0.9\) to illustrate slow mixing due to correlation, and show how a simple coordinate rotation accelerates mixing.

9.4.1 Slow mixing at high correlation


Show code
set.seed(42)

gibbs_bivariate <- function(n_steps, rho, start = c(0, 0)) {
  x <- numeric(n_steps)
  y <- numeric(n_steps)
  x[1] <- start[1]
  y[1] <- start[2]
  cond_sd <- sqrt(1 - rho^2)

  for (t in 2:n_steps) {
    # Update X | Y
    x[t] <- rnorm(1, mean = rho * y[t - 1], sd = cond_sd)
    # Update Y | X
    y[t] <- rnorm(1, mean = rho * x[t], sd = cond_sd)
  }

  cbind(x = x, y = y)
}

rho_high <- 0.9
chain_high <- gibbs_bivariate(n_steps = 10000, rho = rho_high, start = c(-3, 3))

# Diagnostics
burn_in <- 1000
samples_high <- chain_high[(burn_in + 1):nrow(chain_high), ]

par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# Trace plots
plot(chain_high[, "x"], type = "l", lwd = 0.8, col = "#d62728",
     main = paste0("Gibbs trace (X), rho = ", rho_high),
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "X")
abline(v = burn_in, col = "grey", lty = 2)

plot(chain_high[, "y"], type = "l", lwd = 0.8, col = "#1f77b4",
     main = "Gibbs trace (Y)", xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "Y")
abline(v = burn_in, col = "grey", lty = 2)

# Scatter of posterior samples
plot(samples_high[, "x"], samples_high[, "y"], pch = 16, cex = 0.3,
     col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.5), xlab = "X", ylab = "Y",
     main = "Posterior samples after burn-in")





Gibbs sampling trace plots for a bivariate Gaussian with ρ = 0.9. The X-trace (top) shows high autocorrelation; the Y-trace (middle) is even slower due to dependence on X. The scatter plot (bottom) reveals the narrow diagonal structure.






9.4.2 Rotated coordinates for faster mixing
The slow mixing arises because the Gibbs updates align with the coordinate axes, while the posterior concentrates along the diagonal. We can decouple the correlation by rotating coordinates. Let \(u = \frac{x + y}{\sqrt{2}}\) and \(v = \frac{x - y}{\sqrt{2}}\). For a standard bivariate Gaussian, the joint distribution of \((u, v)\) is: \[
u \sim N(0, (1 + \rho)), \quad v \sim N(0, (1 - \rho)),
\] and \(u\) and \(v\) are independent.


Show code
gibbs_rotated <- function(n_steps, rho, start = c(0, 0)) {
  # Transform starting point to rotated coordinates
  u_start <- (start[1] + start[2]) / sqrt(2)
  v_start <- (start[1] - start[2]) / sqrt(2)

  u <- numeric(n_steps)
  v <- numeric(n_steps)
  u[1] <- u_start
  v[1] <- v_start

  sd_u <- sqrt(1 + rho)
  sd_v <- sqrt(1 - rho)

  for (t in 2:n_steps) {
    # u and v are now independent, so Gibbs reduces to independent sampling
    u[t] <- rnorm(1, mean = 0, sd = sd_u)
    v[t] <- rnorm(1, mean = 0, sd = sd_v)
  }

  # Transform back to original coordinates
  x <- (u + v) / sqrt(2)
  y <- (u - v) / sqrt(2)

  cbind(x = x, y = y)
}

chain_rotated <- gibbs_rotated(n_steps = 10000, rho = rho_high, start = c(-3, 3))
samples_rotated <- chain_rotated[(burn_in + 1):nrow(chain_rotated), ]

par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# Trace plots for rotated coordinates
plot(chain_rotated[, "x"], type = "l", lwd = 0.8, col = "#2ca02c",
     main = "Rotated Gibbs trace (X after rotation)",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "X")
abline(v = burn_in, col = "grey", lty = 2)

plot(chain_rotated[, "y"], type = "l", lwd = 0.8, col = "#ff7f0e",
     main = "Rotated Gibbs trace (Y after rotation)",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "Y")
abline(v = burn_in, col = "grey", lty = 2)

# Scatter plot
plot(samples_rotated[, "x"], samples_rotated[, "y"], pch = 16, cex = 0.3,
     col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.5), xlab = "X", ylab = "Y",
     main = "Posterior samples (rotated Gibbs)")





Gibbs sampling in rotated coordinates. With ρ = 0.9, rotated coordinates decouple the posterior into independent components, yielding much lower autocorrelation and faster mixing.




Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))




9.4.3 Autocorrelation comparison


Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

acf_high <- acf(samples_high[, "x"], lag.max = 50, plot = FALSE)
acf_rotated <- acf(samples_rotated[, "x"], lag.max = 50, plot = FALSE)

plot(acf_high$lag, acf_high$acf, type = "h", lwd = 2, col = "#d62728",
     main = "Standard Gibbs (rho = 0.9)",
     xlab = "Lag", ylab = "ACF", ylim = c(-0.1, 1))
abline(h = 0, col = "black", lwd = 0.5)

plot(acf_rotated$lag, acf_rotated$acf, type = "h", lwd = 2, col = "#2ca02c",
     main = "Rotated Gibbs (rho = 0.9)",
     xlab = "Lag", ylab = "ACF", ylim = c(-0.1, 1))
abline(h = 0, col = "black", lwd = 0.5)





Autocorrelation functions for X in both samplers. Standard Gibbs (dashed red) shows high persistence; rotated Gibbs (solid green) decorrelates in one or two lags.




Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
section: 9.5 Comparison: independence sampler vs. Gibbs sampler vs. random-walk Metropolis
text:
9.5 Comparison: independence sampler vs. Gibbs sampler vs. random-walk Metropolis
The three samplers embody different philosophies and trade-offs:









Aspect
Independence Sampler
Gibbs Sampler
Random-Walk MH




Proposal
\(q(x')\) (global, fixed)
\(p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D)\)
\(x' \sim N(x, \Sigma)\)


Move type
Global jumps anywhere
Coordinate-wise updates
Local steps


Acceptance
MH ratio (often low)
Always 1 (deterministic)
Variable (tunable)


Main requirement
Good global approximation
Tractable conditionals
Reasonable scale estimate


Fails when
\(q\) poorly matches \(\pi\)
Strong parameter correlations
Proposal scale wrong


Autocorrelation
Low if \(q \approx \pi\)
High if correlated structure
Moderate (tunable)


Dimension scaling
Exponential decay \(\sim \exp(-cd)\)
Polynomial (depends on correlation)
Exponential decay


Typical use
Approximate posteriors
Conjugate/hierarchical models
Flexible fallback




Remark. In practice, these are rarely used in isolation. Modern samplers often combine ideas:

Metropolis-within-Gibbs: Use Gibbs for tractable coordinates, MH for intractable ones.
Block Gibbs: Group correlated coordinates and sample jointly to reduce autocorrelation.
Adaptive proposals: Learn a proposal from preliminary runs (adaptive MCMC).
HMC within Gibbs: Combine gradient-based efficient exploration with block structure.

The choice depends on the model structure, the availability of efficient samplers for conditionals, and computational constraints.
crumbs: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
text:
9.6 Summary
The independence sampler represents a global exploration strategy: it can jump anywhere but only works well if the proposal closely approximates the target. Its acceptance probability directly reflects the importance weights, making it a bridge between importance sampling and MCMC. However, the exponential degradation of weights in high dimensions—a manifestation of concentration of measure—limits its applicability to high-dimensional problems.
The Gibbs sampler exploits model structure by updating parameters from their full conditionals. Every step is accepted, eliminating the rejection waste of Metropolis algorithms. However, when parameters are strongly correlated, the chain mixes slowly because coordinate-wise moves struggle to explore elongated posterior geometries efficiently. Rotation to decorrelated coordinates, block updates, or auxiliary variable methods can dramatically improve performance.
Both methods highlight the central tension in MCMC: acceptance rates, exploration speed, and computational tractability are not independent. A low acceptance rate signals a poorly matched proposal; a high rate (even 100%) does not guarantee fast mixing if the effective step size is small. Successful MCMC practice requires diagnosing mixing, adapting proposals, and sometimes redesigning the parameterization to align with the posterior structure.
crumbs: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
text:
9.7 Exercises

(Written test, 15 June 2026 — Q6.) A discrete-time Markov chain has state space \(\{0, 1, 2\}\) and transition matrix \[T = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - a - b & a & b \\ b & 1 - a - b & a \\ a & b & 1 - a - b \end{pmatrix},\] where \(a > 0\), \(b > 0\), and \(a + b \leq 1\).

Find the stationary distribution \(\pi = (\pi_0, \pi_1, \pi_2)\).
State the detailed-balance equations for this chain.
Derive the condition on \(a\) and \(b\) under which detailed balance can hold.



Solution. 

The chain is doubly stochastic (every column also sums to \(1\)), so the uniform vector is invariant: \[\pi = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}\right).\]
Detailed balance requires \(\pi_i T(i,j) = \pi_j T(j,i)\) for all \(i\neq j\); with \(\pi_i = 1/3\) this reduces to \[T(0,1)=T(1,0),\quad T(1,2)=T(2,1),\quad T(0,2)=T(2,0).\]
Substituting the entries gives \(a = b\) in each equation, so detailed balance holds iff \(a = b\). When \(a \neq b\) the chain is stationary but only cyclically so (clockwise vs. counter-clockwise probability flux differs by \(a-b\)).



Two-state Markov chain with \(T(0 \to 1) = \alpha\) and \(T(1 \to 0) = \beta\), where \(\alpha, \beta \in (0, 1)\). Find the stationary distribution \(\pi\) in closed form and verify detailed balance.


Solution. Stationarity \(\pi T = \pi\) yields \(\pi_0 \alpha = \pi_1 \beta\), and together with \(\pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1\), \[\pi_0 = \frac{\beta}{\alpha + \beta}, \qquad \pi_1 = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}.\] Detailed balance reads \(\pi_0 T(0\to1) = \pi_1 T(1\to 0)\), i.e. \(\tfrac{\beta\alpha}{\alpha+\beta} = \tfrac{\alpha\beta}{\alpha+\beta}\), which holds identically. Every two-state irreducible chain is reversible.


Three-state birth-death chain on \(\{0, 1, 2\}\) with \(T(i \to i+1) = p\) and \(T(i \to i-1) = q\) (where defined), self-loops chosen so each row sums to one. Find the stationary distribution in terms of the ratio \(r = p/q\).


Solution. Birth-death chains are reversible; detailed balance \(\pi_i T(i,i+1) = \pi_{i+1} T(i+1,i)\) gives the recursion \[\pi_{i+1} = \frac{p}{q}\,\pi_i = r\,\pi_i,\] so \(\pi_1 = r\pi_0\) and \(\pi_2 = r^2 \pi_0\). Normalising, \[\pi = \frac{1}{1 + r + r^2}\,(1, r, r^2).\]


Construct a 3-state Markov chain that admits a stationary distribution but does not satisfy detailed balance. (Hint: try a deterministic cycle \(0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 0\).) Verify both claims explicitly.


Solution. Take \(T(i, i+1 \bmod 3) = 1\), all other entries \(0\). This is doubly stochastic so \(\pi = (\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13)\) is stationary: \((\pi T)_j = \sum_i \pi_i T(i,j) = \tfrac13 \cdot 1 = \pi_j\). But \[\pi_0 T(0,1) = \tfrac13 \cdot 1 = \tfrac13, \qquad \pi_1 T(1,0) = \tfrac13 \cdot 0 = 0,\] so detailed balance fails. The chain is stationary by cyclic probability flux, not by pairwise balance.


Independence sampler with target \(\pi(x) = \text{Beta}(2, 2)\) on \((0,1)\) and proposal \(q(x) = \text{Uniform}(0,1)\). Write the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance probability \(\alpha(x \to x')\) in terms of \(x\), \(x'\), and identify the smallest constant \(M\) that would make every proposal accepted in a rejection sampler.


Solution. Here \(\pi(x) = 6x(1-x)\) and \(q(x) = 1\), so \(w(x) = 6x(1-x)\) and \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\!\left\{1,\,\frac{x'(1-x')}{x(1-x)}\right\}.\] The weight is maximised at \(x = 1/2\) with \(w(1/2) = 3/2\), so the smallest envelope constant for rejection sampling is \[M = \sup_x \frac{\pi(x)}{q(x)} = \tfrac{3}{2}.\]


Independence sampler with target \(\pi = \text{Exp}(1)\) and proposal \(q = \text{Exp}(2)\). Compute the importance weight \(w(x) = \pi(x)/q(x)\) and the acceptance ratio \(\alpha(x \to x') = \min\{1, w(x')/w(x)\}\).


Solution. With \(\pi(x) = e^{-x}\) and \(q(x) = 2e^{-2x}\) on \(x \geq 0\), \[w(x) = \frac{e^{-x}}{2e^{-2x}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{x}.\] Hence \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\!\left\{1, \frac{w(x')}{w(x)}\right\} = \min\!\left\{1, e^{x' - x}\right\}.\] The weight grows unboundedly with \(x\), so \(w\) is not bounded — the proposal has lighter tails than the target and the chain will tend to stall whenever it reaches large \(x\).


Show that an independence sampler with proposal \(q = \pi\) accepts every move and therefore generates i.i.d. samples from \(\pi\).


Solution. The importance weight is constant: \(w(x) = \pi(x)/q(x) = 1\). Therefore \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\!\left\{1, \frac{w(x')}{w(x)}\right\} = 1\] for every \(x, x'\). Every proposal is accepted, and since the proposal is independent of the current state and equal to \(\pi\), the successive draws are i.i.d. samples from \(\pi\).


Explain qualitatively why an independence sampler whose proposal \(q\) has lighter tails than the target \(\pi\) tends to get stuck in the tails of \(\pi\). Express your answer in terms of the importance weight \(w(x)\).


Solution. Light-tailed \(q\) means \(q(x) \ll \pi(x)\) as \(|x| \to \infty\), so the weight \[w(x) = \frac{\pi(x)}{q(x)} \to \infty.\] If the chain ever reaches a tail state \(x\) with very large \(w(x)\), the acceptance probability \(\alpha(x \to x') = \min\{1, w(x')/w(x)\}\) is tiny for any typical proposal \(x'\) from \(q\) (which lives near the mode and has \(w(x') \ll w(x)\)). The chain rejects almost every move and remains stuck at \(x\), producing a “sticking” pathology and an unbounded weight ratio (no \(C < \infty\) in \(\pi \leq C q\)).


Gibbs sampler for a bivariate Gaussian \((X, Y) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)\) with \(\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\). Derive the full conditionals \(p(x \mid y)\) and \(p(y \mid x)\) and give a Gibbs sweep update rule.


Solution. For a centred Gaussian the conditional mean and variance follow from \(E[X\mid Y=y] = \Sigma_{XY}\Sigma_{YY}^{-1}y = \rho y\) and \(\mathrm{Var}(X\mid Y) = 1 - \rho^2\). Hence \[X \mid Y = y \sim \mathcal{N}(\rho y,\, 1 - \rho^2),\qquad Y \mid X = x \sim \mathcal{N}(\rho x,\, 1 - \rho^2).\] One Gibbs sweep at step \(t\) is \[x^{(t)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\rho y^{(t-1)},\, 1-\rho^2\right), \qquad y^{(t)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\rho x^{(t)},\, 1-\rho^2\right).\]


Beta–Binomial hierarchical model: \(x \mid \theta \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \theta)\), \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\). Derive the full conditional \(p(\theta \mid x, \alpha, \beta)\) and identify it as a Beta distribution.


Solution. By Bayes’ rule, \[p(\theta \mid x, \alpha, \beta) \propto p(x \mid \theta)\,p(\theta) \propto \theta^x(1-\theta)^{n-x}\cdot \theta^{\alpha-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1} = \theta^{\alpha + x - 1}(1-\theta)^{\beta + n - x - 1}.\] This is the kernel of a Beta distribution, so \[\theta \mid x, \alpha, \beta \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha + x,\, \beta + n - x).\] Conjugacy makes the Gibbs update a closed-form Beta draw.


Two-component Gaussian mixture: \(X_i \mid Z_i = k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k, 1)\) with \(Z_i \in \{1, 2\}\) and \(P(Z_i = 1) = w\). Given \(\mu_1, \mu_2, w\) and the data, write the full conditional \(p(z_i = 1 \mid x_i)\) (the E-step of EM, but in the Gibbs context).


Solution. By Bayes’ rule, with \(\phi(x;\mu) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\{-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^2\}\), \[p(z_i = 1 \mid x_i) = \frac{w\,\phi(x_i;\mu_1)}{w\,\phi(x_i;\mu_1) + (1-w)\,\phi(x_i;\mu_2)}.\] Cancelling the common \(1/\sqrt{2\pi}\) factor gives the explicit form \[p(z_i = 1 \mid x_i) = \frac{w\,\exp\{-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_i-\mu_1)^2\}}{w\,\exp\{-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_i-\mu_1)^2\} + (1-w)\,\exp\{-\tfrac{1}{2}(x_i-\mu_2)^2\}}.\] The Gibbs update samples \(z_i\) from this Bernoulli; the EM E-step uses the same expression as a soft responsibility.


Show that the Gibbs sampler is a special case of Metropolis–Hastings in which the proposal for component \(k\) is the full conditional \(\pi(x_k \mid x_{-k})\), and that the corresponding acceptance probability is always \(1\).


Solution. Take the MH proposal \(q(x_k' \mid x_k, x_{-k}) = \pi(x_k' \mid x_{-k})\). The MH ratio factorises as \[\frac{\pi(x_k', x_{-k})\,\pi(x_k \mid x_{-k})}{\pi(x_k, x_{-k})\,\pi(x_k' \mid x_{-k})} = \frac{\pi(x_k'\mid x_{-k})\pi(x_{-i})\,\pi(x_k\mid x_{-i})}{\pi(x_k\mid x_{-i})\pi(x_{-i})\,\pi(x_k'\mid x_{-i})} = 1,\] using \(\pi(x_k, x_{-k}) = \pi(x_k \mid x_{-k})\pi(x_{-k})\). Hence \(\alpha(x \to x') = \min\{1, 1\} = 1\) for every Gibbs step.


Block Gibbs. Suppose \((\theta_1, \theta_2)\) have posterior correlation \(\rho\) close to \(1\). Sketch (no calculator) why coordinate-wise Gibbs mixes slowly and why updating \((\theta_1, \theta_2)\) jointly fixes the problem.


Solution. When \(\rho \approx 1\) the posterior concentrates on a narrow diagonal ridge. Each axis-aligned Gibbs step has conditional variance \(1-\rho^2 \ll 1\), so the chain inches along the ridge by small horizontal/vertical moves and the effective step along the ridge is \(O(\sqrt{1-\rho^2})\). The block update \[(\theta_1, \theta_2) \sim p(\theta_1, \theta_2 \mid \text{rest}, D)\] draws from the joint conditional, which has full variance along the ridge direction. One block step accomplishes what hundreds of coordinate-wise steps would require, eliminating the dependence on \(\rho\).


Reversibility from detailed balance. Verify that if a Markov chain satisfies \(\pi(x) T(x, y) = \pi(y) T(y, x)\) for all states \(x, y\), then \(\pi\) is stationary. Is the converse true?


Solution. Summing detailed balance over \(x\): \[\sum_x \pi(x) T(x,y) = \sum_x \pi(y) T(y,x) = \pi(y) \sum_x T(y,x) = \pi(y),\] so \((\pi T)(y) = \pi(y)\) and \(\pi\) is stationary. The converse is false: the deterministic cyclic chain \(0\to 1 \to 2 \to 0\) has \(\pi = (\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13)\) stationary but violates detailed balance.


Trivial chain. A Markov chain has transition kernel \(T(x, y) = \pi(y)\) for every \(x\) (i.e. each step is an independent draw from \(\pi\)). Verify detailed balance and compute the integrated autocorrelation time of any well-behaved test function.


Solution. Detailed balance: \(\pi(x)T(x,y) = \pi(x)\pi(y) = \pi(y)\pi(x) = \pi(y)T(y,x)\). Successive states are i.i.d. from \(\pi\), so for any test function \(f\) with finite variance the lag-\(k\) autocorrelation is \(\rho_k = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\), and \[\tau_{\mathrm{int}} = \tfrac{1}{2} + \sum_{k=1}^\infty \rho_k = \tfrac{1}{2}.\] This is the minimum possible value — i.i.d. sampling is the gold standard.


(Define and explain.) Define the independence sampler. Write its acceptance probability in terms of the importance weights \(w(x) = \pi(x)/q(x)\). Explain its connection to importance sampling.


Solution. The independence sampler is a Metropolis–Hastings algorithm whose proposal \(q(x' \mid x) = q(x')\) does not depend on the current state. The MH ratio collapses to \[\alpha(x \to x') = \min\!\left\{1,\,\frac{\pi(x')\,q(x)}{\pi(x)\,q(x')}\right\} = \min\!\left\{1,\,\frac{w(x')}{w(x)}\right\},\] the ratio of importance weights \(w(x) = \pi(x)/q(x)\). It is the MCMC analogue of importance sampling: \(q\) plays the role of a global proposal that should approximate \(\pi\), and the acceptance step (rather than weighting) corrects the mismatch. Ergodicity requires the envelope bound \(w(x) \leq C < \infty\).


(Define and explain.) Define the Gibbs sampler. Show that every Gibbs step is accepted, i.e. the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio equals \(1\) when the proposal is the full conditional.


Solution. The Gibbs sampler updates parameters \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_d)\) cyclically by sampling each coordinate from its full conditional \[\theta_i^{(t)} \sim p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i}, D).\] Viewed as MH with proposal \(q(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}) = p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i}, D)\), the acceptance ratio factorises via \(p(\theta_i, \theta_{-i}) = p(\theta_i\mid\theta_{-i})p(\theta_{-i})\): \[\frac{p(\theta_i',\theta_{-i})\,p(\theta_i \mid \theta_{-i})}{p(\theta_i,\theta_{-i})\,p(\theta_i' \mid \theta_{-i})} = \frac{p(\theta_i'\mid\theta_{-i})p(\theta_i\mid\theta_{-i})}{p(\theta_i\mid\theta_{-i})p(\theta_i'\mid\theta_{-i})} = 1.\] Hence \(\alpha = 1\) — every Gibbs proposal is accepted.


(Compare and contrast.) For a target with strongly correlated coordinates, compare the mixing behaviour of coordinate-wise Gibbs, block Gibbs, and random-walk Metropolis. State which one you would prefer in this situation and why.


Solution. 

Coordinate-wise Gibbs: always accepts but takes \(O\!\left(\tfrac{1}{1-\rho^2}\right)\) steps to cross the posterior ridge — slow when \(|\rho| \to 1\).
Random-walk MH with proposal covariance \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{prop}}\): also slow unless \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{prop}}\) is tuned to match the elongated posterior covariance \(\Sigma\); an isotropic proposal wastes effort across the ridge.
Block Gibbs: samples \((\theta_1,\theta_2)\) jointly from the exact joint conditional, removing the dependence on \(\rho\) entirely.

Preferred: block Gibbs, because it eliminates the bottleneck at its source. If the joint conditional is intractable, the next best option is random-walk MH with a preconditioned proposal \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{prop}} \propto \Sigma\) (or HMC).


(Define and explain.) Define integrated autocorrelation time. Explain in one sentence why grouping (blocking) strongly correlated parameters in a Gibbs sampler lowers it.


Solution. For a stationary chain and test function \(f\) with lag-\(k\) autocorrelation \(\rho_k\), \[\tau_{\mathrm{int}} = \tfrac{1}{2} + \sum_{k=1}^\infty \rho_k,\] so that the effective sample size is \(N_{\mathrm{eff}} = N/(2\tau_{\mathrm{int}})\). Blocking strongly correlated coordinates lets one update move along the posterior ridge in a single step (instead of many small axis-aligned moves), which collapses the slow tail of \(\rho_k\) and therefore reduces \(\tau_{\mathrm{int}}\).

title: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling
text:
9.8 Sources

Robert, C. P., & Casella, G. (2004). Monte Carlo Statistical Methods (2nd ed., Springer Texts in Statistics, Chapters 7 & 10).
Falasco, G. (2026). Advanced statistics for physics analysis (Sections 6.4–6.5, lecture notes).
crumbs: 9  Independence sampler and Gibbs sampling

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.1 Introduction
Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods form the backbone of modern Bayesian inference, but they face a fundamental challenge: as dimension and posterior curvature increase, standard samplers become inefficient. Random-walk Metropolis–Hastings requires ever-smaller step sizes to maintain acceptance, leading to slow exploration and highly autocorrelated samples. Independence samplers, which propose large moves, suffer equally in high dimensions as their proposals become increasingly unlikely.
Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) breaks this curse of dimensionality by exploiting the geometry of the target distribution. Instead of proposing moves blindly, it uses gradient information to propose distant, geometrically informed steps that remain probable under the posterior. By augmenting the state space with auxiliary momentum variables, HMC transforms random exploration into ballistic motion guided by the log-posterior surface.
This chapter synthesizes the mechanics, practical implementation, and automatic tuning of HMC. We cover the theoretical foundation (Hamiltonian dynamics and energy conservation), the discrete leapfrog integrator, the Metropolis correction for discretization error, and modern variants like the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) that remove manual tuning. We also discuss when HMC is preferable to alternatives and highlight its role in packages like Stan.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
section: 10.2 Why random-walk Metropolis fails in high dimensions
text:
10.2 Why random-walk Metropolis fails in high dimensions

Remark. Standard random-walk Metropolis proposes local moves: \[
x' = x + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_{\text{prop}}).
\]
Acceptance probability is \(\alpha = \min(1, e^{-\Delta \log \pi})\), where \(\Delta \log \pi = \log \pi(x') - \log \pi(x)\).
The curse: In \(d\) dimensions, for a fixed acceptance rate (typically \(\sim 0.23\) for the Metropolis algorithm in the limit), the optimal step size scales as \(O(d^{-1/2})\). This means: - To maintain constant acceptance as \(d\) grows, step sizes must shrink like \(d^{-1/2}\). - Distance explored per iteration scales as step size \(\times \sqrt{d} \sim O(1)\), regardless of dimension. - Autocorrelation grows linearly with \(d\), making the effective sample size collapse.
For correlated posteriors with high curvature (e.g., a narrow ridge), the problem is even worse: proposals must shrink to match the local geometry, requiring \(O(d)\) or \(O(d^2)\) steps to traverse the parameter space.


Bivariate Gaussian with strong correlation
Consider a target \(\pi(x) = \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)\) with \[
\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \rho = 0.95.
\]
The ridge is elongated; random-walk MH must take tiny steps perpendicular to the ridge to avoid rejection, then many small steps along the ridge. For a \(d=100\)-dimensional elongated Gaussian, RW-MH becomes prohibitively slow. HMC, by following the natural flow of the posterior, explores this space in far fewer iterations.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.3 Hamiltonian mechanics: the continuous story
Hamiltonian dynamics govern many physical systems. We repurpose them for sampling by augmenting the parameter space with momentum variables, then exploiting the conservation laws of the resulting flow.

Augmented state space. Let \(\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^d\) be the parameters of interest (confusingly called “position” in physics). We introduce momentum \(\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^d\), an auxiliary variable sampled from a Gaussian with covariance matrix \(M\) (the “mass matrix”).


The augmented target distribution is \[
\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) = \pi(\mathbf{q}) \, f(\mathbf{p} \mid M),
\] where \(f(\mathbf{p} \mid M) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, M)\) is the momentum density.
Crucial observation: Marginalizing over \(\mathbf{p}\) recovers the original target: \[
\int \pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) \, d\mathbf{p} = \pi(\mathbf{q}) \int \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, M) \, d\mathbf{p} = \pi(\mathbf{q}).
\]
Thus, sampling \((\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p})\) and discarding \(\mathbf{p}\) gives samples from \(\pi(\mathbf{q})\).


The Hamiltonian function. The joint density factorizes as \[
\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) = \pi(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, M) \propto \exp\left[-U(\mathbf{q}) - K(\mathbf{p})\right],
\] where we define the Hamiltonian \(H(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) = U(\mathbf{q}) + K(\mathbf{p})\).



Potential energy: \(U(\mathbf{q}) = -\log \pi(\mathbf{q})\).
Kinetic energy: \(K(\mathbf{p}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{p}^T M^{-1} \mathbf{p}\).

The Hamiltonian is \(H(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) = U(\mathbf{q}) + K(\mathbf{p})\).
The joint density becomes \(\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) \propto \exp[-H(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p})]\).

The mass matrix \(M\) controls the geometry: a diagonal \(M\) with larger entries allows larger steps in those directions. The default is \(M = I\) (identity), but adaptive mass matrices can be tuned to match posterior covariance.

Hamilton’s equations. The dynamics of a Hamiltonian system are governed by the canonical equations of motion.


Hamilton’s equations of motion: \[
\frac{d\mathbf{q}}{dt} = \nabla_{\mathbf{p}} H = M^{-1} \mathbf{p},
\] \[
\frac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} H = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U(\mathbf{q}) = \nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \log \pi(\mathbf{q}).
\]
Interpretation: - Position evolves along the momentum direction, scaled by \(M^{-1}\). - Momentum evolves in the direction of the gradient (natural gradient of log-posterior). - Key property: These equations naturally navigate toward high-probability regions via \(\nabla \log \pi\).


Energy conservation and volume preservation. An exact Hamiltonian trajectory conserves total energy \(H(\mathbf{q}(t), \mathbf{p}(t)) = \text{const}\), with profound consequences for sampling.


Energy conservation: Along an exact Hamiltonian trajectory, \(\frac{dH}{dt} = 0\).
Proof: \[
\frac{dH}{dt} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}^T \nabla_{\mathbf{q}} H + \dot{\mathbf{p}}^T \nabla_{\mathbf{p}} H = (\nabla_{\mathbf{p}} H)^T \nabla_{\mathbf{q}} H + (-\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} H)^T \nabla_{\mathbf{p}} H = 0.
\]

Exact trajectories move along level sets of the Hamiltonian, staying on surfaces of constant \(H\). Since \(\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) \propto e^{-H}\), exact trajectories move along level sets of the joint density — they are ideal proposals! Additionally, Hamiltonian flow is volume-preserving (symplectic) in phase space.

Liouville’s theorem: The divergence of the Hamiltonian velocity field is zero: \[
\nabla \cdot \begin{pmatrix} \dot{\mathbf{q}} \\ \dot{\mathbf{p}} \end{pmatrix} = \nabla_{\mathbf{q}} \cdot (M^{-1} \mathbf{p}) + \nabla_{\mathbf{p}} \cdot (-\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U) = 0 + 0 = 0.
\]
Thus Hamiltonian flow preserves volume in phase space and leaves \(\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p})\) invariant.

Together, energy conservation and volume preservation imply that Hamiltonian flow preserves \(\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p})\). But running the dynamics alone would never leave the level set determined by the initial condition: to explore the whole space we must periodically jump between level sets of \(H\) by refreshing \(\mathbf{p}\) from its Gaussian marginal.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.4 The leapfrog integrator
We cannot simulate Hamiltonian dynamics exactly on a computer. Instead, we discretize time using the leapfrog integrator, which maintains approximate energy conservation and reversibility while being computationally efficient.
