Leapfrog discretization. Given current state \((\mathbf{q}_0, \mathbf{p}_0)\), step size \(\varepsilon > 0\), and number of steps \(L\), one leapfrog step is defined below. The name “leapfrog” reflects the staggered half-steps: momentum is updated at half-steps, position at full steps.


One leapfrog step (from \((\mathbf{q}_t, \mathbf{p}_t)\) to \((\mathbf{q}_{t+1}, \mathbf{p}_{t+1})\)):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_{t+\varepsilon/2} &= \mathbf{p}_t - \frac{\varepsilon}{2} \nabla U(\mathbf{q}_t), \\
\mathbf{q}_{t+\varepsilon} &= \mathbf{q}_t + \varepsilon M^{-1} \mathbf{p}_{t+\varepsilon/2}, \\
\mathbf{p}_{t+\varepsilon} &= \mathbf{p}_{t+\varepsilon/2} - \frac{\varepsilon}{2} \nabla U(\mathbf{q}_{t+\varepsilon}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Repeating \(L\) times produces a trajectory of length \(T = L \varepsilon\).


Why leapfrog? The integrator has three essential properties that make it ideal for HMC.


The leapfrog integrator is:

Reversible: If we run the integrator backward (reversing \(\varepsilon \to -\varepsilon\) and flipping momentum at the end), we recover the initial state exactly (up to numerical precision). This ensures the proposal \((\mathbf{q}', \mathbf{p}') = \text{Leapfrog}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \varepsilon, L)\) and its reverse satisfy \(q((\mathbf{q}', \mathbf{p}') \to (\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p})) = q((\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) \to (\mathbf{q}', \mathbf{p}'))\), so the forward and reverse probabilities cancel in the Metropolis ratio.
Volume-preserving: The leapfrog map is a composition of volume-preserving transformations and preserves the Jacobian determinant. This reduces the Metropolis acceptance formula.
Symplectic: The integrator is a symplectic integrator, meaning it preserves the symplectic structure \(\mathbf{q} \wedge \mathbf{p}\). The error in energy per step is \(O(\varepsilon^2)\), so total error over \(L\) steps is \(O(\varepsilon^2)\).


For small \(\varepsilon\), the discretization error is tiny, energy error \(\Delta H \approx O(\varepsilon^2)\) accumulates slowly, and acceptance rates remain high. If \(\varepsilon\) is too large, energy errors accumulate and the rejection rate increases.

10.4.1 Example: 2D leapfrog trajectory
Let us visualize a leapfrog trajectory in 2D phase space for the bivariate Gaussian example.


Show code
# Define target (log-posterior and gradient)
rho <- 0.95
Sigma_inv <- solve(matrix(c(1, rho, rho, 1), 2, 2))
log_pi <- function(q) {
  -0.5 * as.numeric(t(q) %*% Sigma_inv %*% q)
}
grad_U <- function(q) {
  Sigma_inv %*% q
}

# Leapfrog integrator
leapfrog <- function(q, p, epsilon, L) {
  p <- p - 0.5 * epsilon * grad_U(q)
  for (i in 1:L) {
    q <- q + epsilon * p
    if (i < L) {
      p <- p - epsilon * grad_U(q)
    }
  }
  p <- p - 0.5 * epsilon * grad_U(q)
  list(q = q, p = -p)
}

# Simulate trajectory and track states
q0 <- c(2, 2)
p0 <- c(1, 0)
epsilon <- 0.1
L <- 100

q_curr <- q0
p_curr <- p0
trajectory_q <- matrix(NA, L+1, 2)
trajectory_p <- matrix(NA, L+1, 2)
trajectory_q[1,] <- q0
trajectory_p[1,] <- p0

for (step in 1:L) {
  # Half-step momentum
  p_curr <- p_curr - 0.5 * epsilon * grad_U(q_curr)
  # Full-step position
  q_curr <- q_curr + epsilon * p_curr
  # Half-step momentum
  p_curr <- p_curr - 0.5 * epsilon * grad_U(q_curr)
  trajectory_q[step+1,] <- q_curr
  trajectory_p[step+1,] <- p_curr
}

# Plot phase space trajectory
plot(trajectory_q[,1], trajectory_p[,1],
     type = "l", lwd = 1.5, col = "#d62728",
     main = "Leapfrog Trajectory in Phase Space (q1, p1)",
     xlab = "q1 (position)", ylab = "p1 (momentum)")
points(q0[1], p0[1], pch = 19, cex = 1.5, col = "#2ca02c")
points(trajectory_q[L+1, 1], trajectory_p[L+1, 1], pch = 19, cex = 1.5, col = "#1f77b4")
legend("topright", legend = c("Trajectory", "Start", "End"),
       col = c("#d62728", "#2ca02c", "#1f77b4"), pch = c(NA, 19, 19),
       lwd = c(1.5, NA, NA), bty = "n")
grid(col = "grey90")





Leapfrog trajectory (red spiral) in 2D phase space for a correlated bivariate Gaussian, starting from q=(2,2), p=(1,0). The trajectory spirals inward toward the mode due to energy dissipation in the discretization, then the Metropolis correction either accepts or rejects.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.5 The HMC algorithm
Combining leapfrog integration with Metropolis acceptance yields the complete HMC algorithm.

HMC Algorithm
Given current position \(\mathbf{q}_t\):

Refresh momentum: Draw \(\mathbf{p} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, M)\).
Store current energy: Compute \(H_{\text{old}} = U(\mathbf{q}_t) + K(\mathbf{p})\).
Integrate: Run leapfrog for \(L\) steps with step size \(\varepsilon\): \[
(\mathbf{q}', \mathbf{p}') = \text{Leapfrog}(\mathbf{q}_t, \mathbf{p}, \varepsilon, L).
\]
Compute proposal energy: \(H_{\text{new}} = U(\mathbf{q}') + K(\mathbf{p}')\).
Metropolis correction: Accept with probability \[
\alpha = \min\left(1, \exp(-\Delta H)\right), \quad \Delta H = H_{\text{new}} - H_{\text{old}}.
\]
Move or reject:

If \(u \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1) < \alpha\): set \(\mathbf{q}_{t+1} = \mathbf{q}'\).
Else: set \(\mathbf{q}_{t+1} = \mathbf{q}_t\).




Why the Metropolis correction? If we could simulate Hamiltonian dynamics exactly, \(\Delta H = 0\) and every proposal would be accepted. However, leapfrog introduces discretization error \(\Delta H \approx O(L \varepsilon^2)\). The Metropolis step removes any bias introduced by discretization, restores exact invariance to the joint distribution \(\pi(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p})\), and ensures that marginalizing over \(\mathbf{p}\) gives exact samples from \(\pi(\mathbf{q})\). A smaller \(\varepsilon\) reduces discretization error and increases acceptance; a larger \(\varepsilon\) reduces computation but risks rejection.
Momentum refresh. In step 1, we draw a fresh momentum independently of the previous momentum. This is crucial: otherwise, the trajectory would continue indefinitely on level sets of \(H\), exploring only one mode. By resampling \(\mathbf{p}\) at each iteration, we jump between level sets of \(H\) (exploring different energies), allow exploration of the full parameter space, and introduce randomness necessary for ergodicity. Notably, resampling \(\mathbf{p} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, M)\) is Gibbs sampling in the \((q, p)\) space.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.6 Tuning and practical guidance
HMC’s efficiency depends critically on three tuning parameters — step size \(\varepsilon\), number of leapfrog steps \(L\), and mass matrix \(M\) — together with a warm-up phase that adapts them automatically.

Step size \(\varepsilon\). This controls discretization granularity, and is the parameter most often tuned automatically.


The step size \(\varepsilon\) controls discretization granularity.

Too small: \(\varepsilon \to 0\) gives accurate proposals, high acceptance, but slow exploration (many gradient evaluations).
Too large: \(\varepsilon\) large causes energy errors to accumulate; acceptance rate \(\alpha\) drops.

Typical rule: Target acceptance rate of 0.65–0.80 (varies slightly with \(d\)). Adjust \(\varepsilon\) to match this during warm-up.


Trajectory length and number of steps \(L\). The product \(T = L \varepsilon\) is the simulated time and determines how far each proposal travels.


The trajectory length is \(T = L \varepsilon\). A longer trajectory explores farther but at higher computational cost.

Too few steps: Behaves like random-walk MH (local exploration).
Too many steps: Trajectories double back on themselves (waste computation and may reject).

Effective range: Aim for \(L \in [20, 100]\) initially; tune via ESS per gradient evaluation.


Mass matrix \(M\). The mass matrix controls how momentum couples to position. With \(M = I\) (identity), kinetic energy is \(K(\mathbf{p}) = \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{p}\|^2\) — isotropic.


Remark. Adaptive mass matrix: If the posterior has vastly different scales or high correlations, an isotropic \(M\) is inefficient. Adaptive HMC (as implemented in Stan) estimates the posterior covariance \(\Sigma_{\text{post}}\) during warm-up and sets \(M = \Sigma_{\text{post}}^{-1}\). This makes the “momentum-space” geometry match the “parameter-space” geometry, enabling larger steps and better mixing.
Equivalently, we can work with a reparametrization or preconditioning. The improvement can be dramatic in correlated or high-dimensional problems.


Warm-up and adaptation. Most HMC implementations (especially Stan) use a warm-up (or “burn-in”) phase to (i) find a reasonable starting point and estimate posterior covariance, (ii) adapt \(\varepsilon\) to target a desired acceptance rate, (iii) adapt \(M\) to match posterior structure, and (iv) discard warm-up samples as they are not from the stationary distribution. After warm-up, \(\varepsilon\) and \(M\) are fixed, and the algorithm becomes a standard Markov chain with invariant distribution \(\pi(\mathbf{q})\).

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.7 Divergent transitions and energy issues
HMC can fail in subtle ways:

Remark. Divergent transitions occur when the numerical integration goes unstable, typically because: - Step size \(\varepsilon\) is too large and energy error \(|\Delta H|\) becomes very large, causing rejection. - Posterior has regions of high curvature that the integrator cannot resolve. - Posterior has heavy tails or mild multimodality.
Indicator: Many proposals have \(\Delta H \gg 10\) (large energy errors).
Solution: Reduce \(\varepsilon\), increase warm-up iterations, or use an adaptive mass matrix. Stan prints warnings about divergences; they suggest the sampler is not exploring properly and posterior inferences may be biased.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.8 NUTS: No-U-Turn Sampler
Modern software like Stan uses the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), which removes the need to manually tune \(L\). Instead of running a fixed number of steps \(L\), NUTS adaptively expands the trajectory until it would begin to double back.

Doubling criterion: A trajectory “doubles back” (makes a U-turn) when the “generalized velocity” \((\mathbf{q}' - \mathbf{q}_{\text{left}})^T \mathbf{p}\) becomes negative, indicating backward motion. More precisely, a U-turn is detected using the dot product: \[
(\mathbf{q}_{\text{right}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{left}})^T \mathbf{p}_{\text{right}} < 0 \quad \text{or} \quad (\mathbf{q}_{\text{right}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{left}})^T \mathbf{p}_{\text{left}} < 0.
\]
NUTS extends the trajectory by “doubling” (adding steps in the same direction) until a U-turn is detected, then accepts from the entire trajectory.

The advantages of NUTS over fixed-\(L\) HMC are:

Eliminates manual tuning of \(L\): The algorithm automatically determines trajectory length.
Prevents wasted computation: Stops before the trajectory begins circling back.
Maintains properties: The randomized doubling ensures detailed balance is preserved (via Metropolis-from-trajectory).
Stan implementation: NUTS is the default sampler in Stan with automatic warm-up and adaptation.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
section: 10.9 Comparison: HMC vs random-walk MH vs Gibbs
text:
10.9 Comparison: HMC vs random-walk MH vs Gibbs

Scaling with dimension \(d\):



Sampler
Acceptance
Auto-correlation
ESS per gradient




RW-MH
\(O(1)\)
\(O(d)\)
\(O(d^{-2})\)


Gibbs
\(1\)
\(O(d)\)
\(O(d^{-1})\) if uncorrelated


HMC
\(0.6\)–\(0.9\)
\(O(\sqrt{d})\) or better
\(O(1)\) to \(O(d^{-1})\)



(Rough asymptotics; constants matter.)
Practical implications: - RW-MH: Simple to implement, no gradients needed. But prohibitively slow for \(d > 20\) unless posterior is nearly isotropic. - Gibbs: Requires conditional distributions. Fast if draws from conditionals are cheap. Struggles with correlations. - HMC: Requires gradients but dramatically outperforms RW-MH in medium to high dimensions (\(d \geq 10\)). Excellent when gradients are available (automatic differentiation).


Comparison on correlated bivariate Gaussian
We sample from \(\mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)\) with \(\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0.95 \\ 0.95 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\) using: 1. Random-walk MH with \(\text{step} = 0.3\). 2. HMC with \(\varepsilon = 0.15\), \(L = 20\).


Show code
# Setup
rho <- 0.95
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, rho, rho, 1), 2, 2)
Sigma_inv <- solve(Sigma)

log_pi <- function(q) {
  -0.5 * as.numeric(t(q) %*% Sigma_inv %*% q)
}

grad_U <- function(q) {
  Sigma_inv %*% q
}

# RW-MH
rw_mh <- function(n_iter, step = 0.3) {
  q <- c(0, 0)
  samples <- matrix(NA, n_iter, 2)
  acc <- 0
  for (i in 1:n_iter) {
    q_prop <- q + rnorm(2, 0, step)
    log_alpha <- log_pi(q_prop) - log_pi(q)
    if (log(runif(1)) < log_alpha) {
      q <- q_prop
      acc <- acc + 1
    }
    samples[i, ] <- q
  }
  list(samples = samples, acc_rate = acc / n_iter)
}

# Leapfrog for HMC
leapfrog <- function(q, p, eps, L) {
  p <- p - 0.5 * eps * grad_U(q)
  for (i in 1:L) {
    q <- q + eps * p
    if (i < L) p <- p - eps * grad_U(q)
  }
  p <- p - 0.5 * eps * grad_U(q)
  list(q = q, p = -p)
}

# HMC
hmc <- function(n_iter, eps = 0.15, L = 20) {
  q <- c(0, 0)
  samples <- matrix(NA, n_iter, 2)
  acc <- 0
  for (i in 1:n_iter) {
    p <- rnorm(2)
    H_old <- 0.5 * as.numeric(t(q) %*% Sigma_inv %*% q) + 0.5 * sum(p^2)
    prop <- leapfrog(q, p, eps, L)
    q_prop <- prop$q
    p_prop <- prop$p
    H_new <- 0.5 * as.numeric(t(q_prop) %*% Sigma_inv %*% q_prop) + 0.5 * sum(p_prop^2)
    log_alpha <- -(H_new - H_old)
    if (log(runif(1)) < log_alpha) {
      q <- q_prop
      acc <- acc + 1
    }
    samples[i, ] <- q
  }
  list(samples = samples, acc_rate = acc / n_iter)
}

# Run samplers
set.seed(42)
n_iter <- 2000
fit_mh <- rw_mh(n_iter, step = 0.3)
fit_hmc <- hmc(n_iter, eps = 0.15, L = 20)

# Plot traces
par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

plot(fit_mh$samples[,1], type = "l", lwd = 1, col = "#1f77b4",
     main = paste0("RW-MH Trace (acc=", round(fit_mh$acc_rate, 3), ")"),
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "q1")
grid(col = "grey90")

plot(fit_hmc$samples[,1], type = "l", lwd = 1, col = "#d62728",
     main = paste0("HMC Trace (acc=", round(fit_hmc$acc_rate, 3), ")"),
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "q1")
grid(col = "grey90")

# Plot ACF
acf(fit_mh$samples[,1], main = "RW-MH Autocorr", lag.max = 50, col = "#1f77b4")
acf(fit_hmc$samples[,1], main = "HMC Autocorr", lag.max = 50, col = "#d62728")





Trace plots (top) and autocorrelation (bottom) for RW-MH (left) vs HMC (right) on correlated 2D Gaussian. HMC explores the ridge efficiently; RW-MH gets stuck.




RW-MH has acceptance \(\approx 0.61\) and poor mixing; autocorrelation decays slowly. HMC has acceptance \(\approx 0.96\) and autocorrelation decays in \(\sim 10\) lags, yielding much larger effective sample size per gradient evaluation.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.10 Practical recommendations

Remark. When to use HMC: - Posterior dimension \(d \geq 10\). - Gradient \(\nabla \log \pi\) is available (exact or via automatic differentiation). - Posterior is not highly multimodal with separated modes. - Computational budget permits gradient evaluations (modern autodiff is fast).
When HMC struggles: - Highly multimodal posterior with isolated modes (HMC gets trapped in one mode; consider parallel tempering or other global methods). - Discrete parameters (HMC requires continuous state; marginalize out discrete variables or use other samplers). - Gradient unavailable and too expensive to compute numerically.
When to prefer alternatives: - Gibbs sampling: If conditional distributions are easy to sample. Better mixing if conditionals are well-separated; worse if highly correlated. - Random-walk MH: Low-dimensional (\(d < 10\)), no gradients, simple implementation preferred. - Slice sampling or other adaptive methods: If gradient unavailable but adaptive exploration is desired.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.11 Stan and modern practice
The probabilistic programming language Stan uses NUTS with automatic warm-up by default. This means:

Users specify the log-posterior (via a model statement).
Stan automatically differentiates to obtain \(\nabla \log \pi\) via the reverse-mode autodiff.
During warm-up, step size \(\varepsilon\) is tuned to target 0.80 acceptance; mass matrix \(M\) is adapted.
NUTS extends trajectories adaptively without manual specification of \(L\).
Convergence diagnostics (\(\hat{R}\), effective sample size) are computed automatically.

This makes HMC accessible to practitioners without deep knowledge of tuning. Stan is now the most widely used tool for general-purpose Bayesian inference, precisely because it combines HMC’s superior performance with ease of use.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.12 Exercises

For a target \(\pi(\theta) \propto \exp(-U(\theta))\) with \(U(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta^\top\theta\) (standard Gaussian), write the Hamiltonian \(H(\theta, p)\) with a unit mass matrix, the canonical equations of motion, and the analytic solution for \((\theta(t), p(t))\).


Solution. With \(M = I\) the Hamiltonian is \[H(\theta, p) = \tfrac{1}{2}\theta^\top\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}p^\top p,\] and Hamilton’s equations give \(\dot\theta = p\), \(\dot p = -\theta\). This is the harmonic oscillator, whose closed-form solution is \[\theta(t) = \theta_0\cos t + p_0\sin t, \qquad p(t) = -\theta_0\sin t + p_0\cos t,\] i.e. a rigid rotation of phase space, so the trajectory stays exactly on the level set \(H = \tfrac{1}{2}(\|\theta_0\|^2+\|p_0\|^2)\).


Show that for a separable Hamiltonian \(H(\theta, p) = U(\theta) + K(p)\) with \(K(p) = \tfrac{1}{2}p^\top M^{-1}p\), the canonical equations decouple into \(\dot\theta = M^{-1}p\) and \(\dot p = -\nabla U(\theta)\).


Solution. Because \(U\) depends only on \(\theta\) and \(K\) only on \(p\), the gradients separate: \(\nabla_p H = \nabla_p K(p)\) and \(\nabla_\theta H = \nabla_\theta U(\theta)\). Hamilton’s equations therefore become \[\dot\theta = \nabla_p H = M^{-1}p, \qquad \dot p = -\nabla_\theta H = -\nabla U(\theta),\] so position is driven only by \(p\) (through \(M^{-1}\)) and momentum only by the potential gradient — the coupling between \(\theta\) and \(p\) is gone, which is exactly what lets leapfrog use alternating explicit half-steps.


Energy conservation. Differentiate \(H(\theta(t), p(t))\) along an exact trajectory and show that \(dH/dt = 0\). State why the leapfrog integrator does not exactly conserve \(H\) but conserves a modified shadow Hamiltonian.


Solution. Along an exact trajectory, \[\frac{dH}{dt} = \nabla_\theta H \cdot \dot\theta + \nabla_p H \cdot \dot p = \nabla_\theta H \cdot \nabla_p H + \nabla_p H \cdot (-\nabla_\theta H) = 0.\] Leapfrog is a discrete symplectic map, not the exact flow, so \(H\) oscillates by \(O(\varepsilon^2)\). By backward-error analysis it instead conserves a shadow Hamiltonian \(\tilde H = H + \varepsilon^2 H_2 + \dots\) exactly, which is why \(\Delta H\) stays bounded over long trajectories instead of drifting.


Write out one full step of the leapfrog integrator for a separable Hamiltonian with step size \(\varepsilon\). Show that the update is symplectic by checking that the Jacobian determinant equals one.


Solution. The three sub-steps are \[p_{1/2} = p_0 - \tfrac{\varepsilon}{2}\nabla U(\theta_0), \quad \theta_1 = \theta_0 + \varepsilon M^{-1}p_{1/2}, \quad p_1 = p_{1/2} - \tfrac{\varepsilon}{2}\nabla U(\theta_1).\] Each sub-step is a shear in either \(\theta\) or \(p\), so its Jacobian is upper- or lower-triangular with unit diagonal, e.g. the half kick has Jacobian \(\begin{pmatrix} I & 0 \\ -\tfrac{\varepsilon}{2}\nabla^2 U & I\end{pmatrix}\) with determinant \(1\). The full step is the composition, hence \(\det J = 1\cdot 1\cdot 1 = 1\), so leapfrog preserves phase-space volume and the Metropolis ratio reduces to \(\exp(-\Delta H)\).


Why random-walk Metropolis fails in high dimensions. Argue that for an isotropic Gaussian target in \(d\) dimensions, the optimal step size scales as \(\varepsilon \sim d^{-1/2}\) and the number of steps to traverse the typical set scales as \(d^{1/2}\), giving a quadratic cost in \(d\). Contrast with HMC’s \(d^{1/4}\) cost.


Solution. A proposal \(\theta' = \theta + \varepsilon\xi\) with \(\xi\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I)\) produces \(\Delta\log\pi\) with mean \(-\tfrac{1}{2}d\varepsilon^2\) and variance \(\sim d\varepsilon^2\), so keeping a non-vanishing acceptance forces \(d\varepsilon^2 = O(1)\), i.e. \(\varepsilon \sim d^{-1/2}\). The displacement per accepted step is then \(\|\Delta\theta\|\sim\varepsilon\sqrt{d}\sim 1\), and crossing the typical set of radius \(\sqrt{d}\) as a diffusion requires \[N_{\text{RW-MH}} \sim d,\qquad \text{i.e. total cost}\propto d,\] which becomes \(d^2\) once correlation length is included. HMC instead tolerates \(\varepsilon\sim d^{-1/4}\) (the leapfrog energy error is \(O(\varepsilon^2)\) summed coherently), so trajectories of length \(T = O(1)\) need \(L\sim d^{1/4}\) gradient evaluations — exponentially better in high \(d\).


HMC acceptance probability. Show that the acceptance probability of a proposed move \((\theta_0, p_0) \to (\theta_L, p_L)\) generated by leapfrog is \[\alpha = \min\!\left\{1,\ \exp\!\big(-[H(\theta_L, p_L) - H(\theta_0, p_0)]\big)\right\}.\]


Solution. The augmented target is \(\pi(\theta,p) \propto e^{-H(\theta,p)}\). Leapfrog combined with the final momentum flip \(p\mapsto -p\) gives a deterministic, volume-preserving, self-inverse proposal, so the forward and reverse proposal densities are equal and their Jacobian is \(1\). The Metropolis–Hastings ratio collapses to \[\alpha = \min\!\left\{1, \frac{e^{-H(\theta_L,p_L)}}{e^{-H(\theta_0,p_0)}}\right\} = \min\{1,\exp(-\Delta H)\}.\] Because exact dynamics would give \(\Delta H = 0\), the only source of rejection is leapfrog’s discretization error.


Optimal acceptance rate. State the result (Beskos et al., 2013) that the optimal HMC acceptance for isotropic Gaussian targets is approximately \(0.65\). Sketch the argument in words: too-low acceptance wastes proposals, too-high acceptance means \(\varepsilon\) is too small.


Solution. For separable Gaussian targets the leapfrog energy error is asymptotically Gaussian, \(\Delta H \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, 2\mu)\) with \(\mu \propto d\varepsilon^4\), so the expected squared jumping distance per gradient evaluation is maximised at \[\alpha^\star = \mathbb{E}[\min\{1,e^{-\Delta H}\}] \approx 0.651.\] Heuristically, very low acceptance means most leapfrog work is thrown away on rejected trajectories, while very high acceptance means \(\varepsilon\) is so small that each accepted trajectory barely moves; the sweet spot trades these costs.


Mass-matrix reparametrisation. Show that running HMC with mass matrix \(M\) on parameter \(\theta\) is equivalent to running HMC with identity mass matrix on the reparametrised variable \(\tilde\theta = M^{1/2}\theta\). State the practical implication for posteriors with correlated parameters.


Solution. Let \(\tilde\theta = M^{1/2}\theta\) and \(\tilde p = M^{-1/2}p\). Then the kinetic energy becomes \[K(p) = \tfrac{1}{2}p^\top M^{-1}p = \tfrac{1}{2}\tilde p^\top\tilde p,\] so in \((\tilde\theta,\tilde p)\) coordinates the kinetic energy uses the identity mass matrix, while the Jacobian \(|\det M^{1/2}|\) is constant and cancels in the acceptance ratio. Practically, choosing \(M\approx \Sigma_{\text{post}}^{-1}\) whitens correlated posteriors: HMC sees an approximately isotropic problem in \(\tilde\theta\), so a single step size works in all directions and step length need not shrink to the smallest eigendirection.


Divergent transitions. Describe when leapfrog energy errors blow up. Give one example posterior geometry (e.g. a “funnel” in hierarchical models) where divergences are common, and the standard remedy (non-centered reparametrisation).


Solution. Leapfrog energy error scales as \(\Delta H = O(\varepsilon^2\|\nabla^2 U\|)\), so wherever the local curvature spikes, \(|\Delta H|\) explodes and Stan flags a divergence (typically \(|\Delta H|\gtrsim 10^3\)). Neal’s funnel \(\theta_i \mid \tau \sim \mathcal{N}(0,e^\tau)\), \(\tau\sim\mathcal{N}(0,9)\) is the canonical example: near \(\tau\ll 0\) the funnel neck is so narrow that no single \(\varepsilon\) works for both the neck and the mouth. The remedy is the non-centred reparametrisation \[\theta_i = e^{\tau/2}z_i, \qquad z_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1),\] which decouples scale from location and gives an approximately isotropic posterior in \((z,\tau)\).


NUTS termination criterion. Describe Hoffman & Gelman’s no-U-turn condition: stop doubling the trajectory once the proposal starts curling back on itself. Why does this remove the need to tune the trajectory length \(L\) manually?


Solution. NUTS keeps doubling the trajectory in random forward/backward directions and terminates once the displacement vector \(\Delta\theta = \theta_{\text{right}} - \theta_{\text{left}}\) stops aligning with the momentum at either endpoint, i.e. \[\Delta\theta^\top p_{\text{right}} < 0 \quad\text{or}\quad \Delta\theta^\top p_{\text{left}} < 0.\] At that instant any further integration would curve the trajectory back on itself, wasting gradient evaluations. Because the criterion is local and computed on the fly, the user no longer has to pick a fixed \(L\): NUTS adapts the trajectory length per iteration to the local geometry while preserving detailed balance through a slice/multinomial sampler over the built tree.


Time reversibility. Show that the leapfrog integrator is reversible: if we apply it for \(L\) steps to go from \((\theta_0, p_0)\) to \((\theta_L, p_L)\), then a single negation \(p_L \mapsto -p_L\) followed by \(L\) leapfrog steps returns to \((\theta_0, -p_0)\).


Solution. The kinetic energy is even in \(p\), so under \(p\mapsto -p\) the equations \(\dot\theta = M^{-1}p\), \(\dot p = -\nabla U\) become \(\dot\theta = -M^{-1}p\), \(\dot p = \nabla U\) — exactly the time-reversed dynamics. For one leapfrog step, applying the three sub-steps with negated momentum literally undoes the original substeps in reverse order: \[\Phi_\varepsilon\circ R\circ\Phi_\varepsilon = R, \qquad R: (\theta,p)\mapsto(\theta,-p).\] Composing \(L\) such involutions gives \(\Phi_\varepsilon^L\circ R\circ\Phi_\varepsilon^L(\theta_0,p_0) = (\theta_0,-p_0)\), which is what makes the leapfrog proposal symmetric so the MH ratio depends only on \(\Delta H\).


Effect of step size on energy error. For leapfrog applied to a quadratic Hamiltonian, show that the per-step energy error scales as \(O(\varepsilon^3)\) but the total error over a trajectory of fixed length scales as \(O(\varepsilon^2)\). Why does this argue for keeping \(\varepsilon\) moderately small but not vanishingly small?


Solution. Leapfrog is a second-order symplectic method, so its local truncation error in \(H\) per step is \(O(\varepsilon^3)\). Over a trajectory of fixed length \(T = L\varepsilon\), the number of steps is \(L = T/\varepsilon\), so the global energy error scales as \[\Delta H_{\text{total}} = L\cdot O(\varepsilon^3) = O(\varepsilon^2).\] Shrinking \(\varepsilon\) further keeps \(\Delta H\) tiny and acceptance near \(1\), but doubles the number of gradient evaluations: cost scales like \(1/\varepsilon\) while ESS gain is sub-linear, so the sweet spot is a moderate \(\varepsilon\) targeting ~65–80% acceptance, not \(\varepsilon\to 0\).


HMC vs random-walk Metropolis. Sketch on a single plot: target trace from random-walk MH with poorly tuned step size, target trace from HMC with reasonable \((\varepsilon, L)\). Identify the integrated autocorrelation time of each visually and explain the gap.


Solution. The RW-MH trace looks like a slow diffusive walk with long flat plateaus (frequent rejections) and visible drift over many iterations, so its integrated autocorrelation time \(\tau_{\text{RW}}\) is large; the HMC trace looks like nearly independent draws hopping across the support, with \(\tau_{\text{HMC}}\approx 1\)–\(5\). The ESS ratio scales as \[\frac{\text{ESS}_{\text{HMC}}}{\text{ESS}_{\text{RW}}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{RW}}}{\tau_{\text{HMC}}} \sim \frac{d}{d^{1/2}} = d^{1/2},\] because each HMC trajectory traverses an \(O(1)\) fraction of the typical set in one move, while RW-MH only takes a step of size \(O(d^{-1/2})\).


Define the typical set of a \(d\)-dimensional posterior. Argue why HMC, which conserves energy and follows gradient flow, stays inside the typical set, while random-walk MH wastes most of its time outside it in high dimensions.


Solution. The typical set \(\mathcal{T}_\delta\) is the thin shell of states whose log-density equals its expectation up to \(\delta\): \[\mathcal{T}_\delta = \{\theta : |\log\pi(\theta) - \mathbb{E}_\pi[\log\pi]| < \delta\},\] which in high \(d\) concentrates near a \((d{-}1)\)-dimensional surface far from the mode (e.g. a thin shell of radius \(\sqrt{d}\) for \(\mathcal{N}(0,I)\)). HMC trajectories conserve \(H = U + K\), and on average \(U(\theta)\approx \mathbb{E}_\pi[U]\), so the dynamics flow along this level set — they orbit inside \(\mathcal{T}_\delta\) by construction. RW-MH proposes isotropically in \(\mathbb{R}^d\), where almost all directions point off the thin shell, so most proposals leave the typical set, get rejected, and the chain barely moves.


Practical tuning. A Stan run reports \(\hat R = 1.01\), \(\text{ESS} = 200\) per chain, divergences = 12 out of 4000. Decide: is this acceptable? If not, list the standard remedies in order of priority.


Solution.

title: 10  Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text:
10.13 Sources

Neal, R. M. (2011). MCMC Using Hamiltonian Dynamics. In Handbook of Markov Chain Monte Carlo (pp. 113–162). Chapman and Hall/CRC. [Canonical reference for HMC theory and variants.]
Robert, C. P., & Casella, G. (2004). Monte Carlo Statistical Methods (2nd ed.). Springer. [Chapter 7.8 covers Hamiltonian mechanics and energy conservation in MCMC.]
Hoffman, M. D., & Gelman, A. (2014). The No-U-Turn Sampler: Adaptively Setting Path Lengths in Hamiltonian Monte Carlo. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 15, 1351–1381. [Foundational paper on NUTS.]
Carpenter, B., et al. (2017). Stan: A probabilistic programming language. Journal of Statistical Software, 76(1). [Stan implementation and practical guidance.]

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.1 Introduction: The multimodality trap
Standard Metropolis–Hastings and Hamiltonian Monte Carlo samplers work well when the target posterior is unimodal or has modes close together. However, when the posterior has several well-separated modes, a single MCMC chain becomes trapped near the mode it encounters first. It cannot escape because crossing the energy barrier between modes occurs with probability decaying exponentially in the barrier height.
Consider two modes separated by a barrier of height \(\Delta E\). The probability of escaping is approximately \[p \approx \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)\] by the Arrhenius formula, where we write the posterior as \(\pi(\theta) = \exp[-E(\theta)]\). When \(\Delta E \gg 1\), this probability is negligibly small, and the chain explores only a tiny fraction of the parameter space.
Simulated annealing and parallel tempering both tackle this problem by introducing a temperature parameter. They exploit a key observation: at high temperature \(T\), the posterior is flattened, and energy barriers become easier to cross. The two methods differ in how they use this idea.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.2 Tempered distributions and temperatures
For any target posterior \(\pi(\theta)\) with energy representation \(\pi(\theta) = \exp[-E(\theta)]\), we define the tempered posterior at inverse temperature \(\beta \in (0, 1]\) by \[\pi_\beta(\theta) \propto \pi(\theta)^\beta = \exp[-\beta E(\theta)].\]
Equivalently, using temperature \(T = 1/\beta\), \[\pi_T(\theta) \propto \pi(\theta)^{1/T} = \exp\left[-\frac{E(\theta)}{T}\right].\]
The two limits play distinct roles:

Cold limit (\(\beta \to 1\) or \(T \to 1\)). \(\pi_\beta(\theta) \to \pi(\theta)\), the original posterior.
Hot limit (\(\beta \to 0\) or \(T \to \infty\)). \(\pi_\beta(\theta)\) becomes nearly flat, and the chain explores the entire space freely.
Concentrated limit (\(T \to 0\), used in optimization). As the temperature decreases to zero, \(\pi_T(\theta)\) concentrates its mass on the global minima of \(E(\theta)\).

At high temperature, the contrast between modes vanishes, making it easy for the chain to move between them. As the temperature decreases, the modes become increasingly separated again.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.3 Simulated annealing
Simulated annealing is an optimization method that cools a single chain to find the maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimator \(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta \pi(\theta)\). It generates a sequence of increasingly concentrated distributions by running a cooling schedule.
Simulated annealing algorithm:

Initialize \(\theta_0\) and temperature \(T_0 > 0\).
For \(k = 0, 1, \ldots, K\):

Propose \(\theta' \sim q(\theta' \mid \theta_k)\).
Accept with probability \(\alpha_T(\theta_k \to \theta') = \min\{1, \exp[-\beta_k(E(\theta') - E(\theta_k))]\}\) where \(\beta_k = 1/T_k\).
Update \(T_{k+1} = \gamma T_k\) for some \(0 < \gamma < 1\) (e.g., geometric cooling) or use \(T_k = c / \log k\) (logarithmic cooling).

Return the state \(\theta\) with highest \(\pi(\theta)\) encountered.

The choice of cooling schedule controls both correctness and cost:

Logarithmic cooling and barrier crossing. With \(T_k = c / \log k\), the chain crosses any fixed energy barrier infinitely often (with probability 1) as \(k \to \infty\), because the cumulative escape probability \[\sum_{k=1}^\infty p_k = \sum_{k=1}^\infty \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E \cdot \log k}{c}\right) = \sum_{k=1}^\infty k^{-\Delta E/c}\] diverges if and only if \(\Delta E/c \leq 1\), i.e., \(c \geq \Delta E\). Simulated annealing is therefore theoretically guaranteed to find the global optimum almost surely with sufficiently slow cooling.
Geometric cooling in practice. Slower cooling requires more iterations, so the geometric schedule \(T_{k+1} = \gamma T_k\) is usually preferred for computational speed, accepting that the optimality guarantee becomes heuristic.
What is returned. The output is the state with the highest \(\pi(\theta)\) encountered (or the final low-temperature state), not a sample from \(\pi\) — annealing answers an optimization question, not a sampling one.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.4 Parallel tempering (replica exchange)
Parallel tempering runs \(K\) chains in parallel, each at a different inverse temperature \(\beta_1 = 1 > \beta_2 > \cdots > \beta_K > 0\). The chain at \(\beta_1 = 1\) is the “cold chain” that targets the posterior of interest. Chains at smaller \(\beta\) are “hot chains” that explore more freely. The key innovation is swaps: occasionally, we exchange the states of neighbouring chains. This allows the cold chain to benefit from discoveries made by hot chains, enabling it to escape local modes without the cold chain ever needing to climb the barrier alone.
The algorithm is built from two pieces — a joint target and two move types:

Joint target. Let \(\theta_l\) denote the state of chain \(l\), write \(\Theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_K)\), and define the product target \[\Pi(\Theta) = \prod_{l=1}^K \pi_{\beta_l}(\theta_l) \propto \prod_{l=1}^K \pi(\theta_l)^{\beta_l}.\]
Within-temperature moves. Each chain runs a standard Metropolis–Hastings update with the tempered acceptance probability \[\alpha_l(\theta_l \to \theta_l') = \min\left\{1, \left(\frac{\pi(\theta_l')}{\pi(\theta_l)}\right)^{\beta_l} \frac{q(\theta_l \mid \theta_l')}{q(\theta_l' \mid \theta_l)}\right\}.\] Hot chains (small \(\beta_l\)) accept uphill moves more readily, allowing them to traverse the landscape freely.


The marginal distribution of \(\theta_1\) (the cold chain) is exactly \[\pi_{\beta_1}(\theta_1) = \pi(\theta_1),\] since \(\beta_1 = 1\). Samples from the cold chain are valid posterior samples, and expectations computed from the cold chain are unbiased estimates of posterior expectations.


11.4.1 Swap moves and detailed balance
The second type of move exchanges states between neighbouring chains \(l\) and \(l+1\): \[(\theta_l, \theta_{l+1}) \to (\theta_{l+1}, \theta_l).\]
The Metropolis acceptance probability for this move is \[\alpha_{\text{swap}}(\theta_l, \theta_{l+1}) = \min\left\{1, \frac{\pi(\theta_{l+1})^{\beta_l} \pi(\theta_l)^{\beta_{l+1}}}{\pi(\theta_l)^{\beta_l} \pi(\theta_{l+1})^{\beta_{l+1}}}\right\}.\]
In log form (preferred numerically): \[\log \alpha_{\text{swap}} = \min\{0, (\beta_l - \beta_{l+1})[E(\theta_l) - E(\theta_{l+1})]\}.\]
The normalizing constants cancel, making the swap probability independent of the partition function.

The combined algorithm of within-temperature MH updates and swap moves satisfies detailed balance with respect to the product target \(\Pi(\Theta)\). Therefore, the cold chain’s marginal converges to \(\pi\).


Proof. Within-temperature moves leave \(\pi_{\beta_l}(\theta_l)\) invariant at each temperature. Swap moves satisfy detailed balance for the product density: the probability of being in state \((\theta_l, \theta_{l+1})\) and swapping to \((\theta_{l+1}, \theta_l)\) equals the probability of being in \((\theta_{l+1}, \theta_l)\) and swapping back (by the Metropolis ratio). The product of these two reversible operations preserves \(\Pi(\Theta)\).

The mechanism that makes the cold chain mix can now be read off the construction:

Hot chains explore freely. Because \(\beta_l\) is small for hot chains, they traverse the landscape easily, finding new modes.
Swaps transport information. When a hot chain discovers a mode, a swap can move it to a colder chain, bringing the discovery closer to the target.
Cold chain remains on target. The cold chain never leaves the target distribution \(\pi\), yet it benefits from the hot chains’ discoveries via successful swaps.

This is the crucial difference from simulated annealing: the temperature is fixed, not decreasing. The algorithm is homogeneous, run indefinitely, and all cold-chain samples are valid posterior samples.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.5 Temperature ladder design
The spacing of temperatures is critical. If \(\beta\) values are too far apart, swap acceptance rates become vanishingly small, and information cannot flow between chains. If they are too close, many chains are needed. Three design choices control the trade-off:

Geometric spacing. A simple and effective choice is \[\beta_l = \beta_1 r^{l-1}, \quad 0 < r < 1,\] which keeps temperatures evenly spaced in a logarithmic sense. Under the standard assumption that the energy variance scales linearly with the dimension, geometric spacing approximately equalises swap acceptance between every adjacent pair.
Target swap acceptance. A practical rule of thumb is to set the ladder so that the swap acceptance probability between adjacent chains is in the range 20–40%. Below this range information stops flowing along the ladder; above it the ladder is denser than necessary and one is paying for redundant chains.
All-pairs vs. neighbour-only swaps. The basic algorithm swaps only neighbouring pairs. Allowing swaps between arbitrary chains (“all-pairs”) can improve mixing but at higher computational cost. For most problems, neighbour-only swaps are sufficient if the ladder is carefully tuned.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.6 Worked example: bimodal 1D mixture

Consider a bimodal posterior that is a mixture of two Gaussians: \[\pi(\theta) \propto 0.35 \, N(\theta; -4, 1) + 0.65 \, N(\theta; 3, 0.7^2).\]
The right mode is higher. We will show that a single-chain MH sampler gets trapped, while parallel tempering explores both modes.



Show code
# Log-posterior for bimodal mixture (minus normalization)
log_pi <- function(x) {
  a <- log(0.35) + dnorm(x, -4, 1, log = TRUE)
  b <- log(0.65) + dnorm(x, 3, 0.7, log = TRUE)
  m <- pmax(a, b)
  m + log(exp(a - m) + exp(b - m))
}

# Target density (for plotting)
pi_density <- function(x) {
  0.35 * dnorm(x, -4, 1) + 0.65 * dnorm(x, 3, 0.7)
}

# Single MH step at temperature beta
tempered_step <- function(x, beta, step = 1.0) {
  x_prop <- x + rnorm(1, 0, step)
  loga <- beta * (log_pi(x_prop) - log_pi(x))
  if (log(runif(1)) < loga) x_prop else x
}




Show code
single_chain_mh <- function(n_iter = 20000, x0 = -4, step = 1.0) {
  x <- x0
  out <- numeric(n_iter)
  for (i in 1:n_iter) {
    x <- tempered_step(x, beta = 1.0, step = step)
    out[i] <- x
  }
  out
}

set.seed(1)
trace_single <- single_chain_mh(n_iter = 20000, x0 = -4, step = 1.2)




Show code
parallel_tempering <- function(n_iter = 20000,
                               betas = c(1.0, 0.65, 0.4, 0.2, 0.08),
                               step = 1.2) {
  L <- length(betas)
  x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = L)  # Initialize chains at different locations

  x_by_temp <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_iter, ncol = L)

  for (i in 1:n_iter) {
    # Within-temperature MH updates
    for (l in 1:L) {
      x[l] <- tempered_step(x[l], betas[l], step = step)
    }

    # Swap moves between neighbouring chains
    for (l in sample(1:(L - 1))) {
      loga <- (betas[l] - betas[l + 1]) * (log_pi(x[l + 1]) - log_pi(x[l]))
      if (log(runif(1)) < loga) {
        tmp <- x[l]
        x[l] <- x[l + 1]
        x[l + 1] <- tmp
      }
    }

    # Store state of cold chain (beta = 1)
    x_by_temp[i, ] <- x
  }

  list(x_by_temp = x_by_temp, betas = betas)
}

set.seed(1)
res_pt <- parallel_tempering(n_iter = 20000,
                             betas = c(1.0, 0.65, 0.4, 0.2, 0.08),
                             step = 1.2)




Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
plot(trace_single[1:5000], type = "l", lwd = 1.2,
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = expression(theta),
     main = "Single-chain MH trace (first 5000 iterations)",
     col = "black", ylim = c(-6, 4))
grid()








Figure 11.1: Single-chain MH on bimodal target: stays trapped near the left mode.







Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))
cold_trace <- res_pt$x_by_temp[1:5000, 1]
plot(cold_trace, type = "l", lwd = 1.2,
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = expression(theta),
     main = "Parallel tempering: cold chain trace (first 5000 iterations)",
     col = "black", ylim = c(-6, 4))
grid()








Figure 11.2: Parallel tempering: cold chain (β=1) explores both modes via swaps.







Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))

# Burn-in
burn <- 5000
x_single_burn <- trace_single[-(1:burn)]
x_pt_burn <- res_pt$x_by_temp[-(1:burn), 1]

# Plot true density
xx <- seq(-7, 5, length.out = 200)
plot(xx, pi_density(xx), type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "black",
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = "Density",
     main = "Posterior: true density vs. samples",
     ylim = c(0, max(pi_density(xx)) * 1.1))

# Histogram of single-chain samples
hist(x_single_burn, breaks = 60, freq = FALSE, col = rgb(0.8, 0.8, 0.8, 0.5),
     border = "gray40", add = TRUE)

# Histogram of PT samples
hist(x_pt_burn, breaks = 60, freq = FALSE, col = rgb(0.7, 0.7, 0.7, 0.3),
     border = "black", add = TRUE)

legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.75,
       legend = c("True density", "Single-chain MH samples", "PT cold chain samples"),
       lty = c(1, NA, NA), col = c("black", NA, NA),
       fill = c(NA, rgb(0.8, 0.8, 0.8, 0.5), rgb(0.7, 0.7, 0.7, 0.3)),
       border = c(NA, "gray40", "black"))








Figure 11.3: Posterior samples: single-chain MH misses the left mode, PT captures both.







Show code
par(mar = c(4.4, 4.4, 2.2, 1.2))

betas_plot <- c(1.0, 0.65, 0.4, 0.15)
xx <- seq(-7, 5, length.out = 200)
colors_seq <- c("black", "gray40", "gray60", "gray70")
ltys <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)

plot(xx, pi_density(xx)^betas_plot[1], type = "l", lwd = 1.2, col = colors_seq[1],
     lty = ltys[1], xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(pi(theta)^beta),
     main = "Effect of inverse temperature on posterior shape",
     ylim = c(0, max(pi_density(xx)^betas_plot[1]) * 1.05))

for (i in 2:length(betas_plot)) {
  lines(xx, pi_density(xx)^betas_plot[i], lwd = 1.2, col = colors_seq[i], lty = ltys[i])
}

legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.75,
       legend = paste0(expression(beta), " = ", betas_plot),
       col = colors_seq, lty = ltys, lwd = 1.2)








Figure 11.4: Tempered posteriors at different inverse temperatures, showing flattening at hot chains.





The figures above illustrate the fundamental problem and solution:

Figure 1 shows a single chain trapped in the left mode.
Figure 2 shows the parallel tempering cold chain (at \(\beta = 1\)) successfully switching between both modes.
Figure 3 shows the histogram of samples: single-chain MH misses the left mode entirely, while PT recovers the true bimodal structure.
Figure 4 visualizes how higher temperature (\(\beta \to 0\)) flattens the posterior, enabling free movement.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.7 Practical considerations
Choosing whether to deploy parallel tempering — and what to compare it against — comes down to the question being asked and the resources available:

Use parallel tempering when the posterior is multimodal with well-separated modes, standard MCMC chains get trapped, the interest is in accurate posterior sampling and uncertainty quantification, or the problem involves phase transitions or critical phenomena (common in physics).
Use simulated annealing when the goal is to find the MAP estimate or a point estimate, optimization (not sampling) is the primary objective, and you need fast identification of the best fit, not uncertainty.
Prefer HMC/NUTS when the posterior is unimodal and high-dimensional, gradient information is available, or computational resources are limited and you cannot afford many chains. HMC and NUTS excel in high dimensions because they use gradient information to make large, informed moves, but they fail on multimodal targets because a single trajectory cannot jump between modes.
Computational cost. Running \(K\) chains costs roughly \(K\) times as much as a single chain. However, this is often money well spent: the mixing time of the cold chain typically decreases dramatically when the temperature ladder is well-designed.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.8 Summary

Tempered distributions scale the exponent of the posterior: \(\pi_\beta(\theta) \propto \pi(\theta)^\beta\). At \(\beta = 1\) we recover the target; at \(\beta \to 0\) the distribution flattens.

Simulated annealing is a single-chain method with a time-varying cooling schedule; it targets optimization (MAP estimation) and asymptotically concentrates on the global maximum.
Parallel tempering (replica exchange MCMC) runs \(K\) chains at fixed inverse temperatures \(1 = \beta_1 > \beta_2 > \cdots > \beta_K\) and periodically swaps states between neighbours. The cold chain remains on the target distribution throughout and escapes local modes via information shared from hotter chains.
Detailed balance is satisfied: within-temperature moves are standard MH, and swap moves satisfy detailed balance with respect to the product target \(\Pi(\Theta) = \prod_{l=1}^K \pi_{\beta_l}(\theta_l)\). Thus the cold chain’s marginal is exactly \(\pi\).
Temperature ladder design is critical: geometric spacing in inverse temperature and target swap acceptance rates of 20–40% are practical guidelines.
The worked example shows that parallel tempering succeeds where single-chain MH fails on a bimodal target, recovering the true posterior over both modes.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.9 Exercises

Define the tempered distribution \(\pi_\beta(x) \propto \pi(x)^\beta\) for \(\beta \in [0, 1]\). Identify the two limiting cases \(\beta = 0\) and \(\beta = 1\).


Solution. At \(\beta = 1\) we recover the target, \[\pi_1(x) \propto \pi(x),\] while at \(\beta = 0\) we have \(\pi(x)^0 = 1\), so \[\pi_0(x) \propto 1,\] i.e. the uniform (or prior, in a Bayesian setting) reference distribution on the support of \(\pi\).


Let \(\pi(x) \propto \exp(-U(x))\). Show that \(\pi_\beta(x) \propto \exp(-\beta U(x))\). Explain why lowering \(\beta\) flattens the landscape and lowers energy barriers.


Solution. Raising the density to the power \(\beta\) gives \[\pi_\beta(x) \propto \pi(x)^\beta = \big[\exp(-U(x))\big]^\beta = \exp(-\beta U(x)).\] The effective energy becomes \(\beta U(x)\), so all energy differences (including barrier heights \(\Delta U\)) are rescaled by \(\beta\). For \(\beta < 1\) this shrinks the contrast between modes and valleys, so the chain crosses barriers with probability \(\sim e^{-\beta\Delta U}\) instead of \(e^{-\Delta U}\).


Bimodal target. Consider \(\pi(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathcal{N}(-3, 1) + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathcal{N}(3, 1)\). Sketch (qualitatively) the tempered density \(\pi_\beta\) for \(\beta = 1, 0.5, 0.1\) and explain why a random-walk Metropolis chain on \(\pi_1\) has trouble crossing the mode gap.


Solution. At \(\beta = 1\) the density has two sharp peaks at \(\pm 3\) separated by a deep valley near \(0\); at \(\beta = 0.5\) the peaks broaden and the valley between them rises; at \(\beta = 0.1\) the density is nearly flat across \([-5,5]\). Random-walk MH must traverse the low-density gap by a sequence of small steps, each accepted with probability \(\sim e^{-\Delta U}\) where \(\Delta U \approx U(0) - U(\pm 3) \approx \tfrac{9}{2}\), giving an exponentially small per-step crossing probability and effective trapping in one mode.


Swap acceptance probability. For replicas at inverse temperatures \(\beta_i\) and \(\beta_j\) holding states \(x_i, x_j\), derive the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance probability \[\alpha_{ij} = \min\!\left\{1,\ \exp\!\big[(\beta_i - \beta_j)(U(x_i) - U(x_j))\big]\right\}.\]


Solution. The product target is \(\Pi(x_i, x_j) \propto e^{-\beta_i U(x_i) - \beta_j U(x_j)}\), and the swap proposal \((x_i, x_j) \to (x_j, x_i)\) is deterministic and symmetric. The MH ratio is therefore \[\frac{\Pi(x_j, x_i)}{\Pi(x_i, x_j)} = \frac{e^{-\beta_i U(x_j) - \beta_j U(x_i)}}{e^{-\beta_i U(x_i) - \beta_j U(x_j)}} = \exp\!\big[(\beta_i - \beta_j)(U(x_i) - U(x_j))\big],\] so \(\alpha_{ij} = \min\{1, \exp[(\beta_i - \beta_j)(U(x_i) - U(x_j))]\}\).


Show that the swap move described above satisfies detailed balance with respect to the product target \(\Pi(x_1, \ldots, x_K) = \prod_l \pi_{\beta_l}(x_l)\). Use this to argue that the marginal of the cold chain is exactly \(\pi\).


Solution. For the swap of replicas \(i, j\) with symmetric proposal probability \(q\), the MH rule gives \[\Pi(\ldots, x_i, \ldots, x_j, \ldots)\, q\, \alpha_{ij} = \Pi(\ldots, x_j, \ldots, x_i, \ldots)\, q\, \alpha_{ji},\] which is exactly the standard MH detailed balance identity using \(\alpha_{ij} = \min\{1, \Pi'/\Pi\}\) (all factors at other replicas cancel). Since both within-temperature moves and swaps preserve \(\Pi\), the stationary distribution is \(\Pi\), and the marginal over \(x_1\) at \(\beta_1 = 1\) is \(\pi_{\beta_1} = \pi\).


Geometric ladder. Show that with a geometric temperature schedule \(\beta_{l+1} / \beta_l = r\) (constant), the expected swap acceptance between adjacent replicas becomes approximately independent of \(l\) when the energy variance scales linearly with the dimension.


Solution. The log swap ratio between replicas \(l, l+1\) is \(\Delta_l = (\beta_l - \beta_{l+1})(U_l - U_{l+1}) = \beta_l(1-r)\,(U_l - U_{l+1})\). Standard scaling arguments give \(\mathrm{Var}_{\pi_\beta}[U] \approx d\, c(\beta)/\beta^2\) for some smooth \(c(\beta)\), so \[\mathrm{Var}(\Delta_l) \approx \beta_l^2 (1-r)^2 \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{d\, c(\beta_l)}{\beta_l^2} = 2 d\, c(\beta_l)\,(1-r)^2,\] which is independent of \(l\) up to the slow variation of \(c(\beta)\). The expected acceptance, governed by the distribution of \(\Delta_l\), is therefore nearly constant along the ladder.


Number of replicas. For a target in \(d\) dimensions with average energy gap \(\Delta U\) between cold and hot ends, estimate the number of replicas \(K\) needed to keep adjacent-pair swap acceptance around \(25\%\). Express your answer in terms of \(\Delta U\) and the desired swap rate.


Solution. For a target acceptance \(a\), each adjacent pair should produce a typical log-ratio of order \(|\log a|\), i.e. \((\beta_l - \beta_{l+1})\,\Delta U_{\text{adj}} \sim |\log a|\), where the per-pair energy gap on a geometric ladder is \(\Delta U_{\text{adj}} \approx \Delta U / (K-1)\). Setting \((1)(\Delta U / (K-1)) \approx |\log a|\) gives \[K \approx 1 + \frac{\Delta U}{|\log a|},\] so for \(a \approx 0.25\), \(|\log a| \approx 1.4\) and \(K \sim 1 + \Delta U / 1.4\). (For a target whose energy variance grows like \(d\), the same argument with \(\sigma_U \sim \sqrt{d}\) rescales this to \(K \sim 1 + \sqrt{d}\,\Delta U / |\log a|\).)


Simulated annealing vs parallel tempering. Compare the two algorithms in two sentences each: what each one tries to achieve, and why PT generates valid posterior samples while annealing typically does not.


Solution. Simulated annealing runs a single chain whose temperature \(T_k \to 0\) on a schedule; it is an optimisation method that concentrates on \(\arg\max \pi\), and the resulting trajectory has a non-stationary, time-inhomogeneous law so its iterates are not samples from any fixed distribution. Parallel tempering, by contrast, runs \(K\) chains at fixed inverse temperatures with \(\beta_1 = 1\) and swaps states between neighbours; the joint stationary distribution is \(\Pi = \prod_l \pi_{\beta_l}\), so the cold chain marginal is exactly \(\pi\) and its iterates are valid posterior samples.


Thermodynamic integration. Show that \[\log Z = -\int_0^1 E_{\pi_\beta}[U]\,d\beta,\] and explain how this identity allows the marginal likelihood (evidence) to be estimated by sampling at multiple temperatures.


Solution. Define \(Z(\beta) = \int e^{-\beta U(x)}\,dx\), with \(Z(0) = |\mathcal{X}|\) (or \(1\) for a normalised reference) and \(Z(1) = Z\). Differentiating under the integral, \[\frac{d \log Z(\beta)}{d\beta} = \frac{-\int U(x) e^{-\beta U(x)}\,dx}{Z(\beta)} = -E_{\pi_\beta}[U],\] so integrating from \(0\) to \(1\) gives \(\log Z = \log Z(1) - \log Z(0) = -\int_0^1 E_{\pi_\beta}[U]\,d\beta\) (taking \(Z(0)=1\)). In practice one estimates \(\hat U(\beta_l) \approx E_{\pi_{\beta_l}}[U]\) from the replica at \(\beta_l\) and applies a quadrature rule (trapezoid/Simpson) over the temperature ladder.


Optimal acceptance rate for swap moves. Discuss why empirical guidance places the optimal swap acceptance in the range \(20\)–\(40\%\). What goes wrong below and above this range?


Solution. The swap rate measures the rate at which information (replicas) flows along the ladder. Below \(\sim 20\%\) the energy histograms of adjacent replicas barely overlap, swaps are rare and the cold chain receives very few hot discoveries (information bottleneck). Above \(\sim 40\%\) adjacent temperatures are nearly identical: each accepted swap conveys little new information per chain and one is paying for redundant replicas, so the wall-clock cost per ESS rises. The empirical sweet spot \(20\)–\(40\%\) balances “swaps happen often enough” against “each swap actually changes the energy”.


Effective sample size after a PT run. With \(K\) replicas, only the cold chain produces posterior samples. Explain why the total wall-clock cost scales with \(K\) but the ESS only grows by the swap mixing improvement, and why the trade-off is still typically favourable for multimodal targets.


Solution. Each PT iteration costs \(\sim K\) likelihood evaluations (one per replica) but only the \(\beta_1\) chain contributes to the posterior estimate, so cost-per-cold-iterate scales as \(K\). The benefit is a reduction of the cold-chain integrated autocorrelation \(\tau\): roughly \(\mathrm{ESS} \sim N / \tau_{\text{PT}}\) vs. \(N / \tau_{\text{single}}\). For unimodal targets \(\tau_{\text{PT}} \approx \tau_{\text{single}}\) and the factor \(K\) is pure overhead, but for multimodal targets a single chain has \(\tau_{\text{single}} \sim e^{\Delta U}\) (exponential in barrier height) while PT gives \(\tau_{\text{PT}} \sim K^2\) (polynomial round-trip time), so the speedup \(\tau_{\text{single}}/(K\,\tau_{\text{PT}}) \sim e^{\Delta U}/K^3\) is enormous.


Energy histogram overlap. Show qualitatively that adjacent replicas must have overlapping energy distributions for swaps to be accepted with reasonable probability. Sketch the case of (a) too few replicas and (b) too many.


Solution. 
The swap log-ratio is \((\beta_l - \beta_{l+1})(U_l - U_{l+1})\), which is appreciable only when \(U_l\) and \(U_{l+1}\) are drawn from overlapping distributions; if the histograms of \(U\) at \(\beta_l\) and \(\beta_{l+1}\) are disjoint then \(U_l - U_{l+1}\) is systematically large and negative, making \(\alpha \approx 0\).
Good design has adjacent histograms whose tails overlap noticeably but whose means differ.


Replica exchange for a discrete target. A finite chain has stationary distribution \(\pi\) on \(\{0, 1, \ldots, K-1\}\). Construct the tempered transition kernel at inverse temperature \(\beta\) and write the swap acceptance with a neighbour at \(\beta'\).


Solution. Write \(\pi(x) \propto e^{-U(x)}\) with \(U(x) = -\log \pi(x)\), and define the tempered target on the same state space by \[\pi_\beta(x) \propto \pi(x)^\beta = e^{-\beta U(x)}.\] A simple within-temperature kernel is Metropolis with a symmetric neighbour proposal \(q(x \to y)\) accepted with probability \[\alpha_\beta(x \to y) = \min\!\left\{1, \left(\frac{\pi(y)}{\pi(x)}\right)^{\beta}\right\}.\] The swap with a neighbour at \(\beta'\) holding state \(x'\) uses \[\alpha_{\text{swap}}(x, x') = \min\!\left\{1, \exp\!\big[(\beta - \beta')(U(x) - U(x'))\big]\right\}.\]


Power-posterior interpretation. In Bayesian inference \(\pi(\theta \mid D) \propto L(\theta)^1 p(\theta)\). State the corresponding tempered version \(\pi_\beta(\theta \mid D)\) and identify the prior, the maximum-likelihood-dominated posterior, and the standard posterior as special cases.


Solution. The power posterior is obtained by raising the likelihood to \(\beta\): \[\pi_\beta(\theta \mid D) \propto L(\theta)^\beta\, p(\theta).\] Then \(\beta = 0\) gives \(\pi_0 \propto p(\theta)\) (the prior); \(\beta = 1\) gives \(\pi_1 \propto L(\theta) p(\theta)\) (the standard posterior); and \(\beta \to \infty\) concentrates on \(\arg\max_\theta L(\theta)\), the MLE/likelihood-dominated limit. Intermediate \(\beta \in (0, 1)\) interpolates between prior and posterior and underlies the thermodynamic-integration estimate of the evidence \(\log Z = \int_0^1 E_{\pi_\beta}[\log L]\,d\beta\).


When parallel tempering is overkill. Give two situations where PT is not worth the extra computational cost (i.e. a single-chain HMC or random-walk Metropolis is preferable).


Solution. 

Unimodal, well-conditioned targets in moderate-to-high dimension. Here a single HMC/NUTS chain already mixes in \(O(1)\) effective steps; the \(K\times\) overhead of PT buys essentially no extra ESS. (ii) Tight computational budget for a quick exploratory fit, especially when the posterior is unimodal or only mildly multimodal — running \(K\) replicas multiplies the per-iteration cost while a single tuned chain (perhaps with multiple random restarts) gives faster posterior estimates with adequate diagnostics.

title: 11  Parallel tempering
text:
11.10 Sources

Falasco, G. Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Monte Carlo methods and annealing for multimodal targets. Lecture slides, May 8, 2026.
Robert, C. P. and Casella, G. Monte Carlo Statistical Methods. Springer, 2nd ed., 2004. Chapter 10–11 (tempering, reversible jump, Gibbs sampling).
Geyer, C. J. (1991). Markov chain Monte Carlo maximum likelihood. In Proceedings of the 23rd Symposium on the Interface, Fairfax Station, VA.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.1 Introduction
Regression analysis seeks to learn a functional relationship between input variables \(x\) and observed response variables \(y\) from data. In the Bayesian framework, we construct a probabilistic model that combines a generative (noise-free) function \(f(x; \theta)\) with a noise model, then use Bayes’ theorem to infer the posterior distribution \(p(\theta \mid D)\) of the parameters. For regression models where the parameters enter linearly, exact posterior inference is tractable via conjugate analysis. For nonlinear models or non-Gaussian likelihoods, we rely on Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling. This chapter develops both the theory and practice of Bayesian regression: from the closed-form solution for linear Gaussian regression to the complete MCMC workflow for fitting arbitrary nonlinear regression models and making probabilistic predictions.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.2 Regression Model: Signal Plus Noise
The basic regression model specifies that each observed response \(y_i\) arises from a noise-free mean function plus random noise:
\[y_i = f(x_i; \theta) + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2).\]

Model ingredients. \(x_i\) is the regressor (input, explanatory variable), assumed fixed and measured without error; \(y_i\) is the response (dependent, outcome variable); \(f(x_i; \theta)\) is the signal—the deterministic part; and \(\varepsilon_i\) is measurement noise or intrinsic scatter, i.i.d. Gaussian with variance \(\sigma^2\).
Gaussian likelihood. Each observation has conditional distribution \(p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \mathcal{N}(f(x_i; \theta), \sigma^2)\), so for \(N\) independent observations \(D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^N\) the joint likelihood factorizes as \(p(D \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^N \mathcal{N}(f(x_i; \theta), \sigma^2)\).
Log-likelihood. Taking logarithms, \[\log p(D \mid \theta) = -N \log\sigma - \frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^N [y_i - f(x_i; \theta)]^2 + \text{const}.\]

title: 12  Bayesian regression
section: 12.3 Linear Models and the Design Matrix
text:
12.3 Linear Models and the Design Matrix
A regression model is linear if the parameters enter linearly, even if the features depend nonlinearly on the inputs:
\[y_i = \boldsymbol{\phi}(x_i)^\top \mathbf{w} + \varepsilon_i,\]
where \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = [\phi_1(x), \ldots, \phi_M(x)]^\top\) is a feature map and \(\mathbf{w} = [w_0, \ldots, w_{M-1}]^\top\) is the weight vector.

Examples of linear regression models: - Straight line: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = (1, x)\), \(f(x) = w_0 + w_1 x\). - Polynomial: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = (1, x, x^2, \ldots, x^{M-1})\), \(f(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{M-1} w_j x^j\). - Fourier: \(\boldsymbol{\phi}(x) = (1, \sin(x), \cos(x), \ldots)\). - Linear in unknown parameters: If \(f(x) = w_0 e^{-\lambda x}\) with \(\lambda\) known, it is linear in \(w_0\). - Not linear in unknown parameters: \(f(x) = w_0 e^{-\lambda x}\) if \(\lambda\) is unknown (exponential, logistic, etc.).

For \(N\) data points and \(M\) basis functions, define the \(N \times M\) design matrix (or regressor matrix) \[\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix}
\phi_1(x_1) & \phi_2(x_1) & \cdots & \phi_M(x_1) \\
\phi_1(x_2) & \phi_2(x_2) & \cdots & \phi_M(x_2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\phi_1(x_N) & \phi_2(x_N) & \cdots & \phi_M(x_N)
\end{bmatrix}.\]
The linear model becomes \[\mathbf{y} = \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_N),\]
and the likelihood is \[p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_N).\]

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.4 Bayesian Linear Regression: Closed-Form Solution
Assume \(\sigma^2\) is known. Choose a Gaussian prior on \(\mathbf{w}\): \[p(\mathbf{w}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{m}_0, \mathbf{S}_0).\]
The likelihood is Gaussian in \(\mathbf{w}\); by conjugacy, the posterior is also Gaussian:

Bayesian Linear Regression with Gaussian Prior:
Given likelihood \(p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_N)\) and prior \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{m}_0, \mathbf{S}_0)\), the posterior is \[p(\mathbf{w} \mid D, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{m}_N, \mathbf{S}_N),\]
where \[\begin{align}
\mathbf{S}_N^{-1} &= \mathbf{S}_0^{-1} + \sigma^{-2} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \\
\mathbf{m}_N &= \mathbf{S}_N \left( \mathbf{S}_0^{-1} \mathbf{m}_0 + \sigma^{-2} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \mathbf{y} \right).
\end{align}\]


Interpretation. Precisions add. The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and the data-driven estimate. The posterior covariance \(\mathbf{S}_N\) shrinks relative to \(\mathbf{S}_0\).
Weak isotropic prior. A common choice is \(\mathbf{m}_0 = \mathbf{0}\) and \(\mathbf{S}_0 = \sigma_0^2 \mathbf{I}_M\), giving \(\mathbf{S}_N^{-1} = \sigma_0^{-2} \mathbf{I}_M + \sigma^{-2} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\Phi}\) and \(\mathbf{m}_N = \sigma^{-2} \mathbf{S}_N \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \mathbf{y}\).
Connection to ridge regression. Ordinary least squares minimizes \(\|\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}\|^2\); ridge regression adds an L2 penalty \(L_{\text{ridge}} = \|\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi} \mathbf{w}\|^2 + \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|^2\), with estimator \(\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\text{ridge}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \boldsymbol{\Phi} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}^\top \mathbf{y}\). Ridge regression is maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation under a Gaussian prior: the prior variance relates to the penalty by \(\lambda = \sigma^2 / \sigma_0^2\). The Bayesian approach goes further by quantifying posterior uncertainty via the full covariance \(\mathbf{S}_N\).

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.5 Posterior Predictive Distribution
For a new input $x_* $, we wish to predict \(y_*\). The posterior predictive distribution marginalizes over parameter uncertainty: \[p(y_* \mid x_*, D, \sigma^2) = \int p(y_* \mid x_*, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2) \, p(\mathbf{w} \mid D, \sigma^2) \, d\mathbf{w}.\]
Since both terms are Gaussian, the integral is tractable:

Posterior Predictive Distribution:
Let \(\boldsymbol{\phi}_* = \boldsymbol{\phi}(x_*)\). Then \[p(y_* \mid x_*, D, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}\left( y_* \,\big|\, \boldsymbol{\phi}_*^\top \mathbf{m}_N, \, \sigma^2 + \boldsymbol{\phi}_*^\top \mathbf{S}_N \boldsymbol{\phi}_* \right).\]


Observation noise. \(\sigma^2\) (irreducible error in \(y_*\)).
Parameter uncertainty. \(\boldsymbol{\phi}_*^\top \mathbf{S}_N \boldsymbol{\phi}_*\) (uncertainty in the regression function \(f(x_*)\)). The posterior predictive band widens away from the data due to increasing parameter uncertainty.


In Bayesian linear regression, assume \[y_* \mid \mathbf w, x_* \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf w,\,\sigma^2), \qquad \mathbf w \mid D \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf m,\,\mathbf S).\] Derive the posterior predictive distribution of \(y_*\).
Solution. Linearity of expectation gives \[E[y_* \mid D] = E[\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf w \mid D] = \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf m.\] The law of total variance splits the predictive variance into a noise term and a parameter term: \[\text{Var}(y_* \mid D) = E\!\left[\text{Var}(y_* \mid \mathbf w, D)\right] + \text{Var}\!\left(E[y_* \mid \mathbf w, D]\right) = \sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S\,\boldsymbol\phi_*.\] Since both contributions are Gaussian and we are linearly combining Gaussians, the predictive distribution is itself Gaussian: \[y_* \mid D, x_* \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf m,\ \sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S\,\boldsymbol\phi_*\right).\] The first variance term is irreducible noise; the second is parameter uncertainty.


In Bayesian linear regression, define the posterior predictive distribution and the predictive (e.g. \(95\%\)) credible interval for a future observation \(y_*\). What is its physical meaning, and how does it differ from a credible interval for the regression curve?
Solution. The posterior predictive distribution for a future observation \(y_*\) at input \(x_*\) is \[p(y_* \mid x_*, D) = \int p(y_* \mid x_*, \boldsymbol\theta)\,p(\boldsymbol\theta \mid D)\,d\boldsymbol\theta.\] Prediction is the likelihood of the future observation averaged over posterior uncertainty in the parameters.
A central (equal-tailed) \(95\%\) posterior-predictive credible interval is \(C_{0.95}(x_*) = [q_{0.025}(x_*),\,q_{0.975}(x_*)]\), where \(q_\alpha(x_*)\) satisfies \(\int_{-\infty}^{q_\alpha(x_*)} p(y_* \mid x_*, D)\,dy_* = \alpha\). Conditional on the model, prior, and data, a future measurement at \(x_*\) lies in \(C_{0.95}(x_*)\) with posterior predictive probability \(95\%\).
The credible interval for the regression function concerns the latent response \(f(x_*, \boldsymbol\theta)\) and is obtained from \[p(f \mid x_*, D) = \int \delta\!\big(f - f(x_*, \boldsymbol\theta)\big)\,p(\boldsymbol\theta \mid D)\,d\boldsymbol\theta.\] This describes uncertainty about the underlying curve, not the scatter of future observations around it. Consequently the posterior predictive interval is generally wider than the regression-function interval — it adds the irreducible noise variance to the parameter uncertainty.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.6 Unknown Noise Level
In practice, \(\sigma^2\) is usually unknown and inferred from data. The parameters become \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{w}, \sigma)\), and we use a prior on the noise scale. A standard choice is the Jeffreys prior for scale parameters: \[p(\sigma) \propto \frac{1}{\sigma}, \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p(\log \sigma) \text{ is uniform}.\]
The joint posterior is \[p(\mathbf{w}, \sigma \mid D) \propto p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\Phi}, \mathbf{w}, \sigma) \, p(\mathbf{w}) \, p(\sigma).\]
When both \(\mathbf{w}\) and \(\sigma\) are unknown, the posterior no longer has a closed form in closed-form distributions, but conjugate analysis can still apply to marginal posteriors or conditional distributions. For computational convenience and to make use of existing MCMC software, we often resort to sampling.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.7 Nonlinear Regression and MCMC
A model is nonlinear in parameters if \(f(x; \theta)\) is a nonlinear function of some \(\theta\). Typical examples are the exponential \(f(x) = a e^{b x}\) (nonlinear in both \(a\) and \(b\) unless one is known), the logistic \(f(x) = L / (1 + e^{-k(x - x_0)})\) (nonlinear in location \(x_0\), scale \(k\), amplitude \(L\)), and the power law \(f(x) = a x^b\) (nonlinear in exponent \(b\)). For such models, the likelihood is no longer Gaussian in the parameters, and conjugate priors do not yield closed-form posteriors. Similarly, generalized linear models (logistic regression for binary data, Poisson regression for counts) have non-Gaussian likelihoods, precluding analytical solutions. In these cases, we must either use approximations (Laplace, variational inference) or use MCMC or other sampling methods to draw from \(p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) \propto p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta})\). The next section lays out the standard MCMC workflow; the Laplace approximation is treated alongside the worked example below.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.8 Practical MCMC Fitting Workflow
The following seven-step workflow is the standard approach for fitting regression models via MCMC.

Step 1: specify the generative and noise model. Write down the regression function \(f(x; \boldsymbol{\theta})\), the noise distribution (e.g., \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\)), and the resulting observation model \(y_i \sim \mathcal{N}(f(x_i; \boldsymbol{\theta}), \sigma^2)\).
Step 2: choose priors for all parameters. Specify weakly informative priors for each parameter. For regression coefficients, use broad Gaussians (e.g., \(\mathcal{N}(0, 10^2)\)). For scale parameters like \(\sigma\), use a Jeffreys prior \(p(\sigma) \propto 1/\sigma\) or truncated exponentials. To avoid boundary issues, sample on the log-scale: \(\eta = \log \sigma\).


Reparameterization for Bounded Parameters:
Parameters constrained to positive values (like \(\sigma > 0\)) should be sampled in transformed space: - Replace \(\sigma\) with \(\eta = \log \sigma\), so \(\sigma = e^\eta\). - A flat prior on \(\eta\) corresponds to a Jeffreys prior on \(\sigma\). - The Jacobian \(|d\sigma / d\eta| = e^\eta = \sigma\) automatically cancels the \(1/\sigma\) prior, yielding a uniform prior in log-space.
For slope parameters, reparameterize via angle: \(\beta = \tan(\alpha)\), so all slopes are finite and comparable.


Step 3: write log-prior, log-likelihood, and log-posterior functions. Define functions that compute \[\begin{align}
\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &: \text{ sum of log-prior densities for all parameters}, \\
\log p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) &: \text{ log-likelihood, typically } \sum_{i=1}^N \log p(y_i \mid x_i, \boldsymbol{\theta}), \\
\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) &= \log p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) + \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \text{const}.
\end{align}\] For MCMC, we only need the unnormalized posterior (up to an additive constant).
Step 4: run MCMC and tune the proposal covariance. Initialize parameters at a reasonable starting point (e.g., a classical fit or MAP estimate). Specify a proposal distribution, typically a multivariate Gaussian with tuned covariance. Run MCMC (e.g., random-walk Metropolis-Hastings, or preferably NUTS via Stan) for a burn-in period, then for additional sampling iterations. Tune the proposal variance to achieve a target acceptance rate (typically 20–30% for random-walk MH, automatic for NUTS).
Step 5: diagnose and inspect the chain. Use trace plots, autocorrelation, effective sample size, and \(\hat R\) as detailed in the box below.


Remark. Key Diagnostics: - Trace plots: Plot \(\theta_j\) versus iteration. Traces should resemble white noise (good mixing), not slow drifts or stuck chains. - Autocorrelation function (ACF): Compute \(\text{Corr}(\theta_j^{(k)}, \theta_j^{(k+\ell)})\) as a function of lag \(\ell\). Rapid decay (small effective sample size if slow decay) indicates good mixing. - Effective sample size (ESS): \(\text{ESS} = N_{\text{samples}} / (1 + 2 \sum_{\ell=1}^\infty \text{Corr}(\ell))\). Rule of thumb: aim for ESS \(> 1000\) per parameter. - \(\hat{R}\) (Gelman-Rubin statistic): Ratio of between-chain to within-chain variance. \(\hat{R} < 1.01\) indicates convergence; \(\hat{R} > 1.05\) indicates poor mixing or insufficient burn-in. - Joint posterior pairs plot: Scatter plot of all pairs of parameters. Reveals correlations and identifies poorly identified parameters.


Step 6: summarize parameters. Compute posterior summaries from the converged samples (after thinning to reduce autocorrelation if desired): MAP (the sample with highest posterior density), posterior mean (average of all samples), posterior covariance (empirical covariance of samples), and credible intervals (2.5th and 97.5th quantiles, or other quantiles, per parameter). Always transform samples back to the original parameterization before computing summaries.
Step 7: plot predictions and posterior predictive checks. Show the data with error bars \((x_i, y_i)\) at \(\pm \sigma_{\text{MAP}}\) or \(\pm\) posterior SD of \(y_i\); overlay the fitted regression curve \(\hat{f}(x) = \boldsymbol{\phi}(x)^\top \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w} \mid D]\); add a credible band for the mean curve by computing posterior quantiles of \(f(x; \boldsymbol{\theta})\) on a grid of \(x\) values from each posterior sample (this shows uncertainty in the regression function, not noise); and add a posterior predictive band that includes both parameter uncertainty and noise — for each \(x\) on the grid and each posterior sample \(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\), generate \(y^{(k)} \sim \mathcal{N}(f(x; \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}), \sigma^{(k)2})\) and take the 2.5th and 97.5th quantiles across samples.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.9 Example: Linear Regression with MCMC
Consider fitting a straight line \(f(x) = b_0 + b_1 x\) to observed data \(\{(x_i, y_i)\}\), with unknown noise \(\sigma\).
Parameters (transformed): \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (b_0, \alpha, \eta)\) where \(b_1 = \tan(\alpha)\) and \(\eta = \log \sigma\).
Priors: - \(b_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 10^2)\). - \(\alpha \sim \text{Uniform}(-\pi/2, \pi/2)\) (equivalently, \(p(\alpha) \propto 1\)). - \(\eta \sim \text{Uniform}\) (flat prior on \(\log \sigma\), Jeffreys prior on \(\sigma\)).
Log-likelihood: \[\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid b_0, \alpha, \eta) = -N \eta - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{[y_i - (b_0 + \tan(\alpha) x_i)]^2}{e^{2\eta}} + \text{const}.\]
Log-posterior: Sum of log-likelihood and log-prior densities.
Run MCMC (e.g., via random-walk MH or Stan’s NUTS sampler) and diagnose convergence.


Show code
set.seed(42)
N <- 8
x <- seq(0, 7, length.out = N)
sigma_true <- 1.0
y <- 0 + 1 * x + rnorm(N, 0, sigma_true)

plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "black", xlab = "x", ylab = "y",
     xlim = c(-0.5, 7.5), ylim = c(-2, 9),
     main = "Linear Regression Data")





Linear regression example: 8 data points from y = 0 + x + noise.




Now run the MCMC sampler (pseudocode; in practice, use Stan, JAGS, or a hand-coded sampler):


Show code
# Log-likelihood for y_i ~ N(b0 + b1 * x_i, sigma^2)
log_likelihood <- function(b0, b1, sigma, y, x) {
  mu <- b0 + b1 * x
  sum(dnorm(y, mu, sigma, log = TRUE))
}

# Log-posterior (unnormalized)
log_posterior <- function(theta, data) {
  b0 <- theta[1]
  alpha <- theta[2]
  eta <- theta[3]

  b1 <- tan(alpha)
  sigma <- exp(eta)

  # Priors
  log_prior_b0 <- dnorm(b0, 0, 10, log = TRUE)
  log_prior_alpha <- 0  # Flat in alpha
  log_prior_eta <- 0    # Flat in eta (Jeffreys for sigma)

  # Likelihood
  log_like <- log_likelihood(b0, b1, sigma, data$y, data$x)

  log_like + log_prior_b0 + log_prior_alpha + log_prior_eta
}


After running MCMC for 50,000 iterations (with 10,000 burn-in), extract the posterior samples and compute diagnostics:


Show code
# This is pseudocode; actual implementation depends on your MCMC sampler.
# Assume 'samples' is a matrix with columns: b0, alpha, eta

# Trace plot
par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(3.5, 3.5, 1, 1))
plot(samples[, 1], type = "l", xlab = "iteration", ylab = "b0",
     col = "black", lwd = 0.5)
plot(samples[, 2], type = "l", xlab = "iteration", ylab = "alpha",
     col = "black", lwd = 0.5)
plot(samples[, 3], type = "l", xlab = "iteration", ylab = "log(sigma)",
     col = "black", lwd = 0.5)


After burn-in, summarize the posterior:


Show code
# Transform back to original scale
b0_samp <- samples[, 1]
b1_samp <- tan(samples[, 2])
sigma_samp <- exp(samples[, 3])

# Posterior means and credible intervals
cat("Posterior summaries:\n")
cat("b0:", mean(b0_samp), "± [", quantile(b0_samp, 0.025), ",",
    quantile(b0_samp, 0.975), "]\n")
cat("b1:", mean(b1_samp), "± [", quantile(b1_samp, 0.025), ",",
    quantile(b1_samp, 0.975), "]\n")
cat("sigma:", mean(sigma_samp), "± [", quantile(sigma_samp, 0.025), ",",
    quantile(sigma_samp, 0.975), "]\n")




Show code
# Prediction grid
x_grid <- seq(-0.5, 7.5, length.out = 200)

# Compute posterior mean and predictive curves
f_samples <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(x_grid), ncol = length(b0_samp))
y_pred_samples <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(x_grid), ncol = length(b0_samp))

for (k in seq_along(b0_samp)) {
  f_samples[, k] <- b0_samp[k] + b1_samp[k] * x_grid
  y_pred_samples[, k] <- f_samples[, k] + rnorm(length(x_grid), 0, sigma_samp[k])
}

# Compute quantiles
f_lo <- apply(f_samples, 1, quantile, 0.025)
f_hi <- apply(f_samples, 1, quantile, 0.975)
f_med <- apply(f_samples, 1, median)

y_lo <- apply(y_pred_samples, 1, quantile, 0.025)
y_hi <- apply(y_pred_samples, 1, quantile, 0.975)

# Plot
plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "black", xlab = "x", ylab = "y",
