     xlim = c(-0.5, 7.5), ylim = c(-4, 10),
     main = "Linear Regression Fit with Uncertainty Bands")

# Posterior predictive band (wider, includes noise)
polygon(c(x_grid, rev(x_grid)), c(y_lo, rev(y_hi)),
        col = rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.3), border = NA)

# Credible band for mean (narrower, only parameter uncertainty)
polygon(c(x_grid, rev(x_grid)), c(f_lo, rev(f_hi)),
        col = rgb(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.4), border = NA)

# Posterior mean fit
lines(x_grid, f_med, col = "black", lwd = 2)

# Legend
legend("topleft", c("Data", "Posterior mean", "Credible band", "Predictive band"),
       pch = c(19, NA, NA, NA), lty = c(NA, 1, NA, NA),
       col = c("black", "black", "gray30", "gray50"),
       fill = c(NA, NA, rgb(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.4), rgb(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.3)),
       border = NA)

title: 12  Bayesian regression
section: 12.10 Nonlinear Models: Laplace Approximation and MCMC
text:
12.10 Nonlinear Models: Laplace Approximation and MCMC
When \(f(x; \theta)\) is nonlinear in \(\theta\), the posterior cannot be written in closed form. Two main approaches:
Laplace Approximation: Expand the log-posterior around the MAP estimate \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) as a multivariate Gaussian: \[p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) \approx \mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}, [-\nabla^2 \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) |_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}]^{-1}).\]
This is fast and often accurate for well-behaved posteriors. For multimodal or heavy-tailed posteriors, it can fail.
MCMC: Sample directly from the posterior, handling arbitrary nonlinearity and non-Gaussian shapes. The disadvantage is computational cost; the advantage is generality and accuracy. Modern samplers like NUTS (in Stan) adapt the step size and use Hamiltonian dynamics, making MCMC scalable.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.11 Posterior Predictive Checks
A fundamental tool in Bayesian regression is posterior predictive checking: generate new data under the posterior and compare to observed data.

For a regression model, the posterior predictive distribution is \[p(y_{\text{new}} \mid x_{\text{new}}, D) = \int p(y_{\text{new}} \mid x_{\text{new}}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid D) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta}.\]
The marginal predictive distribution averages over parameter uncertainty, automatically accounting for both the fitted model and the noise in the data.
Posterior predictive checks: 1. For each posterior sample \(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\), generate \(y_{\text{rep}}^{(k)}(x_i) \sim p(y \mid x_i, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)})\) at each observed \(x_i\). 2. Compare the distribution of simulated \(y_{\text{rep}}\) to observed \(y\) using summary statistics (e.g., mean, variance, quantiles, or max absolute residual). 3. If the posterior predictive distribution is consistent with the observed data, the model is plausible.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.12 Robustness and Extensions

12.12.1 Student-\(t\) Noise for Robustness
Gaussian noise is sensitive to outliers. A robust alternative is Student-\(t\) noise: \[y_i \sim t_\nu(f(x_i; \theta), \sigma^2),\] where \(\nu\) controls tail heaviness (small \(\nu\) allows larger outliers). As \(\nu \to \infty\), recovers Gaussian. Often set \(\nu = 3\) or \(\nu = 4\) for robustness.
The likelihood becomes \[p(y_i \mid \ldots) \propto \left[ 1 + \frac{[y_i - f(x_i; \theta)]^2}{\nu \sigma^2} \right]^{-(\nu+1)/2},\] which is heavier-tailed than Gaussian. MCMC is needed to fit such models.


12.12.2 Errors in All Variables
If both \(x_i\) and \(y_i\) are measured with error, the problem becomes significantly harder: \[x_i = x_i^{\text{true}} + \delta_{x,i}, \quad y_i = f(x_i^{\text{true}}; \theta) + \delta_{y,i}.\]
The true \(x_i^{\text{true}}\) must be treated as latent variables, increasing dimensionality. Bayesian approaches handle this naturally via hierarchical modeling, but require additional priors and tuning.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.13 Summary
Bayesian regression provides a principled framework for learning functional relationships:

Linear models (parameters enter linearly) admit conjugate closed-form posteriors via Gaussian theory, enabling exact inference and efficient prediction.
Posterior prediction integrates over parameter uncertainty, yielding predictive distributions that account for both fitted function and observation noise.
Nonlinear and generalized models (nonlinear parameters, non-Gaussian likelihoods) require MCMC sampling or approximations.
Practical MCMC workflow (specify model, choose priors, write log-posterior, run sampler, diagnose, summarize, plot) is systematic and reproducible.
Posterior predictive checks verify model adequacy and reveal misfit.
Robustness (heavy-tailed noise) and latent variable models (measurement error) extend the framework to real data.

The same logic applies across simple linear regression, polynomial fits, nonlinear curves, and generalized linear models: only the parameterization and likelihood change; the Bayesian template—prior \(\times\) likelihood $/ $ evidence—remains constant.

title: 12  Bayesian regression
text:
12.14 Exercises

Conjugate posterior. Observe \(y \mid \mathbf w, x \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol\phi(x)^\top \mathbf w,\,\sigma^2)\) with known \(\sigma^2\) and prior \(\mathbf w \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf 0, \tau^2 \mathbf I)\). Derive the posterior parameters \((\mathbf m_N, \mathbf S_N)\) in terms of the design matrix \(\boldsymbol\Phi\) and the response vector \(\mathbf y\).


Solution. Completing the square in \(\mathbf w\) in \(\log p(\mathbf w\mid D) = -\tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|\mathbf y - \boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf w\|^2 - \tfrac{1}{2\tau^2}\|\mathbf w\|^2 + \text{const}\) gives a Gaussian posterior \(\mathcal N(\mathbf m_N, \mathbf S_N)\) with \[\mathbf S_N^{-1} = \tau^{-2}\mathbf I + \sigma^{-2}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi, \qquad \mathbf m_N = \mathbf S_N\,\sigma^{-2}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \mathbf y,\] which is the precision-form update with \(\mathbf S_0 = \tau^2 \mathbf I\) and \(\mathbf m_0 = \mathbf 0\).


Show that posterior precisions add: \(\mathbf S_N^{-1} = \mathbf S_0^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi\). Interpret each term.


Solution. The quadratic in \(\mathbf w\) in \(\log p(\mathbf w\mid D)\) is the sum of the prior quadratic \(-\tfrac12 \mathbf w^\top \mathbf S_0^{-1}\mathbf w\) and the likelihood quadratic \(-\tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\mathbf w^\top \boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi\,\mathbf w\), so by matching to \(-\tfrac12 \mathbf w^\top \mathbf S_N^{-1}\mathbf w\), \[\mathbf S_N^{-1} = \mathbf S_0^{-1} + \sigma^{-2}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi.\] The first term is prior information (precision from the prior); the second is data information (Fisher information per unit noise variance). Precisions, not variances, add.


Single-observation update. Take \(\sigma^2 = 1\), \(\mathbf w \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf 0, \mathbf I)\), \(\boldsymbol\phi = (1, 0)^\top\), observe \(y = 3\). Compute the posterior mean and covariance in closed form.


Solution. With \(\mathbf S_0 = \mathbf I\), \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\boldsymbol\Phi = \boldsymbol\phi\boldsymbol\phi^\top = \operatorname{diag}(1,0)\) and \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \mathbf y = 3\,\boldsymbol\phi = (3,0)^\top\), so \[\mathbf S_N^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf S_N = \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf m_N = \mathbf S_N\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3/2 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}.\] The first weight is pulled halfway to the data; the second remains at its prior because it is unobserved.


Predictive variance away from data. Show that the posterior predictive variance \(\sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\,\boldsymbol\phi_*\) grows when \(\boldsymbol\phi_*\) aligns with directions where \(\mathbf S_N\) has large eigenvalues. Connect this to “extrapolation uncertainty”.


Solution. Diagonalise \(\mathbf S_N = \sum_k \lambda_k \mathbf u_k \mathbf u_k^\top\); then \[\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\,\boldsymbol\phi_* = \sum_k \lambda_k (\mathbf u_k^\top \boldsymbol\phi_*)^2,\] which is dominated by the eigendirections of largest \(\lambda_k\) (least-constrained weight directions). Since \(\mathbf S_N^{-1} = \mathbf S_0^{-1} + \sigma^{-2}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi\), large \(\lambda_k\) corresponds to directions where \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\boldsymbol\Phi\) has little support, i.e. feature directions far from those covered by the training data, so the predictive variance balloons under extrapolation.


Ridge regression as MAP. Show that the MAP under \(\mathbf w \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf 0, \tau^2 \mathbf I)\) solves the ridge problem \(\arg\min_{\mathbf w}\big[\|\mathbf y - \boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf w\|^2 + (\sigma^2/\tau^2)\|\mathbf w\|^2\big]\).


Solution. The negative log-posterior, up to constants in \(\mathbf w\), is \[-\log p(\mathbf w\mid D) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|\mathbf y - \boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf w\|^2 + \frac{1}{2\tau^2}\|\mathbf w\|^2 + \text{const}.\] Multiplying by \(2\sigma^2\) gives the ridge objective with \(\lambda = \sigma^2/\tau^2\), and setting the gradient to zero yields the MAP \(\hat{\mathbf w}_{\text{MAP}} = (\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\boldsymbol\Phi + \lambda \mathbf I)^{-1}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \mathbf y = \mathbf m_N\).


Lasso as MAP. Show that the MAP under a Laplace (double-exponential) prior on each \(w_j\) corresponds to an \(L^1\)-penalised least-squares problem (the lasso).


Solution. With independent Laplace priors \(p(w_j) = \tfrac{1}{2b}\exp(-|w_j|/b)\), the negative log-posterior is \[-\log p(\mathbf w\mid D) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|\mathbf y - \boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf w\|^2 + \frac{1}{b}\sum_j |w_j| + \text{const}.\] Minimising this is the lasso \(\arg\min_{\mathbf w}\big[\|\mathbf y - \boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf w\|^2 + \lambda\|\mathbf w\|_1\big]\) with \(\lambda = 2\sigma^2/b\); the non-differentiable \(\ell^1\) penalty drives some coefficients exactly to zero.


Marginal likelihood (evidence). Derive the marginal likelihood \(p(\mathbf y \mid \boldsymbol\Phi)\) for the Bayesian linear regression with Gaussian prior and Gaussian likelihood. Identify the Occam factor.


Solution. Marginalising the Gaussian likelihood against the Gaussian prior gives a Gaussian on \(\mathbf y\), \[p(\mathbf y\mid\boldsymbol\Phi) = \mathcal N\!\left(\mathbf y\,\big|\,\boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf m_0,\ \sigma^2\mathbf I + \boldsymbol\Phi\mathbf S_0\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\right).\] Equivalently, \(\log p(\mathbf y\mid\boldsymbol\Phi) = \log p(\mathbf y\mid\hat{\mathbf w}_{\text{MAP}}) + \tfrac12\log\frac{|\mathbf S_N|}{|\mathbf S_0|} + \text{const}\); the best-fit likelihood at the MAP times the Occam factor \(\sqrt{|\mathbf S_N|/|\mathbf S_0|}\), which is the ratio of posterior to prior volume in \(\mathbf w\)-space and penalises models that need to fine-tune their parameters.


Conjugate NIG update. Add an unknown noise scale \(\sigma^2\) with prior \(\sigma^2 \sim \text{IG}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\) and conditional prior \(\mathbf w \mid \sigma^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf 0, \sigma^2 \mathbf V_0)\). Write the posterior parameters \((\mathbf m_N, \mathbf V_N, \alpha_N, \beta_N)\) after observing \(\mathbf y, \boldsymbol\Phi\).


Solution. The Normal–Inverse-Gamma family is conjugate; absorbing the common \(\sigma^2\) factor, the posterior is again NIG with \[\mathbf V_N^{-1} = \mathbf V_0^{-1} + \boldsymbol\Phi^\top\boldsymbol\Phi, \qquad \mathbf m_N = \mathbf V_N(\mathbf V_0^{-1}\mathbf m_0 + \boldsymbol\Phi^\top \mathbf y),\] \[\alpha_N = \alpha_0 + \tfrac{N}{2}, \qquad \beta_N = \beta_0 + \tfrac12\big(\mathbf y^\top \mathbf y + \mathbf m_0^\top \mathbf V_0^{-1}\mathbf m_0 - \mathbf m_N^\top \mathbf V_N^{-1}\mathbf m_N\big).\] Note the precision-form mean/covariance update is identical to the known-noise case with \(\mathbf S_0 = \sigma^2\mathbf V_0\), while \((\alpha_N,\beta_N)\) accumulate \(N\) observations and the residual sum of squares.


Equal-tail \(95\%\) predictive interval. For a new input \(x_*\), write the interval explicitly in terms of \(\boldsymbol\phi_*\), \(\mathbf m_N\), \(\mathbf S_N\), \(\sigma^2\), and \(z_{0.975} \approx 2\).


Solution. Since \(y_*\mid D\sim\mathcal N(\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top\mathbf m_N,\ \sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\boldsymbol\phi_*)\), the equal-tail \(95\%\) predictive interval is \[\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf m_N \ \pm\ z_{0.975}\,\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\,\boldsymbol\phi_*} \ \approx\ \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf m_N \ \pm\ 2\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\,\boldsymbol\phi_*}.\]


Posterior predictive vs plug-in. Argue with one line of algebra why the posterior predictive variance is strictly larger than the plug-in variance unless the posterior is a point mass.


Solution. By the law of total variance, \[\mathrm{Var}(y_*\mid D) = E[\mathrm{Var}(y_*\mid\mathbf w)] + \mathrm{Var}(E[y_*\mid\mathbf w]) = \sigma^2 + \boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\boldsymbol\phi_* \;\ge\; \sigma^2,\] with equality only when \(\boldsymbol\phi_*^\top \mathbf S_N\boldsymbol\phi_* = 0\), i.e. the posterior puts all mass on a single \(\mathbf w\). Hence the plug-in variance \(\sigma^2\) systematically understates uncertainty.


Effect of prior width. Take a one-feature linear regression with prior \(w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\) and known \(\sigma^2\). Compute the posterior mean and discuss its behaviour as \(\tau \to 0\) and \(\tau \to \infty\).


Solution. With \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\boldsymbol\Phi = \sum_i x_i^2 \equiv S_{xx}\) and \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \mathbf y = \sum_i x_i y_i \equiv S_{xy}\), the precision-form update gives \[m_N = \frac{S_{xy}/\sigma^2}{\tau^{-2} + S_{xx}/\sigma^2} = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx} + \sigma^2/\tau^2}.\] As \(\tau\to 0\) the prior dominates and \(m_N\to 0\) (full shrinkage); as \(\tau\to\infty\) the prior is uninformative and \(m_N\to S_{xy}/S_{xx} = \hat w_{\text{OLS}}\).


Effective degrees of freedom. For ridge regression with prior precision \(\lambda = \sigma^2/\tau^2\), the effective degrees of freedom is \(\text{df}(\lambda) = \text{tr}\big[\boldsymbol\Phi(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi + \lambda \mathbf I)^{-1}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\big]\). Compute it for a single feature.


Solution. With \(\boldsymbol\Phi = (x_1,\dots,x_N)^\top\), \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\boldsymbol\Phi = S_{xx}\) is a scalar, so \[\text{df}(\lambda) = \operatorname{tr}\!\left[\boldsymbol\Phi\boldsymbol\Phi^\top\right]\frac{1}{S_{xx}+\lambda} = \frac{S_{xx}}{S_{xx}+\lambda}\in(0,1).\] As \(\lambda\to 0\) we recover \(1\) degree of freedom (OLS); as \(\lambda\to\infty\), \(\text{df}\to 0\) (full shrinkage).


Sequential update. Show that updating the conjugate posterior on \((\mathbf y_1, \boldsymbol\Phi_1)\) and then on \((\mathbf y_2, \boldsymbol\Phi_2)\) gives the same posterior as a single update on the stacked data.


Solution. After the first batch, \(\mathbf S_1^{-1} = \mathbf S_0^{-1} + \sigma^{-2}\boldsymbol\Phi_1^\top \boldsymbol\Phi_1\) and \(\mathbf S_1^{-1}\mathbf m_1 = \mathbf S_0^{-1}\mathbf m_0 + \sigma^{-2}\boldsymbol\Phi_1^\top \mathbf y_1\). Using \((\mathbf m_1,\mathbf S_1)\) as the new prior and applying the same rule to \((\mathbf y_2,\boldsymbol\Phi_2)\) yields \[\mathbf S_N^{-1} = \mathbf S_0^{-1} + \sigma^{-2}(\boldsymbol\Phi_1^\top \boldsymbol\Phi_1 + \boldsymbol\Phi_2^\top \boldsymbol\Phi_2), \quad \mathbf S_N^{-1}\mathbf m_N = \mathbf S_0^{-1}\mathbf m_0 + \sigma^{-2}(\boldsymbol\Phi_1^\top \mathbf y_1 + \boldsymbol\Phi_2^\top \mathbf y_2),\] which is exactly the single-step update with stacked \(\boldsymbol\Phi = [\boldsymbol\Phi_1;\boldsymbol\Phi_2]\) and \(\mathbf y = [\mathbf y_1;\mathbf y_2]\). Bayesian updating is associative.


Posterior mean as shrinkage. Show that the posterior mean in the conjugate model with prior \(\mathbf w \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf 0, \tau^2 \mathbf I)\) is a shrunk version of the OLS estimator \(\hat{\mathbf w}_{\text{OLS}}\), and identify the shrinkage factor in the eigenbasis of \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top \boldsymbol\Phi\).


Solution. 


Posterior predictive check. Describe in one sentence what a posterior predictive check is, what diagnostic plots you would draw, and what would indicate a misspecified model.


Solution.

text:
12.15 Sources

Sivia, D. S., & Skilling, J. (2006). Data analysis: A Bayesian tutorial (2nd ed.). Oxford University Press. Chapters 3 and 6.
van der Linden, W. J., & Hambleton, R. K. (Eds.). (2013). Handbook of modern item response theory. Springer. Chapter 21 (regression and related methods).
Gelman, A., Vehtari, A., Simpson, D., Burkner, P. C., & Gabry, J. (2020). Bayesian workflow. arXiv preprint arXiv:2011.01808.

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.1 Introduction: from hand-coded to declarative models
In preceding chapters, we implemented Markov chain Monte Carlo by hand: we wrote log-likelihood and log-prior functions, tuned proposal covariance matrices, diagnosed convergence, and iterated. This approach is powerful pedagogically—it reveals exactly what is being sampled and how—but it is labour-intensive and error-prone for realistic models.
Real-world inference often requires many hyperparameters, hierarchical structure, and thousands of latent variables. Writing the full log-posterior by hand for such models is impractical. We need a language in which to declare the model in terms of its statistical structure, and delegate the numerical sampling to software.
Probabilistic programming languages (PPLs) solve this. They allow you to write: \[\text{model + data} \to p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y}).\]
Two mainstream PPLs in the applied Bayesian community are JAGS and Stan. We also cover variational inference, an alternative to MCMC that solves the posterior by optimisation, and its automation in ADVI (automatic differentiation variational inference).
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.2 The classical limitation: conjugacy
In the first chapters of this course, we exploited conjugate priors: when the prior and likelihood share a specific functional form, the posterior is tractable analytically. For example, a Beta prior on a Bernoulli probability yields a Beta posterior; a Normal prior on a Gaussian mean (with known variance) yields a Normal posterior.
Conjugacy is rare in practice. Most real models are non-conjugate: the posterior is not available in closed form. In such cases we have two paths:

MCMC: Sample from the posterior.
Variational inference: Approximate the posterior with a tractable family.

Both JAGS and Stan use MCMC. JAGS uses Gibbs sampling or Metropolis-within-Gibbs (no gradient information). Stan uses Hamiltonian Monte Carlo with automatic differentiation (gradient-based), which mixes much faster.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
section: 13.3 JAGS: Gibbs sampling with a model syntax
text:
13.3 JAGS: Gibbs sampling with a model syntax

13.3.1 Overview
JAGS stands for Just Another Gibbs Sampler. It is a language and engine for specifying Bayesian hierarchical models and sampling from them using Gibbs updates or Metropolis-Hastings steps.
The key idea: you write the model in a declarative syntax that lists the conditional distribution of each variable given its parents. JAGS parses this, determines which variables can be sampled by Gibbs and which need Metropolis-Hastings, and executes the sampler automatically.


13.3.2 Model syntax
A JAGS model comprises three optional blocks: data, model, and inits.
The data block declares observed quantities (fixed).
The model block specifies the generative structure: for each stochastic node (parameter, latent variable, observation), you write its conditional distribution.
The inits block (usually separate in R) provides starting values.
Example: centered quadratic regression.
model {
  # Likelihood
  for (i in 1:N) {
    y[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], tau)
    mu[i] <- a0 + a1 * z[i] + a2 * pow(z[i], 2)
  }

  # Priors
  a0 ~ dnorm(0, 1/100)
  a1 ~ dnorm(0, 1/100)
  a2 ~ dnorm(0, 1/100)

  # Precision parameterization
  tau <- 1 / pow(sigma, 2)
  sigma ~ dunif(0, 20)
}
Key syntax notes:

JAGS uses precision \(\tau = 1/\sigma^2\), not standard deviation.
Deterministic transformations use <-; stochastic nodes use ~.
Distributions are prefixed d: dnorm, dunif, dbinom, etc.
The prior on \(\sigma\) must be proper.



13.3.3 Workflow in R with rjags
library(rjags)
library(coda)

# Prepare data
jags_data <- list(
  N = nrow(obsdata),
  z = obsdata$z,
  y = obsdata$y
)

# Initialize
inits <- function() list(a0=0, a1=0, a2=0, sigma=1)

# Compile
jm <- jags.model(textConnection(model_string),
                 data=jags_data,
                 inits=inits,
                 n.chains=4)

# Adapt (burn-in)
update(jm, 2000)

# Sample
samp <- coda.samples(jm,
                     variable.names=c("a0","a1","a2","sigma"),
                     n.iter=20000)

# Summarize
summary(samp)
effectiveSize(samp)
Advantages of JAGS:

Simple declarative syntax.
Automatic Gibbs / MH switching.
Handles discrete and continuous parameters.

Disadvantages:

Gibbs sampling mixes slowly for correlated posteriors.
No gradient information; cannot exploit manifold geometry.
Precision parameterization less intuitive than standard deviation.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
section: 13.6 Variational inference: approximate posterior via optimisation
text:
13.6 Variational inference: approximate posterior via optimisation

13.6.1 The KL divergence decomposition
MCMC samples exactly (asymptotically) from the posterior \(p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y})\). Variational inference instead optimises over a family of tractable distributions \(\mathcal{Q}\) to find the member \(q^*(\theta)\) that best approximates the posterior.
Define the Kullback–Leibler divergence: \[\mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p) = \mathbb{E}_q \left[ \log \frac{q(\theta)}{p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y})} \right].\]
By the law of total probability, \[\log p(\mathbf{y}) = \mathbb{E}_q [ \log p(\mathbf{y}, \theta) - \log q(\theta) ] + \mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p),\]
which rearranges to
\[\boxed{\log p(\mathbf{y}) = \mathcal{L}(q) + \mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p)}.\]
Here \(\mathcal{L}(q)\) is the evidence lower bound (ELBO): \[\mathcal{L}(q) = \mathbb{E}_q [ \log p(\mathbf{y}, \theta) - \log q(\theta) ].\]
Since \(\log p(\mathbf{y})\) is fixed and \(\mathrm{KL}(q \parallel p) \geq 0\) always, maximising the ELBO minimises the KL divergence. Thus the optimal approximation is
\[q^*(\theta) = \arg\max_{q \in \mathcal{Q}} \mathcal{L}(q).\]


13.6.2 Mean-field approximation
To make the optimisation tractable, we restrict \(\mathcal{Q}\) to a mean-field family: \[q(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} q_i(\theta_i),\]
i.e., a product of independent marginal distributions (one per parameter). This destroys posterior correlations but is far easier to optimise than a full covariance Gaussian.
For each parameter, \(q_i\) is typically a standard distribution (Gaussian, Beta, Gamma, etc.).


13.6.3 The reparameterisation trick
To optimise the ELBO by stochastic gradient descent, we need to differentiate through expectations. The reparameterisation trick rewrites an expectation: \[\mathbb{E}_{q(\theta)} [f(\theta)] = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon \sim N(0, I)} [f(\mu + \sigma \epsilon)],\]
where \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) are the (optimisable) parameters of \(q\), and \(\epsilon\) is auxiliary randomness. Now we can differentiate w.r.t. \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) using the chain rule.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.7 ADVI: Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference

13.7.1 Automatic transformation to unconstrained space
Many parameters have constraints: \(\sigma > 0\), \(p \in [0, 1]\), etc. ADVI automatically transforms such parameters to an unconstrained space, optimises a Gaussian approximation there, and back-transforms samples.
For example, \(\sigma > 0\) is mapped to \(\eta = \log \sigma \in \mathbb{R}\). The variational family becomes \(q(\eta)\) (a univariate Gaussian in \(\eta\)), and we sample \(\eta \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), then set \(\sigma = \exp(\eta)\).


13.7.2 Gaussian variational family
ADVI uses a multivariate Gaussian approximation: \[q(\theta) = N(\theta \mid \mu, \Sigma),\]
where \(\Sigma\) is either diagonal (mean-field) or full-rank. The parameters \((\mu, \Sigma)\) are optimised by stochastic gradient ascent on the ELBO.


13.7.3 Stan’s vb() interface
# Variational inference in Stan (instead of MCMC)
fit_vb <- mod$variational(
  data = stan_data,
  algorithm = "meanfield",  # or "fullrank"
  iter = 10000,
  seed = 123
)

# Approximately posterior samples
draws <- fit_vb$draws()
Advantages of ADVI:

Much faster than MCMC for high-dimensional models.
Deterministic; no convergence diagnostics needed.
Useful for exploratory analysis.

Disadvantages:

Mean-field assumption often too restrictive; underestimates posterior variance.
No automatic diagnostic (unlike MCMC diagnostics like \(\hat{R}\)).
For posterior inference, MCMC is often preferable.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
section: 13.8 Practical workflow: from hand-rolled MCMC to PPLs
text:
13.8 Practical workflow: from hand-rolled MCMC to PPLs
The progression we have traced reflects a real scientific workflow:

Prototype with hand-coded MCMC (Chapter 11 onwards): Learn the model, understand the geometry of the posterior, diagnose what works and what doesn’t.
Apply parametrisation tricks: Center and scale predictors, use whitening transforms, exploit posterior covariance structure.
Upgrade to a gradient-based sampler: Use HMC or NUTS to exploit smoothness of the log-posterior.
Automate with Stan or JAGS: Declare the model once, let the software handle sampling, diagnostics, and adaptation.
Consider variational inference if:

You need very fast approximate inference for exploration.
Your model is too large for MCMC.
You are happy accepting mean-field correlations.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.9 Failure modes and remedies

13.9.1 Multimodal posteriors
If the target distribution has multiple distinct modes, MCMC may get stuck in one mode and miss others. JAGS and Stan both struggle with multimodality.
Remedies:

Use parallel tempering (Chapter 10).
Run many short chains from dispersed starting values.
Reparametrise to reduce multimodality (e.g., via identifiability constraints).



13.9.2 Discrete parameters
Stan requires all parameters to be continuous. If your model includes discrete latent variables (e.g., mixture component labels), you must marginalise them out analytically (integrate over all possible values) before passing the model to Stan.
JAGS handles discrete nodes naturally.


13.9.3 Heavy-tailed posteriors
If the posterior has heavy tails (e.g., a mixture of normals with small weights), the leapfrog integrator in HMC can take very long steps and diverge. Divergent transitions are a warning.
Remedies:

Increase adapt_delta (0.8 → 0.95 or higher).
Increase max_treedepth.
Reparametrise to reduce tail heaviness (e.g., use a Student-\(t\) prior to allow more dispersed posteriors).



13.9.4 Weak identifiability
If two parameters affect the likelihood in nearly the same way, the posterior is highly correlated or even degenerate. MCMC mixes slowly; variational inference underestimates uncertainty.
Remedy:

Include informative priors that break the symmetry.
Reparametrise (e.g., use a sum-of-effects rather than separate effects).
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI
text:
13.10 What to report
For any Bayesian analysis using JAGS or Stan, report:

Model specification: Likelihood, priors, parametrisation.
Sampling method: Algorithm, number of chains, iterations, warmup.
Convergence diagnostics: Trace plots, \(\hat{R}\), ESS, acceptance rate, divergent transitions (Stan only).
Posterior summaries: Mean or median, 95% credible intervals.
Posterior predictions: Regression curves with credible intervals; posterior predictive intervals if relevant.

Do not report precise posterior point estimates from a badly mixing chain—they are numerical artifacts, not inference.
crumbs: 13  Probabilistic programming: JAGS, Stan, ADVI

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.1 Introduction: When linear regression fails
Classical Gaussian linear regression assumes that the response variable \(y_i\) is continuous and approximately normally distributed with mean \(\mu_i = f(x_i; \beta)\) and constant variance \(\sigma^2\). This assumption works well for many physical measurements with small, additive noise.
However, in many experiments we encounter response variables that violate this assumption fundamentally:
Fitting a standard linear model \(y_i \sim N(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i, \sigma^2)\) to such data produces:
Generalized linear models extend regression to non-Gaussian responses by replacing the normality assumption with a member of the exponential family and introducing a link function that maps the linear predictor onto the valid parameter space.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
Count data: number of detected photons in a time window, decay events in a bin, or success counts in repeated trials. These are non-negative integers with variance proportional to the mean, not constant.
Binary outcomes: detection (yes/no), selection success (pass/fail), or signal presence (present/absent). The mean must lie in \((0,1)\) to represent a probability.
Positive-only data: time-to-event, energy, or rate parameters. The mean must be strictly positive.



Invalid predictions: fitted means may fall outside the natural parameter space (e.g., negative counts or probabilities outside \([0,1]\)).
Incorrect uncertainty quantification: constant-variance assumptions badly misrepresent the variability structure of heteroscedastic responses.
Biased inference: parameter estimates become inefficient and confidence intervals lose their intended coverage.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.2 The three ingredients of a GLM
text:
14.2 The three ingredients of a GLM
A generalized linear model comprises three interconnected components:

Definition (GLM structure): A generalized linear model for observations \(y_1, \ldots, y_N\) is specified by:

Random component: \(y_i \mid \theta_i \sim p(y_i; \theta_i)\), where \(p\) belongs to an exponential-family distribution parameterized by a natural parameter \(\theta_i\).
Systematic component: a linear predictor \[\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots + \beta_p x_{i,p} = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta},\] where \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is a vector of regression coefficients and \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the design vector for observation \(i\).
Link function: a smooth, invertible function \(g(\cdot)\) that relates the mean \(\mu_i = \mathbb{E}[y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i]\) to the linear predictor: \[g(\mu_i) = \eta_i, \quad \text{equivalently} \quad \mu_i = g^{-1}(\eta_i).\] The inverse link, \(g^{-1}\), is called the mean function.


The link function ensures that even though \(\eta_i\) can take any real value, the predicted mean \(\mu_i = g^{-1}(\eta_i)\) respects the constraints of the response distribution. Gaussian linear regression is the special case in which \(p\) is Gaussian and \(g\) is the identity, so \(\mu_i = \eta_i\).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.3 The exponential family and canonical form
text:
14.3 The exponential family and canonical form
The exponential family is a large class of distributions sharing a common parameterization that unifies likelihood inference for GLMs.

Definition (Exponential family canonical form): A probability distribution for \(y\) belongs to the exponential family if it can be written as \[p(y; \theta, \phi) = h(y, \phi) \exp\left(\frac{\theta y - A(\theta)}{\phi}\right),\] where: - \(\theta\) is the natural parameter (or canonical parameter). - \(\phi > 0\) is the dispersion parameter, often related to variance. - \(A(\theta)\) is the cumulant function or log-partition function. - \(h(y, \phi)\) is the base measure (carries \(y\)-dependent normalization and \(\phi\) terms not involving \(\theta\)).
The term \(A(\theta)\) ensures the distribution integrates or sums to one.


Theorem (Moment relations): For a distribution in exponential-family form, \[\mathbb{E}[y] = A'(\theta), \quad \mathrm{Var}(y) = \phi \cdot A''(\theta).\]
Proof sketch: The log-likelihood is \(\ell(\theta; y) = \theta y - A(\theta) + \text{const}\). The score is \(\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = y - A'(\theta)\). Setting \(\mathbb{E}[\text{score}] = 0\) gives \(\mathbb{E}[y] = A'(\theta)\). The variance follows from the Hessian of \(\ell\).

These moment relations are powerful: they immediately connect the natural parameter \(\theta\) to the mean \(\mu\) and relate the mean to the variance through \(A''(\theta)\).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.4 Canonical links and the link-function table
text:
14.4 Canonical links and the link-function table
A canonical link is the link function such that \(g(\mu) = \theta\). For canonical links, the natural parameter equals the linear predictor directly, which simplifies both interpretation and computation (the sufficient statistics for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) are linear in the data).
The following table summarizes the most common GLM families used in practice:



Table 14.1: Table: Exponential families and canonical links












Distribution
\(A(\theta)\)
\(\theta = g(\mu)\) (canonical)
\(g^{-1}(\eta)\) (inverse link)
Constraint




Gaussian
\(\theta^2/2\)

\(\mu \in \mathbb{R}\)


Bernoulli

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.5 Logistic regression: Bernoulli response
Logistic regression models a binary response \(y_i \in \{0, 1\}\) as a Bernoulli trial: \[y_i \mid \pi_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\pi_i),\] where \(\pi_i = \Pr(y_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_i)\) is the success probability.

Canonical link. The Bernoulli distribution has mean \(\mu_i = \pi_i\) and variance \(\pi_i(1 - \pi_i)\). The cumulant function is \(A(\theta) = \log(1 + e^\theta)\), so the canonical link is the logit: \[g(\pi_i) = \log\frac{\pi_i}{1 - \pi_i} = \eta_i = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}.\] The inverse logit or logistic function recovers the probability: \[\pi_i = \sigma(\eta_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta_i}}.\]
Likelihood. The likelihood for \(N\) independent observations is \[p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i^{y_i} (1 - \pi_i)^{1 - y_i},\] with log-likelihood \[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}; D) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \eta_i - \log(1 + e^{\eta_i}) \right].\]
Log-odds and odds ratios. The logit transforms the probability \(\pi\) into the log-odds. The odds are \(\frac{\pi}{1-\pi}\), the ratio of success to failure probability. Logistic regression is linear on the log-odds scale: \(\log\frac{\pi_i}{1-\pi_i} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots\). A one-unit increase in \(x_j\) changes the log-odds by \(\beta_j\), or equivalently, multiplies the odds by \(e^{\beta_j}\). If \(\beta_1 = 0.5\), a one-unit increase in \(x_1\) multiplies the odds of success by \(e^{0.5} \approx 1.65\). The change in probability is nonlinear: the same increase in \(x\) produces a larger change in \(\pi\) near \(\pi = 0.5\) and smaller changes near 0 or 1.
Variance is heteroscedastic. Since \(\mathrm{Var}(y_i \mid \pi_i) = \pi_i(1-\pi_i)\), the noise is largest near \(\pi = 1/2\) and shrinks to zero near 0 or 1. Logistic regression estimates a detection probability, not a continuous measured response.

The logistic function below shows how \(\eta\) is squashed into \((0,1)\).


Show code
eta <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 200)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-eta))

plot(eta, pi, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "black",
     xlab = expression(eta), ylab = expression(pi),
     ylim = c(0, 1), las = 1)
grid(col = "gray80")
abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "gray40", lwd = 1.5)
abline(v = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray40", lwd = 1.5)





The logistic function \(\sigma(\eta) = 1/(1+\exp(-\eta))\).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.6 Poisson regression: Count response
Poisson regression models count data \(y_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}\) as a Poisson distribution: \[y_i \mid \lambda_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i),\] where \(\lambda_i > 0\) is the expected count or rate. Both mean and variance equal \(\lambda_i\): \(\mathbb{E}[y_i] = \mathrm{Var}(y_i) = \lambda_i\).

Canonical link. The cumulant function is \(A(\theta) = e^\theta\), and the canonical link is the log link: \[g(\lambda_i) = \log \lambda_i = \eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots,\] so \(\lambda_i = e^{\eta_i}\).
Likelihood. The Poisson likelihood is \[p(D \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\lambda_i^{y_i} e^{-\lambda_i}}{y_i!},\] with log-likelihood \[\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}; D) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \lambda_i - \lambda_i - \log(y_i!) \right].\]
Exponential effects. The log link means that coefficients have exponential interpretation. A one-unit increase in \(x_j\) multiplies the expected count by \(e^{\beta_j}\): \[\frac{\lambda(x_j + 1)}{\lambda(x_j)} = e^{\beta_j}.\] If \(\beta_1 = 0.3\), then increasing \(x_1\) by one unit multiplies the expected count by \(e^{0.3} \approx 1.35\), a 35% increase. Log-linear models naturally describe rates that change by multiplicative factors.
Offsets and exposure. In many applications, observations have different exposure times or bin widths. For example, detector data collected over different durations \(E_i\). The expected count scales with exposure: \(\lambda_i = E_i r_i\), where \(r_i\) is the event rate. Using the log link, \(\log \lambda_i = \log E_i + \log r_i = \log E_i + \eta_i\), where \(\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i,1} + \cdots\). The term \(\log E_i\) is a known offset, a coefficient fixed at 1. In Stan or R, specify log(exposure) + w0 + w1*x in the linear predictor; the software interprets the known contribution separately.

The simulation below shows a Poisson regression with \(\log \lambda_i = 0.5 + 0.35 x_i\).


Show code
set.seed(101)
N <- 30
x <- seq(0, 5, length.out = N)
w0_true <- 0.5
w1_true <- 0.35
eta <- w0_true + w1_true * x
lambda <- exp(eta)
y <- rpois(N, lambda = lambda)

plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "black", xlab = "x", ylab = "count",
     ylim = c(0, max(y) * 1.1), cex = 0.9, las = 1)
lines(x, lambda, lwd = 2.5, col = "#b8123e")
grid(col = "gray80")
legend("topleft", legend = c("observed", "true rate"),
       pch = c(19, NA), lty = c(NA, 1), lwd = c(NA, 2.5),
       col = c("black", "#b8123e"), bty = "n")





Simulated Poisson regression data and fitted rate curve.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.7 Illustration: link functions
The choice of link function transforms the linear predictor onto the appropriate scale. Here we compare logit (for Bernoulli) and log (for Poisson) side by side:


Show code
eta_seq <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Logit (inverse)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-eta_seq))
plot(eta_seq, pi, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#b8123e",
     xlab = expression(eta), ylab = expression(mu),
     main = "Logit inverse link", las = 1)
grid(col = "gray80")

# Log (inverse)
lambda <- exp(eta_seq)
plot(eta_seq, lambda, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#b8123e",
     xlab = expression(eta), ylab = expression(mu),
     main = "Log inverse link", ylim = c(0, 55), las = 1)
grid(col = "gray80")





Canonical link functions: logit maps \(\eta\) to \((0,1)\) for probabilities; log maps \(\eta\) to \((0, \infty)\) for rates.




Show code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: 14  Generalized linear models
14  Generalized linear models
section: 14.9 Residual diagnostics and posterior predictive checks
text:
14.9 Residual diagnostics and posterior predictive checks

Deviance. In a fitted GLM, the deviance is twice the log-likelihood difference between the saturated model (one parameter per observation) and the fitted model: \[D = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \ell_{\text{sat}}(y_i) - \ell_{\text{fitted}}(\hat{\mu}_i, y_i) \right].\] For logistic regression, this simplifies to a sum of log-likelihood comparisons; for Poisson, it involves Kullback–Leibler divergence between observed and fitted counts.
Pearson residuals. These scale differences by the estimated standard deviation: \[r_i^{\text{Pearson}} = \frac{y_i - \hat{\mu}_i}{\sqrt{\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(y_i)}}.\] In well-fitting models, Pearson residuals should be approximately standard normal. Large residuals flag individual observations that the model does not explain well.
Posterior predictive checks. In Bayesian inference, we can generate replicated data \(y_{\text{rep}}^{(s)}\) from the posterior predictive distribution for each MCMC draw \((s)\). By comparing summaries of observed data \(y\) to summaries of replicated data, we can assess whether the model is plausible:

Poisson: Check the proportion of zeros, the maximum count, the ratio of variance to mean.
Bernoulli: Check the total number of successes, the success fraction in subgroups, the patterns of runs.

If the observed summary is consistently in the tail of the replicated-data distribution, the model misses important structure.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.10 Coefficient interpretation and prediction

Logistic coefficients. A one-unit increase in \(x_j\) (holding others fixed) changes the odds by a factor of \(e^{\beta_j}\): if \(\beta_j = 0.5\), the odds increase by a factor of \(e^{0.5} \approx 1.65\) (65% increase); if \(\beta_j = -0.3\), the odds decrease by a factor of \(e^{-0.3} \approx 0.74\) (26% decrease). The corresponding probability change depends on the current probability level. Near \(\pi = 0.5\) (maximum sensitivity), a one-unit change in \(x_j\) shifts \(\pi\) by approximately \(\beta_j / 4\). Near extreme probabilities, the shift is much smaller. Always report uncertainty: credible intervals for \(\beta_j\) quantify the precision of the estimate; transform endpoints through \(e^{\beta_j}\) to report odds ratios and their intervals.
Poisson coefficients. \(\beta_j\) is the change in log rate per unit increase in \(x_j\). The expected count is multiplied by \(e^{\beta_j}\) per unit increase: if \(\beta_1 = 0.3\), a one-unit increase in \(x_1\) multiplies the expected count by \(e^{0.3} \approx 1.35\) (35% increase); if \(\beta_2 = -0.2\), the expected count is multiplied by \(e^{-0.2} \approx 0.82\) (18% decrease). These are relative effects: they describe multiplicative changes in the rate, not absolute changes. In a detector experiment, a 35% increase in count rate might be substantial or negligible depending on the baseline and the experimental goal.
Posterior predictive intervals for logistic regression. Posterior draws of \((w_0, w_1)\) give posterior samples of \(\pi_* = \sigma(w_0 + w_1 x_*)\) at a new location \(x_*\). The credible interval for \(\pi_*\) quantifies uncertainty in the probability; a separate predictive interval addresses the binary outcome itself (\(y_* \in \{0, 1\}\) with some probability).
Posterior predictive intervals for Poisson regression. The posterior predictive interval for a new count \(y_*\) at \(x_*\) involves: (1) drawing \((w_0^{(s)}, w_1^{(s)})\) from the posterior, (2) computing \(\lambda_*^{(s)} = \exp(w_0^{(s)} + w_1^{(s)} x_*)\), (3) drawing \(y_*^{(s)} \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_*^{(s)})\), and (4) summarizing the empirical distribution of \(y_*^{(s)}\) by quantiles (e.g., 2.5%, 50%, 97.5%). The predictive interval is discrete and generally asymmetric, reflecting the discrete and skewed nature of Poisson noise.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.11 Overdispersion and the negative binomial
A fundamental property of the Poisson is that variance equals the mean. In real count data, the variance often exceeds the mean—a phenomenon called overdispersion. Causes include:

Unobserved heterogeneity: different subgroups have different rates.
Clustering: observations within groups are not independent.
Extra-Poisson variability: a genuine excess beyond Poisson noise.


Overdispersion test: Compute \(\hat{\lambda}\) from fitted Poisson regression. If the sample variance of residuals \(\sum_i (y_i - \hat{\lambda}_i)^2 / (N - p)\) is much larger than the average fitted rate, overdispersion is present.

The negative binomial distribution extends Poisson regression by adding a shape parameter \(\phi\) that allows variance to exceed the mean: \[\mathrm{Var}(y_i) = \lambda_i + \frac{\lambda_i^2}{\phi}.\] As \(\phi \to \infty\), the negative binomial approaches Poisson. A negative-binomial GLM is fitted similarly to Poisson but with an additional dispersion parameter to estimate.
The simulation below contrasts Poisson noise (variance equal to mean) with overdispersed counts:


Show code
set.seed(303)
N <- 100
x <- seq(0, 4, length.out = N)
lambda <- 2 + 3 * x

# Poisson: variance = mean
y_poisson <- rpois(N, lambda = lambda)

# Overdispersed: inflate variance by factor of 2
y_overdispersed <- sapply(lambda, function(mu) {
  nb_size <- mu / 1  # size parameter for negative binomial
  rnbinom(1, size = nb_size, mu = mu)
})

plot(x, y_poisson, pch = 19, col = "gray40", cex = 0.8,
     xlab = "x", ylab = "count", las = 1, ylim = c(0, 30))
points(x, y_overdispersed, pch = 1, col = "#b8123e", cex = 0.8)
lines(x, lambda, lwd = 2, col = "black")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Poisson (Var=mean)", "Overdispersed (Var>mean)", "true mean"),
       pch = c(19, 1, NA), lty = c(NA, NA, 1), col = c("gray40", "#b8123e", "black"),
       bty = "n")





Poisson data (dashed line, Var = mean) vs. overdispersed count data (solid line, Var >> mean).

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.12 Common pitfalls and best practices

Common mistakes:

Ignoring distributional mismatch: Fitting Gaussian regression to counts or binary data leads to invalid predictions and misaligned uncertainty.
Forgetting the offset: In exposure-weighted Poisson models, omitting the offset biases rate estimates and inflates uncertainty.
Misinterpreting coefficients: Logistic coefficients are log-odds, not probability changes. Poisson coefficients are log-rates, not expected count changes.
Reporting point estimates without credible intervals: GLM coefficients are estimated quantities; always include uncertainty via posterior quantiles.
Confusing credible and predictive intervals: The credible interval for \(\mu\) (or \(\pi\), \(\lambda\)) reflects estimation uncertainty. The predictive interval for \(y\) also includes observation noise and is wider.
Ignoring posterior predictive checks: A high likelihood value does not guarantee model adequacy. Generate replicated data and compare to observed summaries.
Trusting diagnostics without thought: High \(\hat{R}\) and large ESS are necessary but not sufficient. Check trace plots, residual patterns, and posterior predictive checks for deeper assessment.



Remark. Best practices:

Center or standardize predictors before fitting to reduce collinearity and improve MCMC mixing. A unit increase in a centered \(x\) is meaningful and comparable across covariates.
Visualize the fitted curve with uncertainty bands overlaid on the data. This reveals whether the model captures the dominant patterns.
Use weakly informative priors that regularize without imposing strong prior beliefs. For coefficients on a standardized scale, \(\text{Normal}(0, 2)\) or \(\text{Normal}(0, 3)\) is reasonable.
Check trace plots for all parameters after sampling. Chains should mix well (no trending, not stuck, high autocorrelation).
Report both point and interval estimates and be explicit about the scale (log-odds, log-rate, probability, count).
Document the model specification (link function, prior, offset, if any) to enable reproducibility and interpretation.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.13 Conclusion
Generalized linear models extend Bayesian regression to count, binary, and other non-Gaussian responses by:

Specifying a response distribution from the exponential family (Poisson, Bernoulli, etc.).
Connecting the mean to a linear predictor via a link function that respects parameter constraints.
Inferring the linear coefficients while propagating uncertainty through the posterior.

The three ingredients—random component, linear predictor, and link function—unify a diverse set of regression tasks under one framework. Logistic regression models probabilities; Poisson regression models rates and counts; gamma and inverse-Gaussian regressions model positive continuous data. MCMC and probabilistic programming allow flexible, fully Bayesian inference, with posterior checks guiding model refinement.

title: 14  Generalized linear models
text:
14.14 Exercises

State the three ingredients of a GLM. Write them out for logistic regression: random component, linear predictor, and link function.


Solution. The three ingredients are: a random component from the exponential family, a linear predictor \(\eta = \boldsymbol\phi^\top\boldsymbol\beta\), and a link \(g(\mu) = \eta\). For logistic regression \[y_i \mid p_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p_i), \quad \eta_i = \boldsymbol\phi_i^\top\boldsymbol\beta, \quad g(p_i) = \log\tfrac{p_i}{1-p_i} = \eta_i,\] so that \(p_i = \sigma(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)\).


Canonical links. State the canonical link for the binomial, Poisson, Gaussian, gamma, and inverse-Gaussian families. Briefly justify why the link is canonical by referring to the natural parameter of the exponential family.


Solution. The canonical link is the function \(g\) such that \(g(\mu) = \theta\), the natural parameter: \[\text{binomial: logit}\;\log\tfrac{\mu}{1-\mu},\quad \text{Poisson: }\log\mu,\quad \text{Gaussian: }\mu,\quad \text{gamma: }-1/\mu,\quad \text{IG: }-1/\mu^2.\] With the canonical link the score has the clean form \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top(\mathbf y - \boldsymbol\mu)\) and \(\eta = \boldsymbol\phi^\top\boldsymbol\beta\) is exactly the natural parameter.


Score for logistic regression. Show that the score function (gradient of the log-likelihood) for logistic regression with a single covariate is \(\sum_i (y_i - p_i)\,x_i\), where \(p_i = \sigma(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i)\) and \(\sigma\) is the logistic function.


Solution. The log-likelihood is \(\ell(\boldsymbol\beta) = \sum_i [y_i\eta_i - \log(1+e^{\eta_i})]\) with \(\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i\). Using \(\partial \log(1+e^{\eta_i})/\partial\beta_1 = \sigma(\eta_i)\,x_i = p_i x_i\), \[\frac{\partial\ell}{\partial\beta_1} = \sum_i (y_i - p_i)\,x_i,\] and analogously \(\partial\ell/\partial\beta_0 = \sum_i (y_i - p_i)\).


Fisher information. For logistic regression with a single feature \(x_i\), derive the Fisher information matrix entry \(\mathcal{I}_{11} = \sum_i p_i(1-p_i)\,x_i^2\).


Solution. Differentiating the score \(\partial\ell/\partial\beta_1 = \sum_i (y_i - p_i)x_i\) with \(\partial p_i/\partial\beta_1 = p_i(1-p_i)x_i\) gives the negative Hessian \[\mathcal{I}_{11} = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{\partial^2\ell}{\partial\beta_1^2}\right] = \sum_i p_i(1-p_i)\,x_i^2.\] In matrix form \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol\beta) = \boldsymbol\Phi^\top W \boldsymbol\Phi\) with \(W = \mathrm{diag}(p_i(1-p_i))\).


Posterior under a Gaussian prior. With prior \(\beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2 \mathbf I)\), write the log-posterior for logistic regression up to a constant. Identify why no closed form exists for the posterior mean.


Solution. Up to an additive constant, \[\log p(\boldsymbol\beta\mid D) = \sum_i\bigl[y_i\eta_i - \log(1+e^{\eta_i})\bigr] - \frac{1}{2\tau^2}\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2.\] The likelihood term \(\log(1+e^{\eta_i})\) is non-conjugate to the Gaussian prior, so the posterior is not Gaussian and the normalising constant has no closed form; one must use IRLS/Laplace or MCMC.


MAP via penalised IRLS. Sketch how iteratively re-weighted least squares (IRLS) reduces to a Newton step on the log-posterior, and how adding a quadratic penalty corresponds to the Gaussian prior. Write down one iteration.


Solution. A Newton step on the penalised log-posterior uses gradient \(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top(\mathbf y - \mathbf p) - \boldsymbol\beta/\tau^2\) and Hessian \(-(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top W\boldsymbol\Phi + \mathbf I/\tau^2)\), yielding \[\boldsymbol\beta^{(t+1)} = \bigl(\boldsymbol\Phi^\top W^{(t)}\boldsymbol\Phi + \tau^{-2}\mathbf I\bigr)^{-1}\boldsymbol\Phi^\top W^{(t)}\mathbf z^{(t)},\] with working response \(\mathbf z^{(t)} = \boldsymbol\Phi\boldsymbol\beta^{(t)} + W^{(t)-1}(\mathbf y - \mathbf p^{(t)})\). The Gaussian prior contributes the \(\tau^{-2}\mathbf I\) ridge term.


Posterior predictive in logistic regression. For a new input \(x_*\) with MCMC samples \(\{\beta^{(s)}\}_{s=1}^S\), write the posterior predictive probability \(P(y_* = 1 \mid x_*, D)\) as a Monte Carlo average.


Solution. Marginalising over the posterior gives \[P(y_* = 1\mid x_*, D) = \int \sigma(\boldsymbol\beta^\top\boldsymbol\phi_*)\,p(\boldsymbol\beta\mid D)\,d\boldsymbol\beta \approx \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S \sigma\!\bigl(\boldsymbol\beta^{(s)\top}\boldsymbol\phi_*\bigr).\]


Log-odds interpretation. In logistic regression with coefficient \(\beta_1\) on feature \(x_1\), what does a one-unit increase in \(x_1\) do to the odds? Give the formula in closed form.


Solution. Since \(\log\tfrac{p}{1-p} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1\), the odds at \(x_1+1\) relative to \(x_1\) satisfy \[\frac{\mathrm{odds}(x_1+1)}{\mathrm{odds}(x_1)} = e^{\beta_1},\] so a unit increase multiplies the odds by \(e^{\beta_1}\).


Rate-ratio interpretation. In Poisson regression with log link and coefficient \(\beta_1\), what does a one-unit increase in \(x_1\) do to the rate? Give the formula and interpret in plain language.


Solution. With \(\log\mu = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1\), \[\frac{\mu(x_1+1)}{\mu(x_1)} = e^{\beta_1},\] so a unit increase in \(x_1\) multiplies the expected count (rate) by \(e^{\beta_1}\); e.g. \(\beta_1 = 0.3\) gives a \(\sim 35\%\) increase.


Deviance residuals. State the formula for the deviance residual for a Poisson GLM. Use it to derive the Pearson residual in the small-count limit.


Solution. The Poisson deviance residual is \[r_i^{\mathrm{dev}} = \mathrm{sign}(y_i - \hat\mu_i)\sqrt{2\!\left[y_i\log(y_i/\hat\mu_i) - (y_i - \hat\mu_i)\right]}.\] Expanding \(y_i\log(y_i/\hat\mu_i)\) around \(y_i \approx \hat\mu_i\) to second order gives \(2[\cdot] \approx (y_i-\hat\mu_i)^2/\hat\mu_i\), recovering the Pearson residual \(r_i^{P} = (y_i - \hat\mu_i)/\sqrt{\hat\mu_i}\).


Overdispersion. Explain why Poisson regression often fails when the data show \(\text{Var}(Y_i) > E[Y_i]\). Identify the standard remedy (negative binomial) and write its variance function \(\text{Var}(Y_i) = \mu_i + \mu_i^2 / \kappa\).


Solution. Poisson enforces \(\mathrm{Var}(Y_i) = \mu_i\), so when data are overdispersed the model underestimates uncertainty and standard errors are too narrow. The negative-binomial GLM adds a shape parameter \(\kappa\) giving \[\mathrm{Var}(Y_i) = \mu_i + \mu_i^2/\kappa,\] which reduces to Poisson as \(\kappa \to \infty\).


Identifiability. In logistic regression, what happens to the MLE when the data are perfectly separable (every \(y = 1\) point has higher \(x\) than every \(y = 0\) point)? How does a Gaussian prior restore identifiability?


Solution. Under perfect separation the log-likelihood is monotone in \(\|\boldsymbol\beta\|\), so the MLE diverges (\(\hat\beta_1 \to \infty\)) while the likelihood approaches its supremum. A Gaussian prior adds the penalty \(-\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2/(2\tau^2)\), \[\log p(\boldsymbol\beta\mid D) = \ell(\boldsymbol\beta) - \tfrac{1}{2\tau^2}\|\boldsymbol\beta\|^2,\] which pulls the posterior mode back to a finite value and restores a proper posterior.


Posterior summary. From an MCMC run on Bayesian logistic regression you obtain posterior samples \(\{\beta_1^{(s)}\}\) with mean \(0.4\) and standard deviation \(0.1\). Give a \(95\%\) equal-tailed credible interval for \(\beta_1\) and for the odds ratio \(e^{\beta_1}\), using \(z_{0.975} \approx 2\).


Solution. Approximating the posterior as Gaussian, \(0.4 \pm 2(0.1)\) gives \[\beta_1 \in (0.2,\, 0.6), \qquad e^{\beta_1} \in (e^{0.2},\, e^{0.6}) \approx (1.22,\, 1.82).\]


Link choice. For binary data, the probit link uses \(\Phi^{-1}\) instead of \(\text{logit}\). Compare the two: which has heavier tails of the latent process, and how does this change the interpretation of large coefficients?


Solution. The logistic latent distribution has heavier tails than the Gaussian used by probit, so for the same extreme \(\eta\) the logit assigns probabilities closer to \(0\) or \(1\) more slowly. Coefficients differ by roughly \(\beta_{\text{logit}} \approx 1.6\,\beta_{\text{probit}}\), and the logit retains clean odds-ratio interpretation \(e^{\beta_1}\), while probit coefficients are read as standard-deviation shifts of the latent Gaussian.


Pitfalls. Identify three common pitfalls of GLM modelling (e.g. unmodelled overdispersion, ignored zero-inflation, mis-specified link) and the diagnostic that would reveal each one.


Solution. 

Unmodelled overdispersion: detected by a Pearson statistic \(\sum_i r_i^{P2}/(N-p) \gg 1\) or by posterior-predictive variance-to-mean checks. (ii) Zero-inflation: detected by comparing the observed proportion of zeros to its posterior-predictive distribution. (iii) Mis-specified link: detected by plotting deviance/Pearson residuals against the linear predictor \(\eta\) and looking for systematic curvature.

title: 15  Appendix: Source code
text:
This appendix collects the R code used to produce the figures, tables and numerical estimates that appear from Chapter 2 onward. Every labelled listing in this appendix is referenced from the body of the book through \ref{lst:…}, with a placeholder of the form “See code \(\ref{lst:…}\) in Appendix for the implementation.”
The R-introduction code (Chapter 1) is not repeated here: its listings appear inline together with their annotations.

Remark. How the move happens. A Pandoc Lua filter (move-code.lua) walks the book and moves every code block that (i) is not the R-introduction chapter, (ii) carries an lst-cap chunk option, and (iii) does not contain # <1>-style annotations or the .no-move class into this appendix as a lstlisting environment.
